Kubernetes Complete Documentation - anihpj Django Project

Comprehensive Kubernetes API reference with practical Django examples using the anihpj Django project with its jobpost app (Skills, Author, Location, and JobPost models)

Kubernetes v1.28+ Django 5.2.5 Python 3.11+ Container Orchestration Microservices

🚀 Kubernetes API Documentation for anihpj Django Project

📚 Table of Contents

🎯 Navigate through comprehensive Kubernetes resources... Each section includes practical Django examples using the anihpj Django project and its jobpost app models (Skills, Author, Location, JobPost) with real API endpoints and containerized deployments.

Pods, Deployments, StatefulSets, DaemonSets, Jobs, CronJobs for anihpj Django applications

Services, Ingress, NetworkPolicy for exposing and securing Django apps

ServiceAccounts, TokenRequest/Review for secure Django deployments

Nodes, Namespaces, Events, APIServices for cluster management

🏗️ Workload Resources

Kubernetes workload resources manage the deployment and execution of your applications. In this section, we'll explore how to deploy and manage your anihpj Django application using various workload resources, starting with ReplicationController - a fundamental resource for maintaining desired replica counts of your Django pods.

📋 Workload Resources Overview

ReplicationController - Ensures desired replica count
Deployment - Declarative updates for apps
StatefulSet - Ordered, persistent apps
DaemonSet - One pod per node
Job - Run-to-completion tasks
CronJob - Scheduled jobs
🎯 Django Focus: This documentation shows practical examples of deploying the anihpj Django project with its jobpost app models (Skills, Author, Location, JobPost) to Kubernetes using real manifests and kubectl commands. The jobpost app provides JSON APIs for job listings, job details, and skills management.

Pod

Pod Overview

PodSpec - Pod specification with containers and volumes
Container - Container configuration and lifecycle
EphemeralContainer - Debugging containers
LifecycleHandler - Container lifecycle hooks
NodeAffinity - Node scheduling preferences
PodAffinity - Pod co-location rules
PodAntiAffinity - Pod separation rules
Probe - Health check configuration
PodStatus - Current pod status and conditions
PodList - Collection of pods
Operations - API operations for pod management
Official Docs: Pod v1 API | API Version: v1

Pod is a collection of containers that can run on a host. This resource is created by clients and scheduled onto hosts.

Full Description

Pod is the smallest and most fundamental workload resource in Kubernetes. A Pod represents a single instance of a running process in your cluster and encapsulates one or more tightly coupled containers that share storage, network, and a specification for how to run the containers. When deploying your anihpj Django application, each Pod runs your Django container alongside any supporting containers (like Redis for caching or database sidecars).

In your anihpj Django project, Pods are typically managed by higher-level controllers like Deployments or ReplicationControllers rather than created directly. However, understanding Pod specifications is crucial as they form the building blocks for all your jobpost application deployments, defining how your Django containers run, what resources they need, and how they communicate.

Syntax

# Create a Pod from YAML file
kubectl create -f pod.yaml
# Apply Pod configuration (create or update)
kubectl apply -f pod.yaml
# List all Pods in current namespace
kubectl get pods
# List all Pods with additional information
kubectl get pods -o wide
# Get detailed information about a specific Pod
kubectl describe pod {pod-name}
# Get Pod logs (single container)
kubectl logs {pod-name}
# Get Pod logs (specific container in multi-container pod)
kubectl logs {pod-name} -c {container-name}
# Execute command in Pod container
kubectl exec {pod-name} -- {command}
# Get interactive shell in Pod container
kubectl exec -it {pod-name} -- /bin/bash
# Port forward to access Pod locally
kubectl port-forward {pod-name} {local-port}:{pod-port}
# Delete a specific Pod
kubectl delete pod {pod-name}

YAML Structure

apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-django-pod
  namespace: default
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: web
spec:
  containers:
  - name: django-web
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    ports:
    - containerPort: 8000
      name: http
    env:
    - name: DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE
      value: "anihpj.settings"
    - name: DEBUG
      value: "False"
    volumeMounts:
    - name: app-storage
      mountPath: /app/data
    resources:
      requests:
        memory: "256Mi"
        cpu: "250m"
      limits:
        memory: "512Mi"
        cpu: "500m"
    livenessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /health/
        port: 8000
      initialDelaySeconds: 30
      periodSeconds: 10
    readinessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /ready/
        port: 8000
      initialDelaySeconds: 5
      periodSeconds: 5
  volumes:
  - name: app-storage
    emptyDir: {}
  restartPolicy: Always

Parameters Description

  • apiVersion: Always "v1" for Pod. This indicates the API version being used for the Pod resource.
  • kind: Must be "Pod" to specify the resource type.
  • metadata.name: Unique name for the Pod within the namespace. Use descriptive names like "anihpj-django-web".
  • metadata.namespace: Kubernetes namespace where the Pod will be created. Defaults to "default".
  • metadata.labels: Key-value pairs for organizing and selecting Pods. Essential for service discovery and management.
  • spec.containers: Array of containers that make up the Pod. Each Django Pod typically has one main application container.
  • spec.containers[].name: Name of the container within the Pod. Must be unique within the Pod.
  • spec.containers[].image: Docker image for the container. Use specific tags for production Django deployments.
  • spec.containers[].ports: Network ports that the container exposes. Django apps typically expose port 8000.
  • spec.containers[].env: Environment variables for the container. Configure Django settings, database connections, etc.
  • spec.containers[].volumeMounts: Mounts for persistent storage. Essential for Django static files and media.
  • spec.containers[].resources: CPU and memory requests/limits. Critical for proper Django application performance.
  • spec.containers[].livenessProbe: Health check to determine if container needs restart. Use Django health endpoints.
  • spec.containers[].readinessProbe: Health check to determine if container is ready for traffic.
  • spec.volumes: Storage volumes available to containers. Configure persistent storage for Django data.
  • spec.restartPolicy: When to restart containers (Always, OnFailure, Never). Use "Always" for Django web services.
  • spec.nodeSelector: Constraints for Pod scheduling to specific nodes based on labels.
  • spec.tolerations: Allow Pods to be scheduled on nodes with matching taints.
  • spec.affinity: Advanced scheduling rules for Pod placement based on node or Pod affinity.

Return Value

Returns the Pod object with its current state, including status information about containers, conditions, and scheduling details.

  • 200 OK: Pod successfully created/updated
  • 201 Created: Pod successfully created
  • 400 Bad Request: Invalid Pod specification
  • 401 Unauthorized: Authentication required
  • 403 Forbidden: Insufficient permissions
  • 404 Not Found: Namespace not found
  • 409 Conflict: Pod already exists
  • 422 Unprocessable Entity: Invalid field values

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Skills Model API Pod (jobpost app)

This example demonstrates deploying a Pod for the Skills API service, providing JSON endpoints for managing available job skills in the anihpj Django project.

apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-api-pod
  namespace: default
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: jobpost
    service: skills
spec:
  containers:
  - name: skills-api
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    ports:
    - containerPort: 8000
      name: http
    env:
    - name: DJANGO_APP
      value: "jobpost"
    - name: MODEL_FOCUS
      value: "skills"
    - name: API_ENDPOINT
      value: "/jobpost/skills/"
    resources:
      requests:
        memory: "128Mi"
        cpu: "100m"
      limits:
        memory: "256Mi"
        cpu: "200m"
    readinessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /jobpost/skills/
        port: 8000
      initialDelaySeconds: 10
      periodSeconds: 5
# Create the Skills API Pod
kubectl apply -f anihpj-skills-api-pod.yaml
pod/anihpj-skills-api-pod created
Example 2: User Authentication Pod (Django User model)

This example shows how to deploy a Pod for user authentication services using Django's built-in User model with the Author extension.

apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-auth-pod
  namespace: default
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: auth
    service: user
spec:
  containers:
  - name: auth-service
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    ports:
    - containerPort: 8000
      name: http
    env:
    - name: DJANGO_APP
      value: "jobpost"
    - name: MODEL_FOCUS
      value: "user"
    - name: AUTH_BACKEND
      value: "django.contrib.auth.backends.ModelBackend"
    - name: SECRET_KEY
      valueFrom:
        secretKeyRef:
          name: django-secrets
          key: secret-key
    volumeMounts:
    - name: user-data
      mountPath: /app/data/users
    resources:
      requests:
        memory: "256Mi"
        cpu: "200m"
      limits:
        memory: "512Mi"
        cpu: "400m"
    livenessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /admin/login/
        port: 8000
      initialDelaySeconds: 30
      periodSeconds: 10
  volumes:
  - name: user-data
    emptyDir: {}
# Create the User Authentication Pod
kubectl create -f anihpj-auth-pod.yaml
pod/anihpj-auth-pod created
Example 3: Author Profile Pod (jobpost app)

This example illustrates deploying a Pod for author profile management with proper resource allocation and health checks.

apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-author-pod
  namespace: default
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: jobpost
    service: author
spec:
  containers:
  - name: author-service
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    ports:
    - containerPort: 8000
      name: http
    env:
    - name: DJANGO_APP
      value: "jobpost"
    - name: MODEL_FOCUS
      value: "author"
    - name: PROFILE_STORAGE
      value: "/app/data/profiles"
    volumeMounts:
    - name: profile-storage
      mountPath: /app/data/profiles
    - name: config-volume
      mountPath: /app/config
    resources:
      requests:
        memory: "200Mi"
        cpu: "150m"
      limits:
        memory: "400Mi"
        cpu: "300m"
    readinessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /jobpost/authors/
        port: 8000
      initialDelaySeconds: 15
      periodSeconds: 10
    livenessProbe:
      exec:
        command:
        - python
        - manage.py
        - check
      initialDelaySeconds: 45
      periodSeconds: 30
  volumes:
  - name: profile-storage
    emptyDir: {}
  - name: config-volume
    configMap:
      name: django-config
# Apply Author Profile Pod configuration
kubectl apply -f anihpj-author-pod.yaml
pod/anihpj-author-pod created
Example 4: Location Service Pod (jobpost app)

This example demonstrates deploying a Pod for location-based services with caching configuration for job post locations.

apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-location-pod
  namespace: default
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: jobpost
    service: location
spec:
  containers:
  - name: location-service
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    ports:
    - containerPort: 8000
      name: http
    env:
    - name: DJANGO_APP
      value: "jobpost"
    - name: MODEL_FOCUS
      value: "location"
    - name: CACHE_BACKEND
      value: "redis://redis-service:6379/1"
    - name: GEO_API_KEY
      valueFrom:
        secretKeyRef:
          name: geo-secrets
          key: api-key
    resources:
      requests:
        memory: "192Mi"
        cpu: "100m"
      limits:
        memory: "384Mi"
        cpu: "250m"
    readinessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /jobpost/locations/
        port: 8000
      initialDelaySeconds: 8
      periodSeconds: 5
    livenessProbe:
      tcpSocket:
        port: 8000
      initialDelaySeconds: 20
      periodSeconds: 15
  restartPolicy: Always
# Create Location Service Pod
kubectl create -f anihpj-location-pod.yaml
pod/anihpj-location-pod created
Example 5: JobPost Management Pod (jobpost app)

This example shows deploying the core JobPost management Pod with comprehensive resource allocation and multi-container support for background tasks.

apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-pod
  namespace: default
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: jobpost
    service: jobpost
spec:
  containers:
  - name: jobpost-web
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    ports:
    - containerPort: 8000
      name: http
    env:
    - name: DJANGO_APP
      value: "jobpost"
    - name: MODEL_FOCUS
      value: "jobpost"
    - name: DATABASE_URL
      valueFrom:
        secretKeyRef:
          name: db-secrets
          key: database-url
    volumeMounts:
    - name: static-files
      mountPath: /app/static
    - name: media-files
      mountPath: /app/media
    resources:
      requests:
        memory: "512Mi"
        cpu: "300m"
      limits:
        memory: "1Gi"
        cpu: "600m"
    readinessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /jobpost/jobs/
        port: 8000
      initialDelaySeconds: 20
      periodSeconds: 10
    livenessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /health/
        port: 8000
      initialDelaySeconds: 60
      periodSeconds: 30
  - name: background-tasks
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    command: ["python", "manage.py", "process_tasks"]
    env:
    - name: DJANGO_APP
      value: "jobpost"
    - name: TASK_QUEUE
      value: "celery"
    resources:
      requests:
        memory: "256Mi"
        cpu: "100m"
      limits:
        memory: "512Mi"
        cpu: "200m"
  volumes:
  - name: static-files
    emptyDir: {}
  - name: media-files
    persistentVolumeClaim:
      claimName: jobpost-media-pvc
  restartPolicy: Always
# Apply JobPost Management Pod with all components
kubectl apply -f anihpj-jobpost-pod.yaml
pod/anihpj-jobpost-pod created

PodSpec

Official Docs: PodSpec v1

PodSpec is a description of a pod, defining containers, volumes, scheduling constraints, and other pod-level configurations.

Full Description

PodSpec is the heart of any Kubernetes workload, defining exactly how your anihpj Django application should run. It specifies everything from the Docker containers to run, to the volumes they need, to where they should be scheduled in your cluster. For your jobpost application, PodSpec will define how Django web servers, Celery workers, and Redis caches are configured and interconnected.

In practice, you rarely create PodSpecs directly - they're usually embedded within higher-level resources like Deployments or Jobs. However, understanding PodSpec is crucial because it defines the actual runtime behavior of your Django application containers, including resource limits, environment variables, health checks, and data persistence.

Syntax

# Basic PodSpec structure within a Pod
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-django-pod
spec:
  containers:
  - name: django-web
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
  volumes:
  - name: static-files
    emptyDir: {}
  restartPolicy: Always

Parameters Description

  • containers: List of containers to run in the pod. Required field - must have at least one container. For anihpj, typically includes Django web server, possibly Celery workers or Redis cache.
  • initContainers: List of containers that run before main containers start. Perfect for Django database migrations, static file collection, or environment setup tasks.
  • volumes: List of volumes that can be mounted by containers. Essential for Django static files, media uploads, database files, and log persistence.
  • restartPolicy: Defines when containers should be restarted. Use "Always" for Django web services, "OnFailure" for batch jobs, "Never" for debugging.
  • nodeSelector: Schedule pods on specific nodes based on labels. Useful for placing Django apps on high-memory nodes or specific availability zones.
  • tolerations: Allow pods to run on nodes with matching taints. Important for dedicated nodes or special hardware requirements.
  • affinity: Advanced scheduling rules for co-locating related services (Django + Redis) or spreading replicas across zones.
  • securityContext: Pod-level security settings including user IDs, file system groups, and privilege escalation controls.
  • serviceAccountName: Kubernetes service account for RBAC permissions. Important if Django needs to interact with Kubernetes APIs.
  • dnsPolicy: DNS resolution behavior. Usually "ClusterFirst" for normal service discovery between Django components.

Return Value

Returns the complete PodSpec configuration object, which is embedded within Pod, Deployment, Job, or other workload resources. Contains the full specification for how containers should be scheduled and run.

Examples

Example 1: Basic Django Web Server PodSpec

Simple Django web application for the jobpost app with basic container configuration.

# Create basic Django pod with PodSpec
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-django-basic
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: web
spec:
  containers:
  - name: django-web
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    ports:
    - containerPort: 8000
    env:
    - name: DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE
      value: "anihpj.settings"
EOF

# Check pod status
kubectl get pod anihpj-django-basic
Example 2: Django with Init Container for Migrations

Django pod with init container to run database migrations before starting the web server.

# Create Django pod with init container for migrations
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-django-migrations
spec:
  initContainers:
  - name: django-migrate
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    command: ["python", "manage.py", "migrate"]
    env:
    - name: DATABASE_URL
      value: "postgresql://user:pass@postgres:5432/anihpj"
  containers:
  - name: django-web
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    command: ["python", "manage.py", "runserver", "0.0.0.0:8000"]
EOF

# Monitor init container completion
kubectl get pod anihpj-django-migrations -w
Example 3: Django with Volume Mounts for Static Files

Django pod with volume configuration for static files and media uploads persistence.

# Create Django pod with volume mounts
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-django-volumes
spec:
  containers:
  - name: django-web
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    volumeMounts:
    - name: static-files
      mountPath: /app/static
    - name: media-files
      mountPath: /app/media
  volumes:
  - name: static-files
    emptyDir: {}
  - name: media-files
    persistentVolumeClaim:
      claimName: anihpj-media-pvc
EOF

# Check volume mounts
kubectl describe pod anihpj-django-volumes
Example 4: Django with Resource Limits and Health Checks

Production-ready Django pod with resource constraints and liveness/readiness probes.

# Create Django pod with resource limits and health checks
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-django-production
spec:
  containers:
  - name: django-web
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    resources:
      requests:
        memory: "256Mi"
        cpu: "250m"
      limits:
        memory: "512Mi"
        cpu: "500m"
    livenessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /health/
        port: 8000
      initialDelaySeconds: 30
    readinessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /ready/
        port: 8000
      initialDelaySeconds: 5
EOF

# Check resource usage
kubectl top pod anihpj-django-production
Example 5: Django with Security Context and Service Account

Secure Django pod configuration with security context and custom service account for RBAC.

# Create service account first
kubectl create serviceaccount anihpj-django-sa

# Create Django pod with security context
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-django-secure
spec:
  serviceAccountName: anihpj-django-sa
  securityContext:
    runAsNonRoot: true
    runAsUser: 1000
    fsGroup: 2000
  containers:
  - name: django-web
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    securityContext:
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
      readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
      capabilities:
        drop:
        - ALL
EOF

                # Verify security settings
kubectl get pod anihpj-django-secure -o jsonpath='{.spec.securityContext}'

Container

Official Docs: Container v1

A single application container that you want to run within a pod.

Full Description

Container is the fundamental building block of Kubernetes applications, defining how your anihpj Django application runs inside a pod. Each container specifies the Docker image to use, resource requirements, environment variables, volume mounts, and health checks. For your jobpost application, containers will run Django web servers, Celery workers, Redis caches, and PostgreSQL databases.

Containers in Kubernetes are more than just Docker containers - they include sophisticated lifecycle management, resource limits, security contexts, and networking configurations. Understanding container configuration is crucial for optimizing your Django application's performance, security, and reliability in production environments.

Syntax

# Basic container configuration
spec:
  containers:
  - name: django-web
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    ports:
    - containerPort: 8000
    env:
    - name: DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE
      value: "anihpj.settings"
    resources:
      requests:
        memory: "256Mi"
        cpu: "250m"
      limits:
        memory: "512Mi"
        cpu: "500m"

Parameters Description

  • name: Unique DNS-compatible name for the container within the pod. Use descriptive names like "django-web", "celery-worker", "redis-cache" to identify different components of your anihpj application.
  • image: Docker image to run. For production Django apps, always use specific version tags like "anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.2.3" instead of "latest" to ensure consistent deployments.
  • command: Override the default container entrypoint. Useful for Django management commands like migrations or custom startup scripts for your jobpost application.
  • args: Arguments passed to the command. Perfect for passing Django management command arguments or configuration flags.
  • ports: List of network ports to expose. Django web containers typically expose port 8000, while Celery Flower monitoring exposes port 5555.
  • env: Environment variables to set in the container. Essential for Django configuration like DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE, DATABASE_URL, and SECRET_KEY.
  • volumeMounts: Mount points for pod volumes. Critical for Django static files, media uploads, and persistent data storage for your jobpost application.
  • resources: CPU and memory requests/limits. Set appropriate values based on your Django application's performance requirements and traffic patterns.
  • livenessProbe: Health check to determine if container should be restarted. Use Django health endpoints to detect unresponsive applications.
  • readinessProbe: Health check to determine if container is ready to receive traffic. Ensure Django has database connectivity before serving requests.

Return Value

Returns the complete Container specification object used within PodSpec. Contains all configuration needed to run your Django application container with proper resource management, health monitoring, and security settings.

Examples

Example 1: Django Web Server Container for Skills Model

Basic Django web container serving the Skills API endpoint for job postings.

# Create pod with Django web container for Skills management
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-web
spec:
  containers:
  - name: django-web
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    ports:
    - containerPort: 8000
      name: http
    env:
    - name: DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE
      value: "anihpj.settings"
    - name: SKILLS_API_ENABLED
      value: "true"
    command: ["python", "manage.py", "runserver", "0.0.0.0:8000"]
EOF

# Check Skills container status
kubectl get pod anihpj-skills-web -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[0].name}'
pod/anihpj-skills-web created
django-web
Example 2: Django Container with Resource Limits for User Model

Django container with CPU/memory limits optimized for User authentication and profile management.

# Create Django container with resource constraints for User management
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-user-service
spec:
  containers:
  - name: django-user
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    resources:
      requests:
        memory: "256Mi"
        cpu: "250m"
      limits:
        memory: "512Mi"
        cpu: "500m"
    env:
    - name: USER_SERVICE_MODE
      value: "active"
    - name: MAX_USERS_PER_REQUEST
      value: "100"
EOF

# Monitor resource usage for User service
kubectl top pod anihpj-user-service
pod/anihpj-user-service created
NAME CPU(cores) MEMORY(bytes) anihpj-user-service 42m 128Mi
Example 3: Django Container with Volume Mounts for Author Model

Django container with persistent storage for Author profiles and uploaded content.

# Create Django container with volume mounts for Author content
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-author-content
spec:
  containers:
  - name: django-author
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    volumeMounts:
    - name: author-uploads
      mountPath: /app/media/authors
    - name: author-cache
      mountPath: /app/cache/authors
    env:
    - name: AUTHOR_UPLOADS_PATH
      value: "/app/media/authors"
  volumes:
  - name: author-uploads
    persistentVolumeClaim:
      claimName: author-uploads-pvc
  - name: author-cache
    emptyDir: {}
EOF

# Verify volume mounts for Author service
kubectl describe pod anihpj-author-content | grep -A 5 "Mounts:"
pod/anihpj-author-content created
Mounts: /app/cache/authors from author-cache (rw) /app/media/authors from author-uploads (rw) /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount from kube-api-access-xyz123 (ro)
Example 4: Django Container with Health Checks for Location Model

Django container with liveness and readiness probes for Location-based job search service.

# Create Django container with health checks for Location service
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-location-service
spec:
  containers:
  - name: django-location
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    ports:
    - containerPort: 8000
    livenessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /health/location/
        port: 8000
      initialDelaySeconds: 30
      periodSeconds: 10
    readinessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /ready/location/
        port: 8000
      initialDelaySeconds: 5
      periodSeconds: 5
    env:
    - name: LOCATION_SERVICE_ENABLED
      value: "true"
EOF

# Check health probe status
kubectl describe pod anihpj-location-service | grep -A 10 "Conditions:"
pod/anihpj-location-service created
Conditions: Type Status Initialized True Ready True ContainersReady True PodScheduled True
Example 5: Django Container with Security Context for JobPost Model

Secure Django container configuration for JobPost management with non-root user and read-only filesystem.

# Create secure Django container for JobPost management
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-secure
spec:
  containers:
  - name: django-jobpost
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    securityContext:
      runAsNonRoot: true
      runAsUser: 1000
      readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
    volumeMounts:
    - name: tmp-volume
      mountPath: /tmp
    - name: jobpost-data
      mountPath: /app/data
    env:
    - name: JOBPOST_SECURE_MODE
      value: "enabled"
  volumes:
  - name: tmp-volume
    emptyDir: {}
  - name: jobpost-data
    emptyDir: {}
EOF

# Verify security context
kubectl get pod anihpj-jobpost-secure -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[0].securityContext}'
pod/anihpj-jobpost-secure created
{"allowPrivilegeEscalation":false,"readOnlyRootFilesystem":true,"runAsNonRoot":true,"runAsUser":1000}

EphemeralContainer

Official Docs: EphemeralContainer v1

An EphemeralContainer is a temporary container that you may add to an existing Pod for user-initiated activities such as debugging.

Full Description

EphemeralContainer is a special type of container designed for debugging running pods without requiring a restart. When your anihpj Django application is experiencing issues in production, you can inject ephemeral containers to troubleshoot problems, inspect files, run diagnostic commands, or access Django shell sessions. These containers share the same network and process namespaces as your main application containers.

Unlike regular containers, ephemeral containers have no resource guarantees and will not be restarted when they exit. They're perfect for investigating issues with your jobpost application, checking database connectivity, examining log files, or running Django management commands without disrupting the running service.

Syntax

# Add ephemeral container to existing pod
kubectl debug {pod-name} -it --image={debug-image} --target={container-name}

# Add ephemeral container with custom command
kubectl debug {pod-name} -it --image={debug-image} -- /bin/sh

# Add ephemeral container to specific namespace
kubectl debug {pod-name} -n {namespace} -it --image={debug-image}

Parameters Description

  • name: Unique DNS-compatible name for the ephemeral container. Must be unique among all containers in the pod including init and regular containers.
  • image: Docker image to use for debugging. Common choices include busybox, alpine, or your Django image with debugging tools installed.
  • targetContainerName: Name of the existing container to share namespaces with. This allows the ephemeral container to see the same processes and network as your Django application.
  • command: Entry point for the ephemeral container. Use /bin/sh for shell access or python manage.py shell for Django shell debugging.
  • args: Arguments passed to the command. Useful for running specific diagnostic commands or Django management tasks.
  • env: Environment variables for the ephemeral container. Should typically match your Django application's environment for consistency.
  • volumeMounts: Mount the same volumes as your Django container to access application files, logs, and data directories.
  • securityContext: Security settings for the ephemeral container. Usually should match or be more restrictive than the target container.
  • stdin: Enable stdin for interactive debugging sessions. Set to true for interactive Django shell or command-line troubleshooting.
  • tty: Allocate a pseudo-TTY for interactive sessions. Required for proper terminal formatting during debugging.

Return Value

Returns the EphemeralContainer specification that was added to the pod. The container will appear in the pod's ephemeralContainers list and can be monitored like regular containers during its lifetime.

Examples

Example 1: Debug Skills Model Database Issues

Add debugging container to investigate Skills model database connectivity and query performance.

# Debug Skills model database issues
kubectl debug anihpj-skills-pod -it --image=anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0 --target=django-web -- python manage.py shell

# Check Skills model data and performance
kubectl get pod anihpj-skills-pod -o jsonpath='{.status.ephemeralContainerStatuses[0].state}'
Targeting container "django-web". If you don't see a command prompt, try pressing enter. Python 3.11.0 (main, Oct 24 2022, 18:26:48) [GCC 11.3.0] on linux Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information. (InteractiveConsole) >>>
{"running":{"startedAt":"2024-09-14T10:30:45Z"}}
Example 2: Debug User Authentication Issues

Add ephemeral container to investigate User model authentication and session problems.

# Debug User authentication issues
kubectl debug anihpj-user-auth-pod -it --image=anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0 -- /bin/bash

# Check ephemeral container status
kubectl describe pod anihpj-user-auth-pod | grep -A 5 "Ephemeral Containers"
Targeting container "django-web". If you don't see a command prompt, try pressing enter. root@anihpj-user-auth-pod:/#
Ephemeral Containers: debugger-xyz123: Container ID: containerd://abcd1234567890 Image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0 State: Running Started: Sat, 14 Sep 2024 10:32:15 +0000
Example 3: Debug Author Content Upload Issues

Add debugging container to investigate Author model file upload and media storage problems.

# Debug Author content upload issues  
kubectl debug anihpj-author-content-pod -it --image=busybox -- /bin/sh

# Verify ephemeral container was added successfully
kubectl get pod anihpj-author-content-pod -o yaml | grep -A 10 ephemeralContainers
Targeting container "django-web". If you don't see a command prompt, try pressing enter. / #
ephemeralContainers: - image: busybox name: debugger-abc789 stdin: true tty: true targetContainerName: django-web status: ephemeralContainerStatuses: - containerID: containerd://efgh5678901234 image: busybox:latest name: debugger-abc789 ready: true restartCount: 0 started: true state: running: startedAt: "2024-09-14T10:33:20Z"
Example 4: Debug Location-Based Search Performance

Add ephemeral container to investigate Location model geographic search and indexing performance.

# Debug Location search performance
kubectl debug anihpj-location-search-pod -it --image=anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0 -- python manage.py shell

# Monitor ephemeral container resource usage
kubectl top pod anihpj-location-search-pod --containers
Targeting container "django-web". If you don't see a command prompt, try pressing enter. Python 3.11.0 (main, Oct 24 2022, 18:26:48) [GCC 11.3.0] on linux Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information. (InteractiveConsole) >>> from jobpost.models import Location >>> Location.objects.count() 1247 >>>
POD NAME CPU(cores) MEMORY(bytes) anihpj-location-search-pod django-web 45m 256Mi anihpj-location-search-pod debugger-def456 12m 32Mi
Example 5: Debug JobPost Publishing Pipeline

Add ephemeral container to investigate JobPost model publishing workflow and Celery task processing.

# Debug JobPost publishing pipeline
kubectl debug anihpj-jobpost-publisher-pod -it --image=anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0 -- /bin/bash

# List all ephemeral containers in the pod
kubectl get pod anihpj-jobpost-publisher-pod -o jsonpath='{.spec.ephemeralContainers[*].name}'
Targeting container "django-web". If you don't see a command prompt, try pressing enter. root@anihpj-jobpost-publisher-pod:/app#
debugger-ghi123

LifecycleHandler

Official Docs: LifecycleHandler v1

LifecycleHandler defines a specific action that should be taken in a lifecycle hook. One and only one of the fields, except TCPSocket must be specified.

Full Description

LifecycleHandler enables you to execute custom actions at specific points in a container's lifecycle - either immediately after the container starts (postStart) or just before it terminates (preStop). For your anihpj Django application, this is crucial for database migrations, cache initialization, graceful shutdowns, and cleanup operations. Lifecycle handlers ensure your jobpost application starts and stops gracefully, maintaining data integrity and system health.

PostStart handlers execute asynchronously immediately after container creation but before the main process starts. Use these for Django migrations, cache warming, or setting up monitoring. PreStop handlers execute synchronously before container termination, allowing graceful shutdown of Django processes, database connections, and Celery workers.

Syntax

# Pod with lifecycle handlers
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
spec:
  containers:
  - name: django-app
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    lifecycle:
      postStart:
        exec:
          command: ["/bin/sh", "-c", "python manage.py migrate"]
      preStop:
        httpGet:
          path: /shutdown
          port: 8000

Parameters Description

  • exec: Execute a command inside the container. Perfect for running Django management commands like migrations, data imports, or cache warm-up scripts during startup or cleanup operations during shutdown.
  • httpGet: Make an HTTP GET request to a specific endpoint. Useful for triggering Django application initialization endpoints, health checks, or graceful shutdown URLs that properly close database connections.
  • sleep: Pause execution for a specified duration. Add delays in lifecycle hooks for Django application startup sequencing, allowing dependent services to initialize, or implementing graceful shutdown timeouts.
  • tcpSocket: (Deprecated) TCP socket connection for lifecycle hooks. No longer supported but maintained for backward compatibility. Use exec or httpGet handlers instead for Django applications.

Return Value

Returns execution status of the lifecycle handler. PostStart handlers must complete successfully for the container to be considered started. PreStop handlers are given a grace period to complete before forceful termination. Failed handlers generate events in the pod status.

Examples

Example 1: Skills Model Database Migration Handler

Add postStart handler to run Skills model migrations and initialization during pod startup.

# Apply Skills migration lifecycle handler
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-migrator
spec:
  containers:
  - name: django-skills
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    lifecycle:
      postStart:
        exec:
          command: ["python", "manage.py", "migrate", "jobpost", "--verbosity=2"]
EOF

# Check lifecycle handler execution
kubectl describe pod anihpj-skills-migrator | grep -A 10 Events
pod/anihpj-skills-migrator created
Events: Type Reason Age From Message ---- ------ ---- ---- ------- Normal Scheduled 30s default-scheduler Successfully assigned default/anihpj-skills-migrator to worker-node-1 Normal Pulled 29s kubelet Container image "anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0" already present on machine Normal Created 29s kubelet Created container django-skills Normal Started 28s kubelet Started container django-skills Normal PostStart 27s kubelet PostStart hook completed successfully
Example 2: User Session Cleanup Handler

Add preStop handler to gracefully cleanup User sessions and authentication data before shutdown.

# Apply User session cleanup lifecycle handler
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-user-session-cleanup
spec:
  containers:
  - name: django-user
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    lifecycle:
      preStop:
        httpGet:
          path: /api/auth/cleanup-sessions
          port: 8000
          scheme: HTTP
EOF

# Monitor lifecycle handler completion
kubectl get pod anihpj-user-session-cleanup -w
pod/anihpj-user-session-cleanup created
NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE anihpj-user-session-cleanup 0/1 Pending 0 0s anihpj-user-session-cleanup 0/1 ContainerCreating 0 2s anihpj-user-session-cleanup 1/1 Running 0 15s
Example 3: Author Content Sync Handler

Add postStart handler to synchronize Author content and media files during container initialization.

# Apply Author content sync lifecycle handler
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-author-content-sync
spec:
  containers:
  - name: django-author
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    lifecycle:
      postStart:
        exec:
          command: ["/bin/sh", "-c", "python manage.py collectstatic --noinput && python manage.py sync_media"]
EOF

# View lifecycle handler logs
kubectl logs anihpj-author-content-sync -f
pod/anihpj-author-content-sync created
PostStart hook executing: /bin/sh -c python manage.py collectstatic --noinput && python manage.py sync_media Operations to perform: Synchronize unmigrated apps: staticfiles Apply all migrations: contenttypes, auth, sessions Running migrations: No migrations to apply. Syncing static files... 0 static files copied to '/app/static/'. Syncing media files... Media synchronization completed successfully.
Example 4: Location Cache Warming Handler

Add postStart handler with sleep delay to warm Location-based caches after dependencies are ready.

# Apply Location cache warming lifecycle handler
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-location-cache-warmer
spec:
  containers:
  - name: django-location
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    lifecycle:
      postStart:
        exec:
          command: ["/bin/sh", "-c", "sleep 30 && python manage.py warm_location_cache"]
EOF

# Check lifecycle handler completion status
kubectl get pod anihpj-location-cache-warmer -o jsonpath='{.status.containerStatuses[0].state}'
pod/anihpj-location-cache-warmer created
{"running":{"startedAt":"2024-09-14T10:45:32Z"}}
Example 5: JobPost Processing Graceful Shutdown

Add preStop handler to gracefully shutdown JobPost processing and complete pending Celery tasks.

# Apply JobPost graceful shutdown lifecycle handler
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-processor
spec:
  containers:
  - name: django-jobpost
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    lifecycle:
      preStop:
        httpGet:
          path: /api/jobpost/graceful-shutdown
          port: 8000
          httpHeaders:
          - name: X-Shutdown-Type
            value: graceful
EOF

# Monitor graceful shutdown process
kubectl describe pod anihpj-jobpost-processor
pod/anihpj-jobpost-processor created
Name: anihpj-jobpost-processor Namespace: default Priority: 0 Service Account: default Node: worker-node-2/192.168.1.102 Start Time: Sat, 14 Sep 2024 10:47:15 +0000 Labels: Annotations: Status: Running IP: 10.244.2.15 Containers: django-jobpost: Container ID: containerd://xyz789abc123 Image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0 State: Running Started: Sat, 14 Sep 2024 10:47:18 +0000 Ready: True Restart Count: 0

NodeAffinity

Official Docs: NodeAffinity v1

Node affinity is a group of node affinity scheduling rules.

Full Description

NodeAffinity provides advanced scheduling rules for placing pods on specific nodes based on node labels and properties. Unlike simple nodeSelector, NodeAffinity supports complex expressions, required and preferred rules, and operator-based matching. For your anihpj Django application, use NodeAffinity to schedule database-heavy workloads on high-memory nodes, place web servers on nodes with SSD storage, or separate production and development environments.

Required rules must be satisfied for pod scheduling, while preferred rules are considered but not mandatory. This ensures your jobpost application runs on nodes with the right hardware specifications and geographic location while maintaining scheduling flexibility when resources are constrained.

Syntax

# Pod with node affinity
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
spec:
  affinity:
    nodeAffinity:
      requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
        nodeSelectorTerms:
        - matchExpressions:
          - key: node-type
            operator: In
            values: ["ssd-nodes"]
      preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
      - weight: 100
        preference:
          matchExpressions:
          - key: zone
            operator: In
            values: ["us-west-1"]

Parameters Description

  • requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution: Hard requirement that must be satisfied for pod scheduling. If no nodes match these criteria, the pod remains unscheduled. Use for critical requirements like SSD storage for database pods or specific CPU architectures.
  • preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution: Soft preference that the scheduler tries to satisfy but will violate if necessary. Each preference has a weight (1-100) to prioritize different requirements. Useful for optimizing performance while maintaining scheduling flexibility.
  • nodeSelectorTerms: List of node selector terms. At least one term must be satisfied (OR logic). Each term contains match expressions or match fields that specify node selection criteria using labels and properties.
  • matchExpressions: Key-value expressions with operators (In, NotIn, Exists, DoesNotExist, Gt, Lt). Provides flexible node selection based on node labels, allowing complex scheduling logic for different Django application components.

Return Value

Returns pod scheduling decisions based on node affinity rules. The scheduler evaluates all rules and places the pod on the best matching node. Includes selected node name, node labels, and scheduling events in pod status.

Examples

Example 1: Skills Model Database on SSD Nodes

Schedule Skills model database pods exclusively on nodes with SSD storage for optimal query performance.

# Apply Skills database with SSD node affinity
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-database
spec:
  affinity:
    nodeAffinity:
      requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
        nodeSelectorTerms:
        - matchExpressions:
          - key: storage-type
            operator: In
            values: ["ssd"]
          - key: memory
            operator: Gt
            values: ["8Gi"]
  containers:
  - name: skills-db
    image: postgres:13
EOF

# Check scheduled node details
kubectl get pod anihpj-skills-database -o wide
pod/anihpj-skills-database created
NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE IP NODE NOMINATED NODE READINESS GATES anihpj-skills-database 1/1 Running 0 45s 10.244.1.8 ssd-node-1
Example 2: User Authentication in Specific Zone

Prefer User authentication pods in us-west zone for latency optimization but allow other zones if needed.

# Apply User auth with zone preference
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-user-auth
spec:
  affinity:
    nodeAffinity:
      preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
      - weight: 80
        preference:
          matchExpressions:
          - key: topology.kubernetes.io/zone
            operator: In
            values: ["us-west-1a", "us-west-1b"]
      - weight: 50
        preference:
          matchExpressions:
          - key: node-type
            operator: In
            values: ["cpu-optimized"]
  containers:
  - name: user-auth
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
EOF

# View node scheduling decision
kubectl describe pod anihpj-user-auth | grep -A 5 "Node-Selectors"
pod/anihpj-user-auth created
Node-Selectors: Tolerations: node.kubernetes.io/not-ready:NoExecute op=Exists for 300s node.kubernetes.io/unreachable:NoExecute op=Exists for 300s Events: Type Reason Age From Message ---- ------ ---- ---- ------- Normal Scheduled 62s default-scheduler Successfully assigned default/anihpj-user-auth to us-west-1a-node
Example 3: Author Content on High-Memory Nodes

Require Author content processing pods on nodes with high memory for media processing workloads.

# Apply Author content with memory requirements
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-author-content
spec:
  affinity:
    nodeAffinity:
      requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
        nodeSelectorTerms:
        - matchExpressions:
          - key: memory
            operator: Gt
            values: ["16Gi"]
          - key: node.kubernetes.io/instance-type
            operator: In
            values: ["m5.2xlarge", "m5.4xlarge"]
  containers:
  - name: author-content
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    command: ["python", "manage.py", "process_media"]
EOF

# Monitor node affinity compliance
kubectl get pod anihpj-author-content -o jsonpath='{.spec.nodeName}'
pod/anihpj-author-content created
high-memory-node-2
Example 4: Location Services on Edge Nodes

Prefer Location services on edge nodes for geographic proximity but allow standard nodes as fallback.

# Apply Location services with edge preference
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-location-services
spec:
  affinity:
    nodeAffinity:
      preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
      - weight: 100
        preference:
          matchExpressions:
          - key: node-role
            operator: In
            values: ["edge"]
      - weight: 60
        preference:
          matchExpressions:
          - key: network-tier
            operator: In
            values: ["premium"]
  containers:
  - name: location-service
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    command: ["python", "manage.py", "run_location_service"]
EOF

# Check affinity scheduling results
kubectl describe pod anihpj-location-services | grep -A 10 Events
pod/anihpj-location-services created
Events: Type Reason Age From Message ---- ------ ---- ---- ------- Normal Scheduled 78s default-scheduler Successfully assigned default/anihpj-location-services to edge-node-1 Normal Pulled 77s kubelet Container image "anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0" already present on machine Normal Created 77s kubelet Created container location-service Normal Started 76s kubelet Started container location-service
Example 5: JobPost Processing on GPU Nodes

Require JobPost processing pods on nodes with GPU support for AI-powered job matching and analysis.

# Apply JobPost processing with GPU requirements
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-ai-processor
spec:
  affinity:
    nodeAffinity:
      requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
        nodeSelectorTerms:
        - matchExpressions:
          - key: accelerator
            operator: In
            values: ["nvidia-tesla-v100", "nvidia-tesla-k80"]
          - key: gpu-memory
            operator: Gt
            values: ["8Gi"]
      preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
      - weight: 90
        preference:
          matchExpressions:
          - key: gpu-count
            operator: Gt
            values: ["2"]
  containers:
  - name: jobpost-ai
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    command: ["python", "manage.py", "run_ai_matching"]
    resources:
      limits:
        nvidia.com/gpu: 1
EOF

# Verify GPU node scheduling
kubectl get pod anihpj-jobpost-ai-processor -o yaml | grep nodeName
pod/anihpj-jobpost-ai-processor created
nodeName: gpu-node-tesla-v100

PodAffinity

Official Docs: PodAffinity v1

Pod affinity is a group of inter pod affinity scheduling rules.

Full Description

PodAffinity enables you to co-locate pods based on the presence of other pods with specific labels. This is crucial for your anihpj Django application to ensure related components are scheduled together for optimal performance and reduced latency. Use PodAffinity to place Django web servers near Redis caches, co-locate database replicas with primary databases, or group application tiers on the same nodes.

Required affinity rules must be satisfied for scheduling, while preferred rules are considered but not mandatory. This ensures your jobpost application components maintain optimal proximity while preserving scheduling flexibility during resource constraints or node failures.

Syntax

# Pod with pod affinity
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
spec:
  affinity:
    podAffinity:
      requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
      - labelSelector:
          matchExpressions:
          - key: app
            operator: In
            values: ["redis"]
        topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname
      preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
      - weight: 100
        podAffinityTerm:
          labelSelector:
            matchLabels:
              tier: database
          topologyKey: topology.kubernetes.io/zone

Parameters Description

  • requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution: Hard requirement for pod co-location. The pod will only be scheduled on nodes where the specified pods already exist. Use for critical performance requirements like placing web servers with their cache layers.
  • preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution: Soft preference for pod co-location with weighted priorities (1-100). The scheduler tries to satisfy these rules but will schedule elsewhere if necessary to maintain application availability.
  • labelSelector: Defines which pods to attract to using label matching. Supports matchLabels for exact matches and matchExpressions for complex queries. Essential for identifying related application components.
  • topologyKey: Defines the scope of affinity (node, zone, region). Common values include kubernetes.io/hostname (same node), topology.kubernetes.io/zone (same zone), or custom topology labels for specific co-location requirements.

Return Value

Returns pod placement decisions based on pod affinity rules. The scheduler evaluates existing pod locations and places new pods accordingly. Includes scheduling events and constraint satisfaction status in pod status.

Examples

Example 1: Skills Search with Redis Cache Co-location

Co-locate Skills search pods with Redis cache pods on the same node for optimal search performance.

# Apply Skills search with Redis affinity
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-search
  labels:
    app: skills-search
    component: web
spec:
  affinity:
    podAffinity:
      requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
      - labelSelector:
          matchLabels:
            app: redis
            tier: cache
        topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname
  containers:
  - name: skills-search
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    command: ["python", "manage.py", "run_skills_search"]
EOF

# Verify co-location with Redis
kubectl get pods -o wide | grep -E "(redis|skills-search)"
pod/anihpj-skills-search created
redis-cache-pod 1/1 Running 0 2m15s 10.244.1.5 worker-node-1 anihpj-skills-search 1/1 Running 0 45s 10.244.1.9 worker-node-1
Example 2: User Session Store with Database Affinity

Prefer User session store pods near database pods but allow different zones if needed.

# Apply User session store with database affinity
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-user-sessions
  labels:
    app: user-sessions
    component: middleware
spec:
  affinity:
    podAffinity:
      preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
      - weight: 80
        podAffinityTerm:
          labelSelector:
            matchExpressions:
            - key: app
              operator: In
              values: ["postgres", "mysql"]
            - key: role
              operator: In
              values: ["primary", "master"]
          topologyKey: topology.kubernetes.io/zone
  containers:
  - name: user-sessions
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    command: ["python", "manage.py", "run_session_store"]
EOF

# Check affinity scheduling results
kubectl describe pod anihpj-user-sessions | grep -A 10 "Pod Affinity"
pod/anihpj-user-sessions created
Pod Affinity: PreferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution: Weight: 80 PodAffinityTerm: LabelSelector: MatchExpressions: key: app operator: In values: postgres,mysql key: role operator: In values: primary,master TopologyKey: topology.kubernetes.io/zone
Example 3: Author Content Processing with Storage Affinity

Co-locate Author content processing pods with persistent storage pods for efficient media processing.

# Apply Author content with storage affinity
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-author-content-processor
  labels:
    app: author-processor
    component: worker
spec:
  affinity:
    podAffinity:
      requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
      - labelSelector:
          matchLabels:
            app: minio
            component: storage
        topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname
      preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
      - weight: 60
        podAffinityTerm:
          labelSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: elasticsearch
              role: data
          topologyKey: topology.kubernetes.io/zone
  containers:
  - name: content-processor
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    command: ["python", "manage.py", "process_author_content"]
EOF

# Monitor pod co-location
kubectl get pods -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,NODE:.spec.nodeName | grep -E "(minio|author-processor)"
pod/anihpj-author-content-processor created
NAME NODE minio-storage-pod worker-node-2 anihpj-author-content-processor worker-node-2
Example 4: Location Services with Analytics Affinity

Prefer Location services near analytics pods for real-time geographic data processing.

# Apply Location services with analytics affinity
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-location-analytics
  labels:
    app: location-analytics
    component: processor
spec:
  affinity:
    podAffinity:
      preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
      - weight: 90
        podAffinityTerm:
          labelSelector:
            matchExpressions:
            - key: app
              operator: In
              values: ["clickhouse", "influxdb"]
            - key: tier
              operator: In
              values: ["analytics"]
          topologyKey: topology.kubernetes.io/zone
      - weight: 70
        podAffinityTerm:
          labelSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: kafka
              component: broker
          topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname
  containers:
  - name: location-analytics
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    command: ["python", "manage.py", "run_location_analytics"]
EOF

# Check affinity compliance
kubectl get pods -l app=location-analytics -o jsonpath='{.items[0].spec.nodeName}'
pod/anihpj-location-analytics created
analytics-zone-1-node
Example 5: JobPost Matching with ML Model Affinity

Co-locate JobPost matching pods with machine learning model servers for low-latency predictions.

# Apply JobPost matching with ML affinity
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-matching
  labels:
    app: jobpost-matching
    component: ml-engine
spec:
  affinity:
    podAffinity:
      requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
      - labelSelector:
          matchLabels:
            app: tensorflow-serving
            model: job-matching
        topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname
      preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
      - weight: 100
        podAffinityTerm:
          labelSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: vector-database
              component: embeddings
          topologyKey: topology.kubernetes.io/zone
  containers:
  - name: jobpost-matching
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    command: ["python", "manage.py", "run_job_matching"]
    env:
    - name: ML_MODEL_ENDPOINT
      value: "http://localhost:8501/v1/models/job-matching"
EOF

# Verify ML model co-location
kubectl describe pod anihpj-jobpost-matching | grep -A 15 Events
pod/anihpj-jobpost-matching created
Events: Type Reason Age From Message ---- ------ ---- ---- ------- Normal Scheduled 92s default-scheduler Successfully assigned default/anihpj-jobpost-matching to ml-node-1 Normal Pulled 91s kubelet Container image "anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0" already present on machine Normal Created 91s kubelet Created container jobpost-matching Normal Started 90s kubelet Started container jobpost-matching

PodAntiAffinity

Official Docs: PodAntiAffinity v1

Pod anti affinity is a group of inter pod anti affinity scheduling rules.

Full Description

PodAntiAffinity ensures pods are scheduled away from other pods with specific labels, promoting high availability and fault tolerance. For your anihpj Django application, use PodAntiAffinity to spread multiple replicas across different nodes or zones, prevent resource contention between similar workloads, and ensure system resilience against node failures.

Required anti-affinity rules prevent co-location absolutely, while preferred rules encourage distribution but allow violations when necessary. This ensures your jobpost application maintains availability even during cluster resource constraints or maintenance operations.

Syntax

# Pod with pod anti-affinity
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
spec:
  affinity:
    podAntiAffinity:
      requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
      - labelSelector:
          matchLabels:
            app: django-web
        topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname
      preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
      - weight: 100
        podAffinityTerm:
          labelSelector:
            matchExpressions:
            - key: component
              operator: In
              values: ["web", "api"]
          topologyKey: topology.kubernetes.io/zone

Parameters Description

  • requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution: Hard requirement preventing co-location. Pods will not be scheduled on nodes containing matching pods. Essential for high availability deployments where replicas must be distributed across different failure domains.
  • preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution: Soft preference for pod separation with weighted priorities (1-100). The scheduler tries to avoid co-location but will violate rules if necessary to maintain overall cluster utilization and pod scheduling.
  • labelSelector: Defines which pods to avoid using label matching. Supports matchLabels for exact matches and matchExpressions for complex queries. Critical for identifying pods that should be separated for reliability.
  • topologyKey: Defines the scope of anti-affinity (node, zone, region). Use kubernetes.io/hostname to prevent same-node scheduling, topology.kubernetes.io/zone for zone distribution, or custom labels for specific separation requirements.

Return Value

Returns pod placement decisions based on anti-affinity rules. The scheduler evaluates existing pod locations and avoids conflicts. Includes scheduling events, constraint violations, and distribution status in pod status.

Examples

Example 1: Skills Service High Availability

Ensure Skills service replicas are never scheduled on the same node for maximum availability.

# Apply Skills service with node anti-affinity
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-service-1
  labels:
    app: skills-service
    component: web
    version: v1
spec:
  affinity:
    podAntiAffinity:
      requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
      - labelSelector:
          matchLabels:
            app: skills-service
            component: web
        topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname
  containers:
  - name: skills-service
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    command: ["python", "manage.py", "run_skills_service"]
EOF

# Deploy second replica to verify anti-affinity
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-service-2
  labels:
    app: skills-service
    component: web
    version: v1
spec:
  affinity:
    podAntiAffinity:
      requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
      - labelSelector:
          matchLabels:
            app: skills-service
            component: web
        topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname
  containers:
  - name: skills-service
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    command: ["python", "manage.py", "run_skills_service"]
EOF

# Verify pods are on different nodes
kubectl get pods -l app=skills-service -o wide
pod/anihpj-skills-service-1 created pod/anihpj-skills-service-2 created
NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE IP NODE anihpj-skills-service-1 1/1 Running 0 45s 10.244.1.8 worker-node-1 anihpj-skills-service-2 1/1 Running 0 30s 10.244.2.9 worker-node-2
Example 2: User Authentication Zone Distribution

Prefer User authentication pods across different availability zones for geographic redundancy.

# Apply User auth with zone anti-affinity
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-user-auth-west
  labels:
    app: user-auth
    component: api
    region: west
spec:
  affinity:
    podAntiAffinity:
      preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
      - weight: 80
        podAffinityTerm:
          labelSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: user-auth
              component: api
          topologyKey: topology.kubernetes.io/zone
      - weight: 60
        podAffinityTerm:
          labelSelector:
            matchExpressions:
            - key: component
              operator: In
              values: ["api", "web"]
          topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname
  containers:
  - name: user-auth
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    command: ["python", "manage.py", "run_auth_service"]
EOF

# Check zone distribution
kubectl get pods -l app=user-auth -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,NODE:.spec.nodeName,ZONE:.metadata.labels.topology\.kubernetes\.io/zone
pod/anihpj-user-auth-west created
NAME NODE ZONE anihpj-user-auth-west worker-node-west us-west-1a anihpj-user-auth-east worker-node-east us-east-1a
Example 3: Author Content Processing Load Balancing

Distribute Author content processing pods to prevent resource contention on individual nodes.

# Apply Author processing with load balancing anti-affinity
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-author-processor-heavy-1
  labels:
    app: author-processor
    workload: heavy
    tier: processing
spec:
  affinity:
    podAntiAffinity:
      requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
      - labelSelector:
          matchLabels:
            app: author-processor
            workload: heavy
        topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname
      preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
      - weight: 90
        podAffinityTerm:
          labelSelector:
            matchExpressions:
            - key: tier
              operator: In
              values: ["processing", "compute"]
          topologyKey: topology.kubernetes.io/zone
  containers:
  - name: author-processor
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    command: ["python", "manage.py", "process_heavy_content"]
    resources:
      requests:
        cpu: "2"
        memory: "4Gi"
EOF

# Monitor resource distribution
kubectl top pods -l app=author-processor --sort-by=memory
pod/anihpj-author-processor-heavy-1 created
NAME CPU(cores) MEMORY(bytes) anihpj-author-processor-heavy-1 1950m 3.8Gi anihpj-author-processor-heavy-2 1890m 3.6Gi anihpj-author-processor-heavy-3 1820m 3.4Gi
Example 4: Location Service Regional Separation

Separate Location services across regions to handle geographic failover and disaster recovery.

# Apply Location service with regional anti-affinity
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-location-service-primary
  labels:
    app: location-service
    role: primary
    datacenter: east
spec:
  affinity:
    podAntiAffinity:
      requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
      - labelSelector:
          matchLabels:
            app: location-service
            role: primary
        topologyKey: topology.kubernetes.io/region
      preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
      - weight: 100
        podAffinityTerm:
          labelSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: location-service
          topologyKey: topology.kubernetes.io/zone
  containers:
  - name: location-service
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    command: ["python", "manage.py", "run_location_primary"]
EOF

# Verify regional distribution
kubectl describe pod anihpj-location-service-primary | grep -A 10 "Anti-affinity"
pod/anihpj-location-service-primary created
Anti-affinity: RequiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution: LabelSelector: MatchLabels: app: location-service role: primary TopologyKey: topology.kubernetes.io/region PreferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution: Weight: 100 PodAffinityTerm: LabelSelector: MatchLabels: app: location-service TopologyKey: topology.kubernetes.io/zone
Example 5: JobPost API Replica Distribution

Ensure JobPost API replicas are spread across nodes and zones for maximum resilience and performance.

# Apply JobPost API with comprehensive anti-affinity
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-api-replica-1
  labels:
    app: jobpost-api
    component: backend
    replica: "1"
spec:
  affinity:
    podAntiAffinity:
      requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
      - labelSelector:
          matchLabels:
            app: jobpost-api
            component: backend
        topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname
      preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
      - weight: 100
        podAffinityTerm:
          labelSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: jobpost-api
          topologyKey: topology.kubernetes.io/zone
      - weight: 80
        podAffinityTerm:
          labelSelector:
            matchExpressions:
            - key: component
              operator: In
              values: ["backend", "api", "web"]
          topologyKey: failure-domain.beta.kubernetes.io/region
  containers:
  - name: jobpost-api
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    command: ["python", "manage.py", "run_jobpost_api"]
    ports:
    - containerPort: 8000
      name: http
EOF

# Create additional replicas and check distribution
for i in {2..3}; do
  kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-api-replica-$i
  labels:
    app: jobpost-api
    component: backend
    replica: "$i"
spec:
  affinity:
    podAntiAffinity:
      requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
      - labelSelector:
          matchLabels:
            app: jobpost-api
            component: backend
        topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname
  containers:
  - name: jobpost-api
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    command: ["python", "manage.py", "run_jobpost_api"]
EOF
done

# Verify replica distribution across nodes
kubectl get pods -l app=jobpost-api -o wide --sort-by=.spec.nodeName
pod/anihpj-jobpost-api-replica-1 created pod/anihpj-jobpost-api-replica-2 created pod/anihpj-jobpost-api-replica-3 created
NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE IP NODE anihpj-jobpost-api-replica-1 1/1 Running 0 2m 10.244.1.8 worker-node-1 anihpj-jobpost-api-replica-2 1/1 Running 0 90s 10.244.2.9 worker-node-2 anihpj-jobpost-api-replica-3 1/1 Running 0 75s 10.244.3.7 worker-node-3

Probe

Official Docs: Probe v1

Probe describes a health check to be performed against a container to determine whether it is alive or ready to receive traffic.

Full Description

Probes are essential health checking mechanisms that monitor your anihpj Django application's container health and readiness. Kubernetes uses three types of probes: liveness probes to restart unhealthy containers, readiness probes to control traffic routing, and startup probes for slow-starting applications. For your jobpost application, properly configured probes ensure automatic recovery from failures, prevent traffic to unhealthy instances, and provide reliable service availability.

Probes can execute commands inside containers, make HTTP requests to endpoints, test TCP connections, or use gRPC health checks. Configure appropriate timing parameters to balance responsiveness with stability, avoiding false positives that could cause unnecessary restarts of healthy Django applications.

Syntax

# Pod with comprehensive health probes
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
spec:
  containers:
  - name: django-app
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    livenessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /health/
        port: 8000
      initialDelaySeconds: 30
      periodSeconds: 10
      timeoutSeconds: 5
      failureThreshold: 3
    readinessProbe:
      exec:
        command: ["python", "manage.py", "check", "--database", "default"]
      initialDelaySeconds: 15
      periodSeconds: 5
    startupProbe:
      tcpSocket:
        port: 8000
      failureThreshold: 30
      periodSeconds: 10

Parameters Description

  • exec: Execute a command inside the container. Perfect for Django management commands that check database connectivity, cache availability, or application-specific health conditions. Return code 0 indicates success.
  • httpGet: Make HTTP GET request to a specific path and port. Ideal for Django health endpoints that validate database connections, external service availability, and application readiness. Success is determined by HTTP status codes 200-399.
  • tcpSocket: Test TCP connection to a specific port. Useful for checking if Django web server is accepting connections without requiring HTTP response validation. Success means successful TCP connection establishment.
  • grpc: Send gRPC health check request. Use for Django applications that expose gRPC services alongside HTTP endpoints, providing standardized health checking for microservice architectures.
  • initialDelaySeconds: Wait time before starting probes. Set to 30-60 seconds for Django applications to complete startup, migrations, and dependency initialization.
  • periodSeconds: Probe execution frequency. Use 10-30 seconds for production Django health checks, balancing responsiveness with resource usage.
  • timeoutSeconds: Maximum probe execution time. Set to 5-10 seconds for Django database-dependent health checks to account for query execution time.
  • failureThreshold: Consecutive failures before considering probe failed. Use 3-5 failures to prevent false positives from temporary Django application delays.
  • successThreshold: Consecutive successes required after failure. Defaults to 1, must be 1 for liveness and startup probes to ensure rapid recovery detection.

Return Value

Returns probe execution results including success/failure status, response time, and error details. Kubernetes uses probe results to make decisions about container restarts, traffic routing, and pod readiness status.

Examples

Example 1: Skills Model Database Health Check

Configure liveness probe to check Skills model database connectivity and restart container on failures.

# Apply Skills service with database health probe
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-health-check
  labels:
    app: skills-service
    component: web
spec:
  containers:
  - name: skills-service
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    command: ["python", "manage.py", "runserver", "0.0.0.0:8000"]
    livenessProbe:
      exec:
        command: 
        - python
        - manage.py
        - shell
        - -c
        - "from jobpost.models import Skills; Skills.objects.count()"
      initialDelaySeconds: 45
      periodSeconds: 30
      timeoutSeconds: 10
      failureThreshold: 3
    readinessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /api/skills/health/
        port: 8000
        httpHeaders:
        - name: X-Health-Check
          value: readiness
      initialDelaySeconds: 20
      periodSeconds: 10
      timeoutSeconds: 5
    ports:
    - containerPort: 8000
      name: http
EOF

# Monitor probe status
kubectl describe pod anihpj-skills-health-check | grep -A 15 "Liveness\|Readiness"
pod/anihpj-skills-health-check created
Liveness: exec [python manage.py shell -c from jobpost.models import Skills; Skills.objects.count()] delay=45s timeout=10s period=30s #success=1 #failure=3 Readiness: http-get http://:8000/api/skills/health/ delay=20s timeout=5s period=10s #success=1 #failure=3 Conditions: Type Status Initialized True Ready True ContainersReady True PodScheduled True
Example 2: User Authentication Service Readiness

Configure readiness probe to ensure User authentication service is ready before receiving traffic.

# Apply User auth with comprehensive probes
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-user-auth-probes
  labels:
    app: user-auth
    component: api
spec:
  containers:
  - name: user-auth
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    command: ["gunicorn", "--bind", "0.0.0.0:8000", "anihpj.wsgi:application"]
    startupProbe:
      tcpSocket:
        port: 8000
      initialDelaySeconds: 10
      periodSeconds: 5
      failureThreshold: 30
      timeoutSeconds: 3
    livenessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /api/auth/ping/
        port: 8000
        scheme: HTTP
      initialDelaySeconds: 30
      periodSeconds: 20
      timeoutSeconds: 8
      failureThreshold: 3
    readinessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /api/auth/ready/
        port: 8000
        httpHeaders:
        - name: User-Agent
          value: Kubernetes-HealthCheck
      initialDelaySeconds: 15
      periodSeconds: 10
      timeoutSeconds: 5
      successThreshold: 1
      failureThreshold: 2
EOF

# Check probe execution results
kubectl get pod anihpj-user-auth-probes -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[?(@.type=="Ready")].status}'
pod/anihpj-user-auth-probes created
True
Example 3: Author Content Processing Startup Probe

Configure startup probe for Author content processing that requires extended initialization time.

# Apply Author processor with startup probe
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-author-content-startup
  labels:
    app: author-processor
    component: worker
spec:
  containers:
  - name: author-processor
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    command: ["python", "manage.py", "run_content_processor"]
    env:
    - name: DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE
      value: "anihpj.settings"
    startupProbe:
      exec:
        command:
        - python
        - manage.py
        - shell
        - -c
        - "from jobpost.models import Author; from django.db import connection; connection.ensure_connection(); print('Startup complete')"
      initialDelaySeconds: 20
      periodSeconds: 10
      failureThreshold: 60
      timeoutSeconds: 15
    livenessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /status/alive/
        port: 8080
      initialDelaySeconds: 30
      periodSeconds: 30
      failureThreshold: 3
    readinessProbe:
      exec:
        command: ["test", "-f", "/tmp/author-processor-ready"]
      initialDelaySeconds: 25
      periodSeconds: 15
    ports:
    - containerPort: 8080
      name: status
EOF

# Monitor startup progression
kubectl get pod anihpj-author-content-startup -w
pod/anihpj-author-content-startup created
NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE anihpj-author-content-startup 0/1 Pending 0 0s anihpj-author-content-startup 0/1 ContainerCreating 0 2s anihpj-author-content-startup 0/1 Running 0 15s anihpj-author-content-startup 1/1 Running 0 45s
Example 4: Location Service TCP and HTTP Probes

Configure multiple probe types for Location service to validate different health aspects.

# Apply Location service with multi-probe setup
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-location-multi-probe
  labels:
    app: location-service
    component: geo-api
spec:
  containers:
  - name: location-service
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    command: ["python", "manage.py", "run_location_service"]
    livenessProbe:
      tcpSocket:
        port: 8000
      initialDelaySeconds: 30
      periodSeconds: 15
      timeoutSeconds: 3
      failureThreshold: 4
    readinessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /api/location/health/
        port: 8000
        httpHeaders:
        - name: Accept
          value: application/json
        - name: X-Service-Check
          value: location-readiness
      initialDelaySeconds: 20
      periodSeconds: 10
      timeoutSeconds: 8
      successThreshold: 1
      failureThreshold: 2
    startupProbe:
      httpGet:
