        path: /api/location/startup/
        port: 8000
      initialDelaySeconds: 15
      periodSeconds: 8
      failureThreshold: 20
    ports:
    - containerPort: 8000
      name: http
EOF

# View detailed probe information
kubectl describe pod anihpj-location-multi-probe | grep -A 25 Containers
pod/anihpj-location-multi-probe created
Containers: location-service: Container ID: containerd://abc123def456 Image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0 State: Running Started: Sat, 14 Sep 2024 11:15:30 +0000 Ready: True Restart Count: 0 Liveness: tcp-socket :8000 delay=30s timeout=3s period=15s #success=1 #failure=4 Readiness: http-get http://:8000/api/location/health/ delay=20s timeout=8s period=10s #success=1 #failure=2 Startup: http-get http://:8000/api/location/startup/ delay=15s timeout=1s period=8s #success=1 #failure=20
Example 5: JobPost API with Custom Health Endpoint

Configure JobPost API with custom health endpoints that validate full application stack health.

# Apply JobPost API with comprehensive health checking
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-api-health
  labels:
    app: jobpost-api
    component: backend
spec:
  containers:
  - name: jobpost-api
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    command: ["python", "manage.py", "runserver", "0.0.0.0:8000"]
    env:
    - name: DEBUG
      value: "False"
    - name: DATABASE_URL
      value: "postgresql://user:pass@postgres:5432/anihpj"
    livenessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /api/jobpost/liveness/
        port: 8000
        httpHeaders:
        - name: Content-Type
          value: application/json
        - name: X-Probe-Type
          value: liveness
      initialDelaySeconds: 40
      periodSeconds: 25
      timeoutSeconds: 10
      failureThreshold: 3
      successThreshold: 1
    readinessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /api/jobpost/readiness/
        port: 8000
        httpHeaders:
        - name: X-Probe-Type
          value: readiness
      initialDelaySeconds: 25
      periodSeconds: 12
      timeoutSeconds: 8
      failureThreshold: 2
    startupProbe:
      exec:
        command:
        - python
        - manage.py
        - check
        - --deploy
        - --fail-level
        - ERROR
      initialDelaySeconds: 20
      periodSeconds: 15
      failureThreshold: 40
      timeoutSeconds: 20
    ports:
    - containerPort: 8000
      name: django-http
    resources:
      requests:
        memory: "512Mi"
        cpu: "250m"
      limits:
        memory: "1Gi"
        cpu: "500m"
EOF

# Monitor all probe types
kubectl get pod anihpj-jobpost-api-health -o jsonpath='{.status.containerStatuses[0].state}' | jq
pod/anihpj-jobpost-api-health created
{ "running": { "startedAt": "2024-09-14T11:18:45Z" } }

PodStatus

Official Docs: PodStatus v1

PodStatus represents information about the status of a pod. Status may trail the actual state of a system, especially if the node that hosts the pod cannot contact the control plane.

Full Description

PodStatus provides comprehensive information about your anihpj Django application's runtime state, including lifecycle phase, network configuration, container health, and resource allocation. Understanding pod status is crucial for monitoring your jobpost application's health, diagnosing deployment issues, and maintaining service reliability. The status reflects real-time information about container execution, networking, and resource utilization.

Pod status includes phase information (Pending, Running, Succeeded, Failed, Unknown), detailed condition states (PodScheduled, ContainersReady, Initialized, Ready), container status for each component, and networking details including assigned IP addresses and node placement.

Syntax

# Get pod status information
kubectl get pod {pod-name} -o yaml | grep -A 50 status:

# Get specific status fields
kubectl get pod {pod-name} -o jsonpath='{.status.phase}'
kubectl get pod {pod-name} -o jsonpath='{.status.podIP}'

# Check pod conditions
kubectl get pod {pod-name} -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[*].type}'

Parameters Description

  • phase: High-level pod lifecycle state (Pending, Running, Succeeded, Failed, Unknown). Critical for understanding Django application deployment progress and current operational state.
  • conditions: Detailed condition array including PodScheduled, ContainersReady, Initialized, and Ready states. Essential for diagnosing Django application startup and health issues.
  • podIP/podIPs: Network IP addresses assigned to the pod. Used for direct communication with Django services and debugging networking issues.
  • hostIP/hostIPs: Node IP addresses where the pod is running. Useful for node-specific debugging and understanding infrastructure placement.
  • containerStatuses: Individual container health information including restart counts, images, and resource usage. Critical for monitoring Django web servers, Celery workers, and database containers.
  • startTime: Pod creation timestamp for performance analysis and debugging startup issues with Django migrations and initialization.
  • qosClass: Quality of Service classification (Guaranteed, Burstable, BestEffort) based on resource requests and limits configuration.

Return Value

Returns comprehensive pod status object containing phase, conditions, networking information, container statuses, and resource allocation details. Status updates reflect real-time pod state and container health.

Examples

Example 1: Monitor Skills Service Status

Check Skills service pod status including phase, readiness, and container health information.

# Create Skills service pod for monitoring
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-service
  labels:
    app: skills-service
    component: web
spec:
  containers:
  - name: skills-web
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    command: ["python", "manage.py", "runserver", "0.0.0.0:8000"]
    readinessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /api/skills/health/
        port: 8000
      initialDelaySeconds: 30
      periodSeconds: 10
EOF

# Get comprehensive status information
kubectl get pod anihpj-skills-service -o yaml | grep -A 80 status:

# Check specific status fields
kubectl get pod anihpj-skills-service -o jsonpath='{.status.phase}{"\n"}{.status.podIP}{"\n"}{.status.conditions[?(@.type=="Ready")].status}'
pod/anihpj-skills-service created
status: conditions: - lastProbeTime: null lastTransitionTime: "2024-09-14T11:10:15Z" status: "True" type: Initialized - lastProbeTime: null lastTransitionTime: "2024-09-14T11:11:30Z" status: "True" type: Ready - lastProbeTime: null lastTransitionTime: "2024-09-14T11:11:30Z" status: "True" type: ContainersReady - lastProbeTime: null lastTransitionTime: "2024-09-14T11:10:15Z" status: "True" type: PodScheduled containerStatuses: - containerID: containerd://abc123def456 image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0 lastState: {} name: skills-web ready: true restartCount: 0 started: true state: running: startedAt: "2024-09-14T11:11:15Z" hostIP: 192.168.1.101 phase: Running podIP: 10.244.1.15 podIPs: - ip: 10.244.1.15 qosClass: BestEffort startTime: "2024-09-14T11:10:15Z"
Running 10.244.1.15 True
Example 2: User Authentication Pod Conditions

Monitor User authentication pod conditions to ensure proper initialization and readiness.

# Check User auth pod conditions
kubectl get pod -l app=user-auth -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.status.phase}{"\t"}{.status.conditions[?(@.type=="Ready")].status}{"\n"}{end}'

# Get detailed condition information
kubectl describe pod -l app=user-auth | grep -A 10 Conditions

# Monitor pod readiness over time
kubectl get pod -l app=user-auth -w --output-watch-events
anihpj-user-auth-1 Running True anihpj-user-auth-2 Running True
Conditions: Type Status Initialized True Ready True ContainersReady True PodScheduled True Conditions: Type Status Initialized True Ready True ContainersReady True PodScheduled True
NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE anihpj-user-auth-1 1/1 Running 0 2m45s anihpj-user-auth-2 1/1 Running 0 2m30s
Example 3: Author Content Processing Container Status

Analyze Author content processing pod container statuses including restart counts and resource usage.

# Get container status details
kubectl get pod -l app=author-processor -o jsonpath='{range .items[*].status.containerStatuses[*]}{.name}{"\t"}{.state}{"\t"}{.restartCount}{"\n"}{end}'

# Check container resource usage
kubectl top pod -l app=author-processor --containers

# View container status with restart information
kubectl get pod -l app=author-processor -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,READY:.status.containerStatuses[0].ready,RESTARTS:.status.containerStatuses[0].restartCount,STATUS:.status.phase
author-processor {"running":{"startedAt":"2024-09-14T11:25:30Z"}} 0
POD NAME CPU(cores) MEMORY(bytes) anihpj-author-processor-1 author-processor 45m 298Mi anihpj-author-processor-2 author-processor 62m 445Mi
NAME READY RESTARTS STATUS anihpj-author-processor-1 true 0 Running anihpj-author-processor-2 true 1 Running
Example 4: Location Service Network Status

Examine Location service pod network configuration including IP addresses and DNS resolution.

# Get network status information
kubectl get pod -l app=location-service -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.status.podIP}{"\t"}{.status.hostIP}{"\n"}{end}'

# Check pod DNS and networking
kubectl exec -it $(kubectl get pod -l app=location-service -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}') -- nslookup kubernetes.default.svc.cluster.local

# Verify network connectivity between pods
kubectl get pod -l app=location-service -o wide
anihpj-location-service-1 10.244.1.20 192.168.1.101 anihpj-location-service-2 10.244.2.18 192.168.1.102
Server: 10.96.0.10 Address: 10.96.0.10#53 Name: kubernetes.default.svc.cluster.local Address: 10.96.0.1
NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE IP NODE anihpj-location-service-1 1/1 Running 0 3m 10.244.1.20 worker-node-1 anihpj-location-service-2 1/1 Running 0 2m 10.244.2.18 worker-node-2
Example 5: JobPost API Quality of Service Status

Monitor JobPost API pod QoS classification and resource allocation status.

# Check QoS class and resource status
kubectl get pod -l app=jobpost-api -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.status.qosClass}{"\t"}{.status.phase}{"\n"}{end}'

# Get detailed resource and status information
kubectl describe pod -l app=jobpost-api | grep -E "(QoS Class|Status|Containers:)"

# Monitor resource allocation and limits
kubectl get pod -l app=jobpost-api -o jsonpath='{range .items[*].spec.containers[*]}{.name}{"\t"}{.resources}{"\n"}{end}' | jq
anihpj-jobpost-api-1 Guaranteed Running anihpj-jobpost-api-2 Burstable Running
QoS Class: Guaranteed Status: Running Containers: QoS Class: Burstable Status: Running Containers:
jobpost-api {"limits":{"cpu":"500m","memory":"1Gi"},"requests":{"cpu":"250m","memory":"512Mi"}}

PodList

Official Docs: PodList v1

PodList is a list of Pods.

Full Description

PodList provides a collection of pods with metadata for bulk operations, monitoring, and management of your anihpj Django application. Use PodList to retrieve multiple pods based on label selectors, field selectors, or namespace filters. This is essential for monitoring your jobpost application's distributed components, performing batch operations, and analyzing system-wide health across Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost services.

PodList supports pagination for handling large numbers of pods, watch operations for real-time updates, and filtering capabilities to target specific subsets of your Django application infrastructure. The list includes complete pod specifications and current status information for each item.

Syntax

# List all pods
kubectl get pods

# List pods with label selector
kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj

# List pods with field selector
kubectl get pods --field-selector status.phase=Running

# Get pods as JSON/YAML
kubectl get pods -o yaml
kubectl get pods -o json | jq '.items[].metadata.name'

Parameters Description

  • apiVersion: API version for the PodList resource, always "v1". Ensures compatibility with Kubernetes API versioning.
  • kind: Resource type identifier, always "PodList". Used by API clients to understand the response structure.
  • metadata: List metadata including resourceVersion for consistency, continue token for pagination, and remainingItemCount for large result sets.
  • items: Array of Pod objects containing complete specifications and status information. Each item represents a fully described pod with all configuration and runtime details.

Return Value

Returns PodList object containing array of pods matching the query criteria, along with metadata for pagination and versioning. Each pod item includes complete specification and current status.

Examples

Example 1: List Skills Service Pods

Retrieve all pods related to Skills service for monitoring and management operations.

# List Skills service pods with details
kubectl get pods -l app=skills-service -o wide

# Get Skills pods with custom output format
kubectl get pods -l app=skills-service -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,STATUS:.status.phase,NODE:.spec.nodeName,IP:.status.podIP

# Export Skills pods configuration
kubectl get pods -l app=skills-service -o yaml > skills-pods-backup.yaml
NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE IP NODE anihpj-skills-service-1 1/1 Running 0 5m 10.244.1.12 worker-node-1 anihpj-skills-service-2 1/1 Running 0 4m 10.244.2.15 worker-node-2
NAME STATUS NODE IP anihpj-skills-service-1 Running worker-node-1 10.244.1.12 anihpj-skills-service-2 Running worker-node-2 10.244.2.15
Example 2: Monitor User Authentication Pods

Watch User authentication pods for real-time status changes and health monitoring.

# List User auth pods with status
kubectl get pods -l component=user-auth --show-labels

# Watch User auth pods for changes
kubectl get pods -l component=user-auth -w

# Get User auth pods with detailed status
kubectl get pods -l component=user-auth -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.status.phase}{"\t"}{.status.conditions[?(@.type=="Ready")].status}{"\n"}{end}'
NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE LABELS anihpj-user-auth-1 1/1 Running 0 3m15s app=user-auth,component=user-auth anihpj-user-auth-2 1/1 Running 0 3m00s app=user-auth,component=user-auth
anihpj-user-auth-1 Running True anihpj-user-auth-2 Running True
Example 3: Author Content Processing Pod Analysis

Analyze Author content processing pods for resource usage and performance monitoring.

# List Author processing pods with resource info
kubectl get pods -l app=author-processor -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.spec.containers[0].resources.requests.memory}{"\t"}{.status.phase}{"\n"}{end}'

# Get top resource consuming Author pods
kubectl top pods -l app=author-processor --sort-by=memory

# Count Author pods by status
kubectl get pods -l app=author-processor -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.status.phase}{"\n"}{end}' | sort | uniq -c
anihpj-author-processor-1 512Mi Running anihpj-author-processor-2 1Gi Running
NAME CPU(cores) MEMORY(bytes) anihpj-author-processor-1 45m 298Mi anihpj-author-processor-2 62m 445Mi
Example 4: Location Service Pod Health Check

Check health status of all Location service pods across the cluster.

# Get Location service pod health summary
kubectl get pods -l tier=location -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.status.containerStatuses[0].ready}{"\t"}{.status.containerStatuses[0].restartCount}{"\n"}{end}'

# List Location pods with age and node information
kubectl get pods -l tier=location -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,AGE:.metadata.creationTimestamp,NODE:.spec.nodeName,STATUS:.status.phase

# Check Location pod readiness across all namespaces
kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -l tier=location --field-selector status.phase=Running
anihpj-location-service-1 true 0 anihpj-location-service-2 true 1
NAME AGE NODE STATUS anihpj-location-service-1 2024-09-14T10:15:30Z worker-node-1 Running anihpj-location-service-2 2024-09-14T10:16:45Z worker-node-2 Running
Example 5: JobPost API Pod Batch Operations

Perform batch operations on JobPost API pods for maintenance and scaling analysis.

# List all JobPost API pods with pagination
kubectl get pods -l app=jobpost-api --limit=10 --continue=""

# Get JobPost pods filtered by multiple criteria
kubectl get pods -l app=jobpost-api,environment=production --field-selector spec.nodeName!=maintenance-node

# Generate JobPost pod report with all details
kubectl get pods -l app=jobpost-api -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,NAMESPACE:.metadata.namespace,STATUS:.status.phase,RESTARTS:.status.containerStatuses[0].restartCount,AGE:.metadata.creationTimestamp,IP:.status.podIP,NODE:.spec.nodeName

# Export JobPost pod list for external analysis
kubectl get pods -l app=jobpost-api -o json | jq '.items[] | {name: .metadata.name, status: .status.phase, ip: .status.podIP, node: .spec.nodeName}' > jobpost-pods-report.json
NAME NAMESPACE STATUS RESTARTS AGE IP NODE anihpj-jobpost-api-1 default Running 0 4m30s 10.244.1.25 worker-node-1 anihpj-jobpost-api-2 default Running 0 4m15s 10.244.2.28 worker-node-2
NAME NAMESPACE STATUS RESTARTS AGE IP NODE anihpj-jobpost-api-1 default Running 0 4m30s 10.244.1.25 worker-node-1 anihpj-jobpost-api-2 default Running 0 4m15s 10.244.2.28 worker-node-2

Pod Operations

Operations Overview

Read - GET single Pod details
List - GET multiple Pods
Create - POST new Pod
Update - PUT/PATCH existing Pod
Delete - DELETE Pod resources
Status - Manage Pod status subresource
Patch - Partially update Pod
Logs - Read Pod container logs

Operations allow you to interact with Pod resources through the Kubernetes API. These operations enable you to manage Django application containers, monitor health status, and troubleshoot deployment issues.

Debug User authentication pods using logs, ephemeral containers, and status operations.

# Get User auth pod status and events
kubectl describe pod -l app=user-auth

# Read logs with timestamps and follow mode
kubectl logs -l app=user-auth --timestamps --follow

# Add debugging ephemeral container
kubectl debug $(kubectl get pod -l app=user-auth -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}') -it --image=busybox --target=user-auth

# Check ephemeral container status
kubectl get pod -l app=user-auth -o jsonpath='{.items[0].status.ephemeralContainerStatuses}'

# Get pod resource usage
kubectl top pod -l app=user-auth
Name: anihpj-user-auth-1 Namespace: default Labels: app=user-auth Status: Running IP: 10.244.1.16 Events: Normal Scheduled 3m default-scheduler Successfully assigned default/anihpj-user-auth-1 to worker-node-1 Normal Pulled 3m kubelet Container image "anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0" already present on machine Normal Created 3m kubelet Created container user-auth Normal Started 3m kubelet Started container user-auth
2024-09-14T11:13:15.123Z [INFO] User authentication service starting... 2024-09-14T11:13:15.456Z [INFO] Database connection established 2024-09-14T11:13:15.789Z [INFO] Redis cache connection successful 2024-09-14T11:13:16.012Z [INFO] User auth service ready on port 8001
Creating debug session for anihpj-user-auth-1 with container busybox
[{"name":"debugger-busybox","state":{"running":{"startedAt":"2024-09-14T11:14:30Z"}},"ready":true,"restartCount":0}]
NAME CPU(cores) MEMORY(bytes) anihpj-user-auth-1 15m 128Mi anihpj-user-auth-2 12m 105Mi
Example 3: Author Content Pod Collection Operations

Manage collections of Author content processing pods using list, filter, and bulk operations.

# List all Author processing pods with custom columns
kubectl get pods -l app=author-processor -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,STATUS:.status.phase,NODE:.spec.nodeName

# Watch Author pods for real-time changes
kubectl get pods -l app=author-processor --watch

# Filter running Author pods only
kubectl get pods -l app=author-processor --field-selector=status.phase=Running

# Delete all failed Author pods
kubectl delete pods -l app=author-processor --field-selector=status.phase=Failed

# Export Author pod configurations
kubectl get pods -l app=author-processor -o yaml > author-pods-backup.yaml
NAME STATUS NODE anihpj-author-processor-1 Running worker-node-1 anihpj-author-processor-2 Running worker-node-2
NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE anihpj-author-processor-1 1/1 Running 0 5m anihpj-author-processor-2 1/1 Running 0 4m anihpj-author-processor-3 0/1 Pending 0 2m
NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE anihpj-author-processor-1 1/1 Running 0 5m anihpj-author-processor-2 1/1 Running 0 4m
pod "anihpj-author-processor-failed" deleted
Exported 2 pods to author-pods-backup.yaml
Example 4: Location Service Pod Status Monitoring

Monitor Location service pod health using various status and condition checks.

# Get detailed pod status information
kubectl get pod -l tier=location -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.status.phase}{"\t"}{.status.conditions[?(@.type=="Ready")].status}{"\n"}{end}'

# Check container readiness and restart counts
kubectl get pod -l tier=location -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.status.containerStatuses[0].ready}{"\t"}{.status.containerStatuses[0].restartCount}{"\n"}{end}'

# Monitor pod resource consumption
kubectl top pod -l tier=location --containers

# Get pod network information
kubectl get pod -l tier=location -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,POD-IP:.status.podIP,HOST-IP:.status.hostIP

# Check pod conditions history
kubectl describe pod -l tier=location | grep -A 10 Conditions
anihpj-location-service-1 Running True anihpj-location-service-2 Running True
anihpj-location-service-1 true 0 anihpj-location-service-2 true 1
POD NAME CPU(cores) MEMORY(bytes) anihpj-location-service-1 location-service 28m 185Mi anihpj-location-service-2 location-service 35m 220Mi
NAME POD-IP HOST-IP anihpj-location-service-1 10.244.1.18 192.168.1.101 anihpj-location-service-2 10.244.2.22 192.168.1.102
Conditions: Type Status Initialized True Ready True ContainersReady True PodScheduled True Conditions: Type Status Initialized True Ready True ContainersReady True PodScheduled True
Example 5: JobPost API Pod Update Operations

Perform various update operations on JobPost API pods including patches and replacements.

# Strategic merge patch to update environment variables
kubectl patch pod anihpj-jobpost-api -p '{
  "spec": {
    "containers": [{
      "name": "jobpost-api",
      "env": [
        {"name": "DEBUG", "value": "False"},
        {"name": "LOG_LEVEL", "value": "INFO"}
      ]
    }]
  }
}'

# JSON patch to add annotations
kubectl patch pod anihpj-jobpost-api --type='json' -p='[
  {"op": "add", "path": "/metadata/annotations/updated-by", "value": "admin"},
  {"op": "add", "path": "/metadata/annotations/update-time", "value": "'$(date -Iseconds)'"}
]'

# Replace pod with updated specification (dry-run first)
kubectl replace -f jobpost-api-updated.yaml --dry-run=client

# Apply changes if dry-run succeeds
kubectl replace -f jobpost-api-updated.yaml

# Verify update completion
kubectl get pod anihpj-jobpost-api -o jsonpath='{.metadata.annotations}' | jq
pod/anihpj-jobpost-api patched
pod/anihpj-jobpost-api patched
pod/anihpj-jobpost-api configured (dry run)
pod/anihpj-jobpost-api replaced
{ "updated-by": "admin", "update-time": "2024-09-14T11:35:45+00:00" }

Read Pod

Read the specified Pod to get complete configuration and status information for your Django application container.

Syntax
kubectl get pod {name} -o yaml
kubectl get pod {name} -o json
kubectl describe pod {name}
HTTP API Syntax
GET /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/pods/{name}
Accept: application/json
Authorization: Bearer {token}
Parameters
  • name (string, required): Name of the Pod
  • namespace (string, required): Object namespace (default: "default")
  • pretty (string): Pretty-print the output
Return Value
  • 200 (Pod): OK - Returns the complete Pod object
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Django Service Examples
Example 1: Skills Service Pod Details (jobpost app)

Read complete Skills service pod configuration to understand container setup and resource usage.

# Get Skills service pod configuration in YAML format
kubectl get pod anihpj-jobpost-skills-pod -o yaml
apiVersion: v1 kind: Pod metadata: name: anihpj-jobpost-skills-pod namespace: default labels: app: anihpj component: jobpost service: skills spec: containers: - name: jobpost-skills image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0 ports: - containerPort: 8000 env: - name: DJANGO_APP value: "jobpost" - name: MODEL_FOCUS value: "skills" status: phase: Running podIP: 10.244.1.15 containerStatuses: - name: jobpost-skills ready: true restartCount: 0
Example 2: User Authentication Pod Description (jobpost app)

Get detailed description of User authentication pod including events and resource consumption.

# Get detailed description of User auth pod
kubectl describe pod anihpj-jobpost-user-pod
Name: anihpj-jobpost-user-pod Namespace: default Node: worker-node-1/192.168.1.101 Labels: app=anihpj component=jobpost service=user Status: Running IP: 10.244.1.16 Containers: jobpost-user: Image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0 Port: 8000/TCP State: Running Ready: True Restart Count: 0 Environment: DJANGO_APP: jobpost MODEL_FOCUS: user Events: Type Reason Age From Message ---- ------ ---- ---- ------- Normal Scheduled 5m default-scheduler Successfully assigned default/anihpj-jobpost-user-pod to worker-node-1 Normal Pulled 5m kubelet Container image "anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0" already present Normal Created 5m kubelet Created container jobpost-user Normal Started 5m kubelet Started container jobpost-user
Example 3: Author Content Pod JSON Output (jobpost app)

Retrieve Author processing pod information in JSON format for programmatic access.

# Get Author pod details in JSON format
kubectl get pod anihpj-jobpost-author-pod -o json
{ "apiVersion": "v1", "kind": "Pod", "metadata": { "name": "anihpj-jobpost-author-pod", "namespace": "default", "labels": { "app": "anihpj", "component": "jobpost", "service": "author" } }, "spec": { "containers": [{ "name": "jobpost-author", "image": "anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0", "env": [{ "name": "DJANGO_APP", "value": "jobpost" }, { "name": "MODEL_FOCUS", "value": "author" }] }] }, "status": { "phase": "Running", "podIP": "10.244.2.18" } }
Example 4: Location Service Pod with Wide Output (jobpost app)

View Location service pod with additional information including node assignment and IP address.

# Get Location pod with wide output showing node and IP
kubectl get pod anihpj-jobpost-location-pod -o wide
NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE IP NODE NOMINATED NODE READINESS GATES anihpj-jobpost-location-pod 1/1 Running 0 10m 10.244.3.20 worker-node-2
Example 5: JobPost Main API Pod Custom Columns (jobpost app)

Extract specific JobPost API pod information using custom column output format.

# Get JobPost pod with custom columns
kubectl get pod anihpj-jobpost-main-pod -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,STATUS:.status.phase,NODE:.spec.nodeName,IP:.status.podIP
NAME STATUS NODE IP anihpj-jobpost-main-pod Running worker-node-3 10.244.1.25

List Pods

List pods in the specified namespace, supporting label selectors and field selectors for filtering Django application pods.

Syntax
kubectl get pods
kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj
kubectl get pods --field-selector status.phase=Running
Parameters
  • namespace (string, required): Object namespace
  • labelSelector (string): Filter by labels (e.g., "app=anihpj")
  • fieldSelector (string): Filter by fields (e.g., "status.phase=Running")
Return Value
  • 200 (PodList): OK - Returns list of Pod objects
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Django Service Examples
Example 1: List Skills Service Pods 
kubectl get pods -l app=skills-service -n anihpj
NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE skills-service-7d4b8c5f9-2xk4m 1/1 Running 0 2h skills-service-7d4b8c5f9-8qw1n 1/1 Running 0 2h skills-service-7d4b8c5f9-mx7p3 1/1 Running 0 1h
Example 2: List User Authentication Pods with Wide Output 
kubectl get pods -l component=user-auth -o wide -n anihpj
NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE IP NODE NOMINATED NODE READINESS GATES user-auth-6b9c4d8f5-4h2k7 1/1 Running 0 3h 10.244.1.5 worker-01 <none> <none> user-auth-6b9c4d8f5-9m5n2 1/1 Running 0 3h 10.244.2.8 worker-02 <none> <none>
Example 3: List Author Content Pods by Status 
kubectl get pods --field-selector=status.phase=Running -l service=author-content -n anihpj
NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE author-content-5f7a9b2c4-7k8l9 1/1 Running 0 4h author-content-5f7a9b2c4-p3q5r 1/1 Running 0 4h
Example 4: List Location Service Pods with Custom Columns 
kubectl get pods -l app=location-service -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,STATUS:.status.phase,NODE:.spec.nodeName -n anihpj
NAME STATUS NODE location-service-8c5d7f9a2-3j6k8 Running worker-01 location-service-8c5d7f9a2-m9p1q Running worker-02 location-service-8c5d7f9a2-x4y7z Running worker-03
Example 5: List All JobPost API Pods with Labels 
kubectl get pods -l tier=api,component=jobpost --show-labels -n anihpj
NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE LABELS jobpost-api-9a7b5c8d1-2f4g6 1/1 Running 0 5h app=jobpost-api,component=jobpost,tier=api,version=v1.2.0 jobpost-api-9a7b5c8d1-8h9j3 1/1 Running 0 5h app=jobpost-api,component=jobpost,tier=api,version=v1.2.0 jobpost-api-9a7b5c8d1-k7l5m 1/1 Running 0 4h app=jobpost-api,component=jobpost,tier=api,version=v1.2.0

Create Pod

Create a new Pod from specification to deploy Django application containers.

Syntax
kubectl create -f pod-spec.yaml
kubectl apply -f pod-spec.yaml
Parameters
  • body (Pod, required): Complete Pod specification
  • namespace (string, required): Target namespace
  • dryRun (string): Validation mode without creation
Return Value
  • 201 (Pod): Created - Pod successfully created
  • 202 (Pod): Accepted - Creation in progress
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Django Service Examples
Example 1: Create Skills Service Pod 
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: skills-service-pod
  namespace: anihpj
  labels:
    app: skills-service
    component: backend
spec:
  containers:
  - name: skills-container
    image: anihpj/skills-service:v1.0.0
    ports:
    - containerPort: 8000
    env:
    - name: DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE
      value: "anihpj.settings"
EOF
pod/skills-service-pod created
Example 2: Create User Authentication Pod with Resources 
kubectl create -f user-auth-pod.yaml
# user-auth-pod.yaml content apiVersion: v1 kind: Pod metadata: name: user-auth-service namespace: anihpj labels: component: user-auth tier: security spec: containers: - name: auth-container image: anihpj/user-auth:v2.1.0 resources: limits: memory: "512Mi" cpu: "500m" requests: memory: "256Mi" cpu: "250m" ports: - containerPort: 8080 --- pod/user-auth-service created
Example 3: Create Author Content Pod with Volume Mounts 
kubectl apply -f author-content-pod.yaml
# Output showing created pod pod/author-content-service created # Pod details kubectl get pod author-content-service -n anihpj -o yaml | head -20 apiVersion: v1 kind: Pod metadata: name: author-content-service namespace: anihpj labels: service: author-content version: v1.5.0 spec: containers: - name: author-container image: anihpj/author-content:v1.5.0 volumeMounts: - mountPath: /app/media name: media-storage
Example 4: Create Location Service Pod with Init Container 
kubectl create pod location-service-init --image=anihpj/location-service:v1.3.0 --namespace=anihpj --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -
pod/location-service-init created # Check pod status during creation kubectl get pod location-service-init -n anihpj --watch NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE location-service-init 0/1 Init:0/1 0 5s location-service-init 0/1 PodInitializing 0 15s location-service-init 1/1 Running 0 25s
Example 5: Create JobPost API Pod with Security Context 
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: jobpost-api-secure
  namespace: anihpj
  labels:
    app: jobpost-api
    security: enabled
spec:
  securityContext:
    runAsUser: 1000
    runAsGroup: 3000
    fsGroup: 2000
  containers:
  - name: jobpost-container
    image: anihpj/jobpost-api:v3.0.0
    securityContext:
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
      readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
    ports:
    - containerPort: 8000
      protocol: TCP
EOF
pod/jobpost-api-secure created # Verify security context kubectl get pod jobpost-api-secure -n anihpj -o jsonpath='{.spec.securityContext}' {"fsGroup":2000,"runAsGroup":3000,"runAsUser":1000}

Update Pod

Replace the specified Pod with updated configuration (limited fields are mutable).

Syntax
kubectl replace -f updated-pod.yaml
kubectl apply -f updated-pod.yaml
Parameters
  • body (Pod, required): Complete updated Pod specification
  • name (string, required): Name of the Pod to update
  • namespace (string, required): Object namespace
Return Value
  • 200 (Pod): OK - Pod successfully updated
  • 201 (Pod): Created - Pod was created
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Django Service Examples
Example 1: Update Skills Service Pod Image 
# Get current pod specification
kubectl get pod skills-service-pod -n anihpj -o yaml > skills-updated.yaml

# Edit the image version and apply
sed -i 's/skills-service:v1.0.0/skills-service:v1.1.0/' skills-updated.yaml
kubectl replace -f skills-updated.yaml
pod/skills-service-pod replaced # Verify the update kubectl get pod skills-service-pod -n anihpj -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[0].image}' anihpj/skills-service:v1.1.0
Example 2: Update User Authentication Pod Labels 
kubectl get pod user-auth-service -n anihpj -o yaml | \
  sed 's/tier: security/tier: production/' | \
  kubectl replace -f -
pod/user-auth-service replaced # Check updated labels kubectl get pod user-auth-service -n anihpj --show-labels NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE LABELS user-auth-service 1/1 Running 0 2h component=user-auth,tier=production
Example 3: Update Author Content Pod Resource Limits 
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: author-content-service
  namespace: anihpj
  labels:
    service: author-content
    version: v1.5.1
spec:
  containers:
  - name: author-container
    image: anihpj/author-content:v1.5.1
    resources:
      limits:
        memory: "1Gi"
        cpu: "1000m"
      requests:
        memory: "512Mi"
        cpu: "500m"
EOF
pod/author-content-service replaced # Verify resource changes kubectl describe pod author-content-service -n anihpj | grep -A 4 "Limits:" Limits: cpu: 1 memory: 1Gi Requests: cpu: 500m memory: 512Mi
Example 4: Update Location Service Pod Environment Variables 
# Update with new environment configuration
kubectl replace -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: location-service-init
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  containers:
  - name: location-container
    image: anihpj/location-service:v1.3.0
    env:
    - name: DATABASE_URL
      value: "postgresql://location_db:5432/locations"
    - name: CACHE_BACKEND
      value: "redis://redis-cluster:6379/2"
    - name: DEBUG_MODE
      value: "false"
EOF
pod/location-service-init replaced # Check environment variables kubectl exec location-service-init -n anihpj -- env | grep -E "(DATABASE_URL|CACHE_BACKEND|DEBUG_MODE)" DATABASE_URL=postgresql://location_db:5432/locations CACHE_BACKEND=redis://redis-cluster:6379/2 DEBUG_MODE=false
Example 5: Update JobPost API Pod with New Configuration 
# Apply updated pod with new config mount
kubectl replace -f updated-jobpost-config.yaml
# updated-jobpost-config.yaml shows: apiVersion: v1 kind: Pod metadata: name: jobpost-api-secure namespace: anihpj spec: containers: - name: jobpost-container image: anihpj/jobpost-api:v3.1.0 volumeMounts: - name: config-volume mountPath: /app/config - name: log-volume mountPath: /app/logs volumes: - name: config-volume configMap: name: jobpost-config - name: log-volume emptyDir: {} # Result: pod/jobpost-api-secure replaced

Patch Pod

Partially update the specified Pod with patch data (strategic merge, JSON merge, or JSON patch).

Syntax
kubectl patch pod {name} -p '{patch-data}'
kubectl patch pod {name} --type='json' -p='[{"op": "replace", "path": "/spec/containers/0/image", "value": "new-image"}]'
Parameters
  • body (Patch, required): Patch data in specified format
  • name (string, required): Name of the Pod to patch
  • namespace (string, required): Object namespace
Return Value
  • 200 (Pod): OK - Pod successfully patched
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Django Service Examples
Example 1: Patch Skills Service Pod Image 
kubectl patch pod skills-service-pod -n anihpj -p '{"spec":{"containers":[{"name":"skills-container","image":"anihpj/skills-service:v1.2.0"}]}}'
pod/skills-service-pod patched # Verify the patched image kubectl get pod skills-service-pod -n anihpj -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[0].image}' anihpj/skills-service:v1.2.0
Example 2: Patch User Authentication Pod Labels with JSON 
kubectl patch pod user-auth-service -n anihpj --type='json' -p='[{"op": "add", "path": "/metadata/labels/environment", "value": "production"}]'
pod/user-auth-service patched # Check added label kubectl get pod user-auth-service -n anihpj -o jsonpath='{.metadata.labels.environment}' production
Example 3: Patch Author Content Pod Resource Requests 
kubectl patch pod author-content-service -n anihpj -p '{
  "spec": {
    "containers": [{
      "name": "author-container",
      "resources": {
        "requests": {
          "memory": "768Mi",
          "cpu": "750m"
        }
      }
    }]
  }
}'
pod/author-content-service patched # Verify resource changes kubectl get pod author-content-service -n anihpj -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[0].resources.requests}' {"cpu":"750m","memory":"768Mi"}
Example 4: Patch Location Service Pod Environment Variable 
kubectl patch pod location-service-init -n anihpj --type='json' -p='[
  {"op": "replace", "path": "/spec/containers/0/env/2/value", "value": "true"}
]'
pod/location-service-init patched # Check environment variable change kubectl exec location-service-init -n anihpj -- env | grep DEBUG_MODE DEBUG_MODE=true
Example 5: Patch JobPost API Pod Annotations 
kubectl patch pod jobpost-api-secure -n anihpj -p '{
  "metadata": {
    "annotations": {
      "deployment.kubernetes.io/revision": "3",
      "monitoring.anihpj.com/scrape": "true",
      "config.anihpj.com/version": "v3.1.0"
    }
  }
}'
pod/jobpost-api-secure patched # Verify annotations kubectl get pod jobpost-api-secure -n anihpj -o jsonpath='{.metadata.annotations}' | jq . { "config.anihpj.com/version": "v3.1.0", "deployment.kubernetes.io/revision": "3", "monitoring.anihpj.com/scrape": "true" }

Delete Pod

Delete the specified Pod and terminate all its containers gracefully.

Syntax
kubectl delete pod {name}
kubectl delete pod {name} --grace-period=30
Parameters
  • name (string, required): Name of the Pod to delete
  • namespace (string, required): Object namespace
  • gracePeriodSeconds (integer): Graceful deletion timeout
Return Value
  • 200 (Pod): OK - Pod successfully deleted
  • 202 (Pod): Accepted - Deletion in progress
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Django Service Examples
Example 1: Delete Skills Service Pod Gracefully 
kubectl delete pod skills-service-pod -n anihpj
pod "skills-service-pod" deleted # Verify deletion kubectl get pod skills-service-pod -n anihpj Error from server (NotFound): pods "skills-service-pod" not found
Example 2: Delete User Authentication Pod with Grace Period 
kubectl delete pod user-auth-service -n anihpj --grace-period=30
pod "user-auth-service" deleted # Monitor deletion process kubectl get pod user-auth-service -n anihpj --watch NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE user-auth-service 1/1 Terminating 0 3h user-auth-service 0/1 Terminating 0 3h # Pod deleted after 30 seconds
Example 3: Force Delete Author Content Pod 
kubectl delete pod author-content-service -n anihpj --force --grace-period=0
warning: Immediate deletion does not wait for confirmation that the running resource has been terminated. The resource may continue to run on the cluster indefinitely. pod "author-content-service" force deleted # Check pod status kubectl get pods -l service=author-content -n anihpj No resources found in anihpj namespace.
Example 4: Delete Location Service Pod and Wait 
kubectl delete pod location-service-init -n anihpj --wait=true --timeout=60s
pod "location-service-init" deleted # Confirm deletion completed kubectl get pod location-service-init -n anihpj Error from server (NotFound): pods "location-service-init" not found # Check if any location pods remain kubectl get pods -l app=location-service -n anihpj No resources found in anihpj namespace.
Example 5: Delete JobPost API Pod with Dry Run 
# First, test deletion with dry-run
kubectl delete pod jobpost-api-secure -n anihpj --dry-run=client
pod "jobpost-api-secure" deleted (dry run) # Actual deletion kubectl delete pod jobpost-api-secure -n anihpj pod "jobpost-api-secure" deleted # Verify all jobpost pods are cleaned up kubectl get pods -l app=jobpost-api -n anihpj No resources found in anihpj namespace.

Read Pod Logs

Read container logs from the specified Pod for debugging and monitoring Django applications.

Syntax
kubectl logs {name}
kubectl logs {name} --follow --tail=100
Parameters
  • name (string, required): Name of the Pod
  • namespace (string, required): Object namespace
  • follow (boolean): Stream logs continuously
  • tailLines (integer): Number of lines from the end
Return Value
  • 200 (string): OK - Returns log content as string
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Django Service Examples
Example 1: Read Skills Service Pod Logs 
kubectl logs skills-service-pod -n anihpj
[2024-01-15 10:30:15] INFO Starting Django server on 0.0.0.0:8000 [2024-01-15 10:30:15] INFO Skills service initialized successfully [2024-01-15 10:30:16] INFO Database connection established [2024-01-15 10:30:16] INFO Loading skills data from database [2024-01-15 10:30:17] INFO Skills API endpoints registered [2024-01-15 10:30:17] INFO Server ready to accept connections
Example 2: Follow User Authentication Pod Logs 
kubectl logs user-auth-service -n anihpj --follow --tail=20
[2024-01-15 10:35:23] INFO User authentication request from 10.244.1.15 [2024-01-15 10:35:23] DEBUG JWT token validation successful [2024-01-15 10:35:23] INFO User 'john.doe@anihpj.com' authenticated successfully [2024-01-15 10:35:45] INFO Password reset request for user ID 1247 [2024-01-15 10:35:45] INFO Email sent to user: password-reset token [2024-01-15 10:36:12] WARNING Failed login attempt for 'invalid@user.com' [2024-01-15 10:36:12] INFO Rate limiting applied to IP 10.244.2.33 # Continues following new logs...
Example 3: Read Author Content Pod Logs with Timestamps 
kubectl logs author-content-service -n anihpj --timestamps --since=1h
2024-01-15T09:45:32.123456789Z [INFO] Content management service started 2024-01-15T09:45:33.789012345Z [INFO] Database migrations check completed 2024-01-15T09:45:34.456789012Z [INFO] Media storage connection verified 2024-01-15T10:15:22.567890123Z [INFO] New article published by author ID 89 2024-01-15T10:25:15.678901234Z [INFO] Content search index updated 2024-01-15T10:35:44.789012345Z [WARNING] High memory usage detected: 85% 2024-01-15T10:45:12.890123456Z [INFO] Automated content backup completed
Example 4: Read Location Service Pod Logs with Container Specification 
kubectl logs location-service-init -c location-container -n anihpj --previous
[2024-01-15 10:20:15] INFO Location service initializing [2024-01-15 10:20:16] INFO Connecting to PostgreSQL database [2024-01-15 10:20:17] INFO Redis cache connection established [2024-01-15 10:20:18] INFO Loading geographical data [2024-01-15 10:20:19] ERROR Failed to load region data for country code 'XY' [2024-01-15 10:20:19] INFO Retrying region data load [2024-01-15 10:20:20] INFO Location service ready [2024-01-15 10:20:21] INFO Listening on port 8000
Example 5: Read JobPost API Pod Logs with Filtering 
kubectl logs jobpost-api-secure -n anihpj | grep -E "(ERROR|WARNING)" | tail -10
[2024-01-15 09:30:15] WARNING High request rate detected from IP 10.244.1.88 [2024-01-15 09:45:23] ERROR Database connection timeout in view 'list_jobs' [2024-01-15 09:45:24] WARNING Retrying database connection (attempt 2/3) [2024-01-15 10:12:34] ERROR Invalid job posting data: missing required field 'location' [2024-01-15 10:25:67] WARNING Cache miss for popular jobs query [2024-01-15 10:30:45] ERROR Failed to process payment webhook from provider [2024-01-15 10:35:12] WARNING Elasticsearch index synchronization delayed [2024-01-15 10:40:23] ERROR User session validation failed for token 'abc123...' [2024-01-15 10:45:34] WARNING Memory usage above 90% threshold [2024-01-15 10:50:45] ERROR Email notification service unavailable

Binding

Binding Overview

Operations - API operations for binding management
Official Docs: Binding v1 API | API Version: v1

Binding ties one object to another; for example, a pod is bound to a node by a scheduler.

Full Description

Binding is a fundamental Kubernetes resource that establishes relationships between objects, most commonly used by the scheduler to bind pods to specific nodes. In your anihpj Django application deployment, the Kubernetes scheduler automatically creates Binding objects when it assigns your Django pods to worker nodes based on resource requirements, node selectors, affinity rules, and cluster policies.

While you typically don't create Binding objects manually, understanding them is crucial for debugging scheduling issues, implementing custom schedulers, or building advanced deployment strategies for your jobpost application. The Binding resource contains minimal information: the source object (usually a pod) and the target object (typically a node) that should be bound together.

Syntax

# Create a binding (typically done by scheduler)
kubectl create binding {binding-name} --namespace={namespace}

# View binding via pod information
kubectl get pod {pod-name} -o wide
kubectl describe pod {pod-name} | grep Node:

# Check node assignments
kubectl get pods -o custom-columns=POD:.metadata.name,NODE:.spec.nodeName

# Monitor scheduling events
kubectl get events --field-selector reason=Scheduled

YAML Structure

apiVersion: v1
kind: Binding
metadata:
  name: anihpj-django-web-binding
  namespace: default
target:
  apiVersion: v1
  kind: Node
  name: worker-node-1

Parameters Description

  • apiVersion: API version for the Binding resource, always "v1". Ensures compatibility with Kubernetes API versioning.
  • kind: Resource type identifier, always "Binding". Used by API clients and the scheduler to understand the object type.
  • metadata: Standard Kubernetes metadata including name, namespace, labels, and annotations. The name typically corresponds to the object being bound.
  • target: ObjectReference to the target object that the source should be bound to. Required field containing apiVersion, kind, name, and optionally namespace and resourceVersion of the target object.

Return Value

Returns the created Binding object with confirmed target assignment. The scheduler uses this to finalize pod-to-node assignments and update pod status accordingly.

Examples

Example 1: Skills Service Pod Binding

Monitor how Skills service pods are bound to nodes by the scheduler.

# Create Skills service pod and monitor binding
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-service
  labels:
    app: skills-service
    component: web
spec:
  containers:
  - name: skills-web
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    resources:
      requests:
        memory: "256Mi"
        cpu: "250m"
EOF

# Check pod binding status
kubectl get pod anihpj-skills-service -o wide

# View scheduling events
kubectl describe pod anihpj-skills-service | grep -A 5 Events:
pod/anihpj-skills-service created
NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE IP NODE anihpj-skills-service 1/1 Running 0 30s 10.244.1.15 worker-node-1
Events: Type Reason Age From Message ---- ------ ---- ---- ------- Normal Scheduled 45s default-scheduler Successfully assigned default/anihpj-skills-service to worker-node-1 Normal Pulled 44s kubelet Container image "anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0" already present on machine
Example 2: User Authentication Pod Node Selection

Create User authentication pod with node selector and observe binding behavior.

# Create User auth pod with node selector
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-user-auth
  labels:
    app: user-auth
    component: security
spec:
  nodeSelector:
    kubernetes.io/os: linux
    node-type: worker
  containers:
  - name: user-auth
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    command: ["python", "manage.py", "run_user_auth"]
EOF

# Monitor node assignment
kubectl get pod anihpj-user-auth -o jsonpath='{.spec.nodeName}'

# Check all pod-node bindings
kubectl get pods -o custom-columns=POD:.metadata.name,NODE:.spec.nodeName,STATUS:.status.phase
pod/anihpj-user-auth created
worker-node-2
POD NODE STATUS anihpj-skills-service worker-node-1 Running anihpj-user-auth worker-node-2 Running
Example 3: Author Content Processing with Affinity

Deploy Author processing pod with node affinity and monitor scheduler binding decisions.

# Create Author processing pod with node affinity
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-author-processor
  labels:
    app: author-processor
    component: backend
spec:
  affinity:
    nodeAffinity:
      requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
        nodeSelectorTerms:
        - matchExpressions:
          - key: node-type
            operator: In
            values: ["worker", "compute"]
  containers:
  - name: author-processor
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    resources:
      requests:
        memory: "512Mi"
        cpu: "500m"
EOF

# Check binding result
kubectl get pod anihpj-author-processor -o wide

# Verify scheduling decision
kubectl get events --field-selector involvedObject.name=anihpj-author-processor
pod/anihpj-author-processor created
NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE IP NODE anihpj-author-processor 1/1 Running 0 25s 10.244.3.18 worker-node-3
LAST SEEN TYPE REASON OBJECT MESSAGE 30s Normal Scheduled pod/anihpj-author-processor Successfully assigned default/anihpj-author-processor to worker-node-3 29s Normal Pulling pod/anihpj-author-processor Pulling image "anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0"
Example 4: Location Service Manual Binding Simulation

Demonstrate manual binding creation for Location service debugging scenarios.

# Create unscheduled Location pod (for demonstration)
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-location-service
  labels:
    app: location-service
    component: geo-api
spec:
  schedulerName: manual-scheduler  # Non-existent scheduler
  containers:
  - name: location-service
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    command: ["python", "manage.py", "run_location_service"]
EOF

# Check pod scheduling status
kubectl get pod anihpj-location-service

# Create manual binding (normally done by scheduler)
kubectl create -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Binding
metadata:
  name: anihpj-location-service
  namespace: default
target:
  apiVersion: v1
  kind: Node
  name: worker-node-1
EOF
pod/anihpj-location-service created
NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE anihpj-location-service 0/1 Pending 0 15s
binding/anihpj-location-service created
Example 5: JobPost API Binding Analysis

Analyze JobPost API pod distribution and binding patterns across cluster nodes.

# Deploy multiple JobPost API replicas
for i in {1..3}; do
  kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-api-$i
  labels:
    app: jobpost-api
    replica: "$i"
spec:
  containers:
  - name: jobpost-api
    image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    resources:
      requests:
        memory: "256Mi"
        cpu: "250m"
EOF
done

# Analyze binding distribution
kubectl get pods -l app=jobpost-api -o custom-columns=POD:.metadata.name,NODE:.spec.nodeName,STATUS:.status.phase

# Check node resource utilization
kubectl top nodes
pod/anihpj-jobpost-api-1 created pod/anihpj-jobpost-api-2 created pod/anihpj-jobpost-api-3 created
POD NODE STATUS anihpj-jobpost-api-1 worker-node-1 Running anihpj-jobpost-api-2 worker-node-2 Running anihpj-jobpost-api-3 worker-node-3 Running
NAME CPU(cores) CPU% MEMORY(bytes) MEMORY% worker-node-1 450m 22% 1024Mi 25% worker-node-2 380m 19% 896Mi 22% worker-node-3 520m 26% 1152Mi 28%

Binding Operations

Operations Overview

Create Binding - POST general binding object
Create Pod Binding - POST pod to node binding

Operations allow you to create binding relationships between Kubernetes resources through the API. These operations are primarily used by the scheduler but understanding them helps with debugging and custom scheduling scenarios.

Create Binding

Create a general binding object to establish relationships between Kubernetes resources, primarily used by the scheduler.

Syntax
kubectl create binding {binding-name} --namespace={namespace}
curl -X POST /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/bindings
HTTP API Syntax
POST /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/bindings
Content-Type: application/json
Authorization: Bearer {token}
Parameters
  • body (Binding, required): Complete Binding object specification
  • namespace (string, required): Target namespace for the binding
  • dryRun (string): Validation mode without actual creation
  • fieldManager (string): Field management identifier
Return Value
  • 201 (Binding): Created - Binding successfully created
  • 202 (Binding): Accepted - Creation in progress
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
  • 409: Conflict - Binding already exists
5 Django Service Examples
Example 1: Skills Service Binding Creation

Create binding for Skills service pod to specific node (typically done by scheduler).

# Create Skills service binding
curl -X POST \
  http://localhost:8080/api/v1/namespaces/default/bindings \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{
    "apiVersion": "v1",
    "kind": "Binding",
    "metadata": {
      "name": "anihpj-skills-service",
      "namespace": "default"
    },
    "target": {
      "apiVersion": "v1",
      "kind": "Node",
      "name": "worker-node-1"
    }
  }'

# Verify binding creation
kubectl get pod anihpj-skills-service -o wide
{ "kind": "Binding", "apiVersion": "v1", "metadata": { "name": "anihpj-skills-service", "namespace": "default", "creationTimestamp": "2024-09-14T11:30:00Z" }, "target": { "kind": "Node", "apiVersion": "v1", "name": "worker-node-1" } }
NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE IP NODE anihpj-skills-service 1/1 Running 0 45s 10.244.1.15 worker-node-1
Example 2: User Authentication Service Binding

Create binding for User authentication pod with error handling.

# Attempt User auth binding with kubectl
kubectl create -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Binding
metadata:
  name: anihpj-user-auth
  namespace: default
target:
  apiVersion: v1
  kind: Node
  name: worker-node-2
EOF

# Check for scheduling conflicts
kubectl get events --field-selector involvedObject.name=anihpj-user-auth
binding/anihpj-user-auth created
LAST SEEN TYPE REASON OBJECT MESSAGE 20s Normal Scheduled pod/anihpj-user-auth Successfully assigned default/anihpj-user-auth to worker-node-2
Example 3: Author Content Service Binding

Create binding for Author content processing pod with specific node requirements.

# Create Author content binding
curl -X POST \
  http://localhost:8080/api/v1/namespaces/default/bindings \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{
    "apiVersion": "v1",
    "kind": "Binding",
    "metadata": {
      "name": "anihpj-author-content",
      "namespace": "default"
    },
    "target": {
      "apiVersion": "v1",
      "kind": "Node",
      "name": "worker-node-3"
    }
  }'

# Verify author pod placement
kubectl get pod anihpj-author-content -o jsonpath='{.spec.nodeName}'
{ "kind": "Binding", "apiVersion": "v1", "metadata": { "name": "anihpj-author-content", "namespace": "default", "creationTimestamp": "2024-09-14T11:32:15Z" }, "target": { "kind": "Node", "apiVersion": "v1", "name": "worker-node-3" } }
worker-node-3
Example 4: Location Service Binding with Validation

Create Location service binding with dry-run validation to ensure node compatibility.

# Validate Location service binding first
curl -X POST \
  "http://localhost:8080/api/v1/namespaces/default/bindings?dryRun=All" \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{
    "apiVersion": "v1",
    "kind": "Binding",
    "metadata": {
      "name": "anihpj-location-service",
      "namespace": "default"
    },
    "target": {
      "apiVersion": "v1",
      "kind": "Node",
      "name": "worker-node-1"
    }
  }'

# Create actual binding after validation
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Binding
metadata:
  name: anihpj-location-service
  namespace: default
target:
  apiVersion: v1
  kind: Node
  name: worker-node-1
EOF
{ "kind": "Binding", "apiVersion": "v1", "metadata": { "name": "anihpj-location-service", "creationTimestamp": null }, "target": { "kind": "Node", "apiVersion": "v1", "name": "worker-node-1" } }
binding/anihpj-location-service created
Example 5: JobPost API Service Binding with Error Handling

Create JobPost API binding with comprehensive error handling and verification.

# Create JobPost API binding with error checking
kubectl create -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Binding
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-api
  namespace: default
target:
  apiVersion: v1
  kind: Node
  name: worker-node-2
EOF

# Check binding status and pod events
kubectl describe pod anihpj-jobpost-api | grep -A5 Events:
kubectl get pod anihpj-jobpost-api -o wide
binding/anihpj-jobpost-api created
Events: Type Reason Age From Message ---- ------ ---- ---- ------- Normal Scheduled 30s default-scheduler Successfully assigned default/anihpj-jobpost-api to worker-node-2 Normal Pulled 25s kubelet Container image "anihpj/jobpost-api:v1.0.0" already present on machine Normal Created 24s kubelet Created container jobpost-api
NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE IP NODE NOMINATED NODE READINESS GATES anihpj-jobpost-api 1/1 Running 0 45s 10.244.2.30 worker-node-2 <none> <none>

Create Pod Binding

Create binding specifically for pod resources using the pod subresource endpoint, commonly used by schedulers.

Syntax
curl -X POST /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/pods/{name}/binding
kubectl create binding {pod-name} --target-node={node-name}
HTTP API Syntax
POST /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/pods/{name}/binding
Content-Type: application/json
Authorization: Bearer {token}
Parameters
  • body (Binding, required): Binding object with target node information
  • name (string, required): Name of the pod to bind
  • namespace (string, required): Pod namespace
  • dryRun (string): Validation mode without actual binding
  • fieldManager (string): Field management identifier
Return Value
  • 201 (Status): Created - Pod successfully bound to node
  • 202 (Status): Accepted - Binding in progress
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
  • 409: Conflict - Pod already bound
5 Django Service Examples
Example 1: Author Content Pod Binding

Bind Author processing pod to specific node using pod binding endpoint.

# Create Author pod binding via API
curl -X POST \
  http://localhost:8080/api/v1/namespaces/default/pods/anihpj-author-processor/binding \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{
    "apiVersion": "v1",
    "kind": "Binding",
    "metadata": {
      "name": "anihpj-author-processor"
    },
    "target": {
      "apiVersion": "v1",
      "kind": "Node",
      "name": "worker-node-3"
    }
  }'

# Verify pod binding result
kubectl describe pod anihpj-author-processor | grep Node:
{ "kind": "Status", "apiVersion": "v1", "metadata": {}, "status": "Success", "code": 201 }
Node: worker-node-3/192.168.1.103
Example 2: Location Service Pod Binding with Validation

Create Location service pod binding with dry-run validation first.

# Validate Location pod binding (dry-run)
curl -X POST \
  "http://localhost:8080/api/v1/namespaces/default/pods/anihpj-location-service/binding?dryRun=All" \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{
    "apiVersion": "v1",
    "kind": "Binding",
    "metadata": {
      "name": "anihpj-location-service"
    },
    "target": {
      "apiVersion": "v1",
      "kind": "Node",
      "name": "worker-node-1"
    }
  }'

# Create actual binding after validation
kubectl create -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Binding
metadata:
  name: anihpj-location-service
target:
  apiVersion: v1
  kind: Node
  name: worker-node-1
EOF
{ "kind": "Binding", "apiVersion": "v1", "metadata": { "name": "anihpj-location-service", "creationTimestamp": null }, "target": { "kind": "Node", "apiVersion": "v1", "name": "worker-node-1" } }
binding/anihpj-location-service created
Example 3: JobPost API Multi-Pod Binding

Create bindings for multiple JobPost API pods across different nodes.

# Create bindings for JobPost API pod replicas
for i in {1..3}; do
  node_num=$i
  kubectl create -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Binding
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-api-$i
  namespace: default
target:
  apiVersion: v1
  kind: Node
  name: worker-node-$node_num
EOF
done

# Verify binding distribution
kubectl get pods -l app=jobpost-api -o wide --sort-by=.spec.nodeName
binding/anihpj-jobpost-api-1 created binding/anihpj-jobpost-api-2 created binding/anihpj-jobpost-api-3 created
NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE IP NODE anihpj-jobpost-api-1 1/1 Running 0 60s 10.244.1.20 worker-node-1 anihpj-jobpost-api-2 1/1 Running 0 58s 10.244.2.25 worker-node-2 anihpj-jobpost-api-3 1/1 Running 0 55s 10.244.3.30 worker-node-3
Example 4: Skills Service Pod Binding with API

Bind Skills service pod to specific node using direct API call with authentication.

# Bind Skills pod using pod binding endpoint
curl -X POST \
  http://localhost:8080/api/v1/namespaces/default/pods/anihpj-skills-service/binding \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIs...' \
  -d '{
    "apiVersion": "v1",
    "kind": "Binding",
    "metadata": {
      "name": "anihpj-skills-service"
    },
    "target": {
      "apiVersion": "v1",
      "kind": "Node",
      "name": "worker-node-1"
    }
  }'

# Verify Skills pod binding
kubectl get pod anihpj-skills-service -o jsonpath='{.spec.nodeName}{"\n"}{.status.phase}'
{ "kind": "Status", "apiVersion": "v1", "metadata": {}, "status": "Success", "code": 201 }
worker-node-1 Running
Example 5: User Authentication Pod Binding with Conflict Resolution

Handle pod binding conflicts and verify successful binding for User authentication service.

# Attempt User auth pod binding with conflict handling
curl -X POST \
  http://localhost:8080/api/v1/namespaces/default/pods/anihpj-user-auth/binding \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{
    "apiVersion": "v1",
    "kind": "Binding",
    "metadata": {
      "name": "anihpj-user-auth"
    },
    "target": {
      "apiVersion": "v1",
      "kind": "Node",
      "name": "worker-node-3"
    }
  }'

# Check for binding conflicts and pod status
kubectl describe pod anihpj-user-auth | grep -E "(Node:|Status:|Events:)" -A3
kubectl get events --field-selector involvedObject.name=anihpj-user-auth --sort-by='.firstTimestamp'
{ "kind": "Status", "apiVersion": "v1", "metadata": {}, "status": "Success", "message": "binding to node worker-node-3 was successful", "code": 201 }
Node: worker-node-3/192.168.1.105 Status: Running Events: Type Reason Age From Message ---- ------ ---- ---- ------- Normal Scheduled 2m default-scheduler Successfully assigned default/anihpj-user-auth to worker-node-3 FIRST SEEN LAST SEEN COUNT NAME KIND SUBOBJECT TYPE REASON SOURCE MESSAGE 5m 5m 1 anihpj-user-auth Pod Normal Scheduled default-scheduler Successfully assigned default/anihpj-user-auth to worker-node-3

PodTemplate

PodTemplate Overview

PodTemplateSpec - Template specification for pods
PodTemplateList - Collection of pod templates
Operations - API operations for management
Official Docs: PodTemplate v1 API | API Version: v1

PodTemplate describes a template for creating copies of a predefined pod.

Full Description

PodTemplate is a fundamental Kubernetes resource that defines a template for creating identical pods. When developing your anihpj Django application, PodTemplates provide a blueprint that other resources like ReplicationControllers, Deployments, and Jobs can use to create pods with consistent configuration. This ensures that every instance of your jobpost application runs with the same container specifications, environment variables, and resource requirements.

While you rarely create PodTemplates directly, understanding them is crucial because they form the foundation for all higher-level workload resources. Every time you create a Deployment or ReplicationController for your Django application, you're essentially defining a PodTemplate that specifies how your containers should be configured and deployed across your cluster.

Syntax

# Create a PodTemplate from YAML file
kubectl create -f podtemplate.yaml

# List all PodTemplates
kubectl get podtemplates

# Get detailed information about a specific PodTemplate
kubectl describe podtemplate {name}

# Get PodTemplate in YAML format
kubectl get podtemplate {name} -o yaml

# Get PodTemplate in JSON format
kubectl get podtemplate {name} -o json

# Delete a specific PodTemplate
kubectl delete podtemplate {name}

YAML Structure

apiVersion: v1
kind: PodTemplate
metadata:
  name: anihpj-django-template
  namespace: default
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: web
template:
  metadata:
    labels:
      app: anihpj
      component: web
  spec:
    containers:
    - name: django-app
      image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
      ports:
      - containerPort: 8000
      env:
      - name: DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE
        value: "anihpj.settings"
      - name: DATABASE_URL
        value: "postgresql://db:5432/anihpj"

Parameters

  • apiVersion (string, required): API version, always "v1" for PodTemplate
  • kind (string, required): Resource type, always "PodTemplate"
  • metadata (ObjectMeta): Standard object metadata including name, namespace, labels, and annotations
  • template (PodTemplateSpec, required): Template that defines the pods to be created from this template

Return Value

  • PodTemplate object: Complete template specification with metadata and pod template details
  • metadata: Object metadata including creation timestamp and resource version
  • template: Pod template specification that will be used to create pods

5 Django Service Examples

Example 1: Skills Service PodTemplate

Create a template for Skills service pods with specific resource requirements.

# Create Skills service PodTemplate
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: PodTemplate
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-template
  namespace: default
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    service: skills
template:
  metadata:
    labels:
      app: anihpj
      service: skills
  spec:
    containers:
    - name: skills-container
      image: anihpj/jobpost-skills:v1.0.0
      ports:
      - containerPort: 8000
      env:
      - name: SERVICE_TYPE
        value: "skills"
EOF
podtemplate/anihpj-skills-template created
Example 2: User Authentication PodTemplate

Create a template for User authentication service with security configurations.

# Create User auth PodTemplate with security context
kubectl create -f user-auth-template.yaml
podtemplate/anihpj-user-auth-template created
Example 3: Author Content PodTemplate

Create a template for Author content management service with volume mounts.

# Create Author content PodTemplate
kubectl apply -f author-content-template.yaml
podtemplate/anihpj-author-content-template created
Example 4: Location Service PodTemplate

Create a template for Location service with custom resource limits.

# Create Location service PodTemplate
kubectl create podtemplate anihpj-location-template --image=anihpj/jobpost-location:v1.0.0 --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -
podtemplate/anihpj-location-template created
Example 5: JobPost API PodTemplate

Create a comprehensive template for JobPost API service with full configuration.

# Create JobPost API PodTemplate with comprehensive config
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: PodTemplate
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-api-template
  namespace: default
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    service: jobpost-api
    tier: backend
template:
  metadata:
    labels:
      app: anihpj
      service: jobpost-api
      tier: backend
  spec:
    containers:
    - name: jobpost-api
      image: anihpj/jobpost-api:v2.0.0
      ports:
      - containerPort: 8000
      resources:
        requests:
          memory: "256Mi"
          cpu: "250m"
        limits:
          memory: "512Mi"
          cpu: "500m"
EOF
podtemplate/anihpj-jobpost-api-template created

PodTemplateSpec

PodTemplateSpec describes the data a pod should have when created from a template. This specification contains the metadata and pod specification that will be applied to every pod created from this template.

Syntax
# View PodTemplateSpec within a PodTemplate
kubectl get podtemplate {name} -o jsonpath='{.template}'

# Extract just the spec part of the template
kubectl get podtemplate {name} -o jsonpath='{.template.spec}'
Parameters
  • metadata (ObjectMeta): Standard object metadata that will be applied to created pods
  • spec (PodSpec): Specification of the desired behavior of pods created from this template
Return Value
  • PodTemplateSpec object: Template specification containing metadata and pod spec
  • Complete pod definition: Ready-to-use specification for pod creation
5 Django Service Examples
Example 1: Skills Service Template Spec

Extract and examine the template specification for Skills service.

# Get Skills template spec
kubectl get podtemplate anihpj-skills-template -o jsonpath='{.template}' | jq .
{ "metadata": { "labels": { "app": "anihpj", "service": "skills" } }, "spec": { "containers": [ { "env": [ { "name": "SERVICE_TYPE", "value": "skills" } ], "image": "anihpj/jobpost-skills:v1.0.0", "name": "skills-container", "ports": [ { "containerPort": 8000 } ] } ] } }
Example 2: User Auth Template Spec

View User authentication template specification with security context.

# Get User auth template spec
kubectl describe podtemplate anihpj-user-auth-template
Name: anihpj-user-auth-template Namespace: default Labels: app=anihpj service=user-auth Annotations: Template: Labels: app=anihpj service=user-auth Containers: user-auth-container: Image: anihpj/jobpost-user-auth:v1.0.0 Port: 8080/TCP Host Port: 0/TCP Environment: AUTH_SECRET_KEY: Optional: false Mounts: Volumes:
Example 3: Author Content Template Spec

Examine Author content template spec with volume configurations.

# Get Author content template spec
kubectl get podtemplate anihpj-author-content-template -o yaml | grep -A20 "template:"
template: metadata: labels: app: anihpj service: author-content spec: containers: - image: anihpj/jobpost-author:v1.0.0 name: author-container ports: - containerPort: 8000 volumeMounts: - mountPath: /app/media name: media-storage volumes: - name: media-storage persistentVolumeClaim: claimName: author-media-pvc
Example 4: Location Service Template Spec

View Location service template spec with resource specifications.

# Get Location template spec with resource details
kubectl get podtemplate anihpj-location-template -o jsonpath='{.template.spec.containers[0].resources}' | jq .
{ "limits": { "cpu": "300m", "memory": "384Mi" }, "requests": { "cpu": "150m", "memory": "192Mi" } }
Example 5: JobPost API Template Spec

Examine comprehensive JobPost API template specification.

# Get JobPost API template spec
kubectl get podtemplate anihpj-jobpost-api-template -o jsonpath='{.template.spec}' | jq .
{ "containers": [ { "image": "anihpj/jobpost-api:v2.0.0", "name": "jobpost-api", "ports": [ { "containerPort": 8000 } ], "resources": { "limits": { "cpu": "500m", "memory": "512Mi" }, "requests": { "cpu": "250m", "memory": "256Mi" } } } ] }

PodTemplateList

PodTemplateList is a collection of PodTemplate resources. This list format is returned when querying multiple pod templates and provides a standardized way to handle collections of templates in your Django application infrastructure.

Syntax
# List all PodTemplates in current namespace
kubectl get podtemplates

# List all PodTemplates across all namespaces
kubectl get podtemplates --all-namespaces

# List PodTemplates with labels
kubectl get podtemplates -l app=anihpj

# Get PodTemplateList in JSON format
kubectl get podtemplates -o json
Parameters
  • apiVersion (string, required): API version, always "v1" for PodTemplateList
  • kind (string, required): Resource type, always "PodTemplateList"
  • metadata (ListMeta): Standard list metadata including resource version and continue token
  • items ([]PodTemplate, required): Array of PodTemplate objects
Return Value
  • PodTemplateList object: Collection containing multiple PodTemplate resources
  • items array: List of individual PodTemplate objects with their specifications
  • metadata: List metadata for pagination and resource versioning
5 Django Service Examples
Example 1: List All Skills Service Templates

List all PodTemplates related to Skills service with filtering.

# List Skills service templates
kubectl get podtemplates -l service=skills
NAME AGE anihpj-skills-template 5m
Example 2: List User Auth Templates with Details

Get detailed list of User authentication templates.

# List User auth templates with wide output
kubectl get podtemplates -l service=user-auth -o wide
NAME AGE anihpj-user-auth-template 3m
Example 3: List Author Content Templates JSON

Get Author content templates in JSON list format.

# Get Author templates as JSON list
kubectl get podtemplates -l service=author-content -o json | jq '.items[].metadata.name'
"anihpj-author-content-template"
Example 4: List Location Service Templates by Age

List Location service templates sorted by creation time.

# List Location templates sorted by age
kubectl get podtemplates -l service=location --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp
NAME AGE anihpj-location-template 2m
Example 5: List All JobPost Templates

Get comprehensive list of all JobPost-related templates.

# List all JobPost templates with labels
kubectl get podtemplates -l app=anihpj --show-labels
NAME AGE LABELS anihpj-skills-template 5m app=anihpj,service=skills anihpj-user-auth-template 3m app=anihpj,service=user-auth anihpj-author-content-template 4m app=anihpj,service=author-content anihpj-location-template 2m app=anihpj,service=location anihpj-jobpost-api-template 1m app=anihpj,service=jobpost-api,tier=backend

Operations

Operations Overview

Read - GET single PodTemplate details
List - GET multiple PodTemplates
Create - POST new PodTemplate
Update - PUT existing PodTemplate
Patch - PATCH PodTemplate fields
Delete - DELETE PodTemplate resources
Delete Collection - DELETE multiple PodTemplates

Operations allow you to interact with PodTemplate resources through the Kubernetes API. These operations enable you to manage Django application templates, create reusable pod configurations, and maintain consistent deployments.

Read PodTemplate

Read the specified PodTemplate to get complete template configuration and metadata for your Django application deployments.

Syntax
kubectl get podtemplate {name} -o yaml
kubectl get podtemplate {name} -o json
kubectl describe podtemplate {name}
Parameters
  • name (string, required): Name of the PodTemplate
  • namespace (string, required): Object namespace (default: "default")
  • pretty (string): Pretty-print the output
Return Value
  • 200 (PodTemplate): OK - Returns the complete PodTemplate object
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Django Service Examples
Example 1: Read Skills Service Template

Get complete Skills service PodTemplate configuration in YAML format.

# Get Skills template configuration
kubectl get podtemplate anihpj-skills-template -o yaml
apiVersion: v1 kind: PodTemplate metadata: creationTimestamp: "2024-09-14T12:00:00Z" labels: app: anihpj service: skills name: anihpj-skills-template namespace: default resourceVersion: "12345" uid: abc123-def456-ghi789 template: metadata: labels: app: anihpj service: skills spec: containers: - env: - name: SERVICE_TYPE value: skills image: anihpj/jobpost-skills:v1.0.0 name: skills-container ports: - containerPort: 8000
Example 2: Read User Auth Template Details

Get detailed description of User authentication PodTemplate.

# Describe User auth template
kubectl describe podtemplate anihpj-user-auth-template
Name: anihpj-user-auth-template Namespace: default Labels: app=anihpj service=user-auth Annotations: Template: Labels: app=anihpj service=user-auth Containers: user-auth-container: Image: anihpj/jobpost-user-auth:v1.0.0 Port: 8080/TCP Host Port: 0/TCP Environment: AUTH_SECRET_KEY: Optional: false Mounts: Volumes: Events:
Example 3: Read Author Content Template JSON

Get Author content PodTemplate in JSON format for processing.

# Get Author template in JSON
kubectl get podtemplate anihpj-author-content-template -o json | jq '.template.spec'
{ "containers": [ { "image": "anihpj/jobpost-author:v1.0.0", "name": "author-container", "ports": [ { "containerPort": 8000 } ], "volumeMounts": [ { "mountPath": "/app/media", "name": "media-storage" } ] } ], "volumes": [ { "name": "media-storage", "persistentVolumeClaim": { "claimName": "author-media-pvc" } } ] }
Example 4: Read Location Template with Custom Output

Get Location service template with custom column output.

# Get Location template with custom columns
kubectl get podtemplate anihpj-location-template -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,AGE:.metadata.creationTimestamp,IMAGE:.template.spec.containers[0].image
NAME AGE IMAGE anihpj-location-template 2024-09-14T12:05:00Z anihpj/jobpost-location:v1.0.0
Example 5: Read JobPost API Template Resources

Get JobPost API template focusing on resource specifications.

# Get JobPost API template resources
kubectl get podtemplate anihpj-jobpost-api-template -o jsonpath='{.template.spec.containers[0].resources}' | jq .
{ "limits": { "cpu": "500m", "memory": "512Mi" }, "requests": { "cpu": "250m", "memory": "256Mi" } }

List PodTemplates

List PodTemplates in the specified namespace, supporting label selectors and field selectors for filtering Django application templates.

Syntax
kubectl get podtemplates
kubectl get podtemplates -l app=anihpj
kubectl get podtemplates --all-namespaces
Parameters
  • namespace (string, required): Object namespace
  • labelSelector (string): Filter by labels (e.g., "app=anihpj")
  • fieldSelector (string): Filter by fields
  • watch (boolean): Watch for changes
Return Value
  • 200 (PodTemplateList): OK - Returns list of PodTemplate objects
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Django Service Examples
Example 1: List All Skills Templates

List all PodTemplates for Skills service with label filtering.

# List Skills service templates
kubectl get podtemplates -l service=skills
NAME AGE anihpj-skills-template 10m
Example 2: List User Auth Templates Wide

List User authentication templates with detailed output.

# List User auth templates with wide output
kubectl get podtemplates -l service=user-auth -o wide
NAME AGE anihpj-user-auth-template 8m
Example 3: List Author Content Templates with Labels

List Author content templates showing all labels.

# List Author templates with labels
kubectl get podtemplates -l service=author-content --show-labels
NAME AGE LABELS anihpj-author-content-template 9m app=anihpj,service=author-content
Example 4: List Location Templates Sorted

List Location service templates sorted by creation time.

# List Location templates sorted by age
