            command: ["python", "manage.py", "process_user_notifications"]
            env:
            - name: NOTIFICATION_BATCH_SIZE
              value: "100"
            - name: EMAIL_BACKEND
              value: "django.core.mail.backends.smtp.EmailBackend"
            - name: MODEL_FOCUS
              value: "user"
            envFrom:
            - secretRef:
                name: email-credentials
            resources:
              requests:
                memory: "200Mi"
                cpu: "150m"
              limits:
                memory: "400Mi"
                cpu: "300m"
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
    # Get the complete CronJobSpec with formatting
kubectl get cronjob user-notifications -o yaml | grep -A 50 "spec:"
    spec: concurrencyPolicy: Allow failedJobsHistoryLimit: 5 jobTemplate: metadata: creationTimestamp: null spec: template: metadata: annotations: batch.kubernetes.io/cronjob: user-notifications creationTimestamp: null labels: app: anihpj component: user-notifications model: user spec: containers: - command: - python - manage.py - process_user_notifications env: - name: NOTIFICATION_BATCH_SIZE value: "100" - name: EMAIL_BACKEND value: django.core.mail.backends.smtp.EmailBackend - name: MODEL_FOCUS value: user envFrom: - secretRef: name: email-credentials image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0 name: notification-processor resources: limits: cpu: 300m memory: 400Mi requests: cpu: 150m memory: 200Mi restartPolicy: OnFailure schedule: 0 8,12,18 * * * successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 10 timeZone: America/New_York

    CronJobStatus

    CronJobStatus represents the current state of a cron job, including information about active jobs, last execution times, and scheduling history. For Django applications, this provides crucial monitoring data for recurring tasks and batch operations.

    Syntax
    status:
  active:
  - apiVersion: batch/v1
    kind: Job
    name: cronjob-name-27845620
    namespace: anihpj-system
    resourceVersion: "12345"
    uid: a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890
  lastScheduleTime: "2024-01-15T14:30:00Z"
  lastSuccessfulTime: "2024-01-15T14:32:45Z"
    Parameters Description
    • active ([]ObjectReference): List of currently running jobs spawned by this CronJob. Each reference includes apiVersion, kind, name, namespace, resourceVersion, and uid.
    • lastScheduleTime (Time): Information about when the last job was scheduled. Important for tracking Django task execution patterns and detecting missed schedules.
    • lastSuccessfulTime (Time): Information about when the last successful job was completed. Critical for monitoring Django application health and task reliability.
    • conditions ([]Condition): Optional. Current service state conditions. For Django apps, may include scheduling errors or resource constraints.
    Return Value

    Returns the CronJobStatus object containing the current execution state and history for the Django application's scheduled tasks.

    • 200 OK: CronJobStatus successfully retrieved
    • 401 Unauthorized: Authentication required
    • 403 Forbidden: Insufficient permissions to access CronJobStatus
    • 404 Not Found: CronJob or namespace not found
    5 Practical Django Examples
    Example 1: Skills Model Cleanup Status

    This example shows monitoring the status of a Skills model cleanup CronJob, tracking active jobs and execution history for the jobpost app.

    status:
  active:
  - apiVersion: batch/v1
    kind: Job
    name: skills-cleanup-27845720
    namespace: anihpj-system
    resourceVersion: "98765"
    uid: skills-123e4567-e89b-12d3-a456-426614174000
  lastScheduleTime: "2024-01-15T03:00:00Z"
  lastSuccessfulTime: "2024-01-15T03:02:30Z"
    # Check Skills cleanup CronJob status
kubectl get cronjob skills-cleanup -o jsonpath='{.status}' | jq
    { "active": [ { "apiVersion": "batch/v1", "kind": "Job", "name": "skills-cleanup-27845720", "namespace": "anihpj-system", "resourceVersion": "98765", "uid": "skills-123e4567-e89b-12d3-a456-426614174000" } ], "lastScheduleTime": "2024-01-15T03:00:00Z", "lastSuccessfulTime": "2024-01-15T03:02:30Z" }
    Example 2: Author Reports Execution Monitoring

    This example demonstrates monitoring Author model report generation status, showing completed execution without active jobs.

    status:
  active: []
  lastScheduleTime: "2024-01-15T09:00:00Z"
  lastSuccessfulTime: "2024-01-15T09:05:12Z"
    # Monitor Author reports execution status
kubectl describe cronjob author-reports | grep -A 10 "Status:"
    Status: Active: Last Schedule Time: Mon, 15 Jan 2024 09:00:00 +0000 Last Successful Time: Mon, 15 Jan 2024 09:05:12 +0000 Events: Type Reason Age From Message ---- ------ ---- ---- ------- Normal SuccessfulCreate 5m cronjob-controller Created job author-reports-27845650 Normal SawCompletedJob 30s cronjob-controller Saw completed job: author-reports-27845650
    Example 3: Location Sync Long-Running Status

    This example shows the status of a Location model synchronization job that's currently running, demonstrating active job tracking.

    status:
  active:
  - apiVersion: batch/v1
    kind: Job
    name: location-sync-27845800
    namespace: anihpj-system
    resourceVersion: "87654"
    uid: loc-456e7890-f12g-34h5-i678-901234567890
  - apiVersion: batch/v1
    kind: Job
    name: location-sync-27845830
    namespace: anihpj-system
    resourceVersion: "87655"
    uid: loc-789a0123-b45c-67d8-e901-234567890123
  lastScheduleTime: "2024-01-15T15:30:00Z"
  lastSuccessfulTime: "2024-01-15T14:32:15Z"
    # Check Location sync active jobs
kubectl get jobs -l app=anihpj,component=location-sync --field-selector status.phase=Running
    NAME COMPLETIONS DURATION AGE location-sync-27845800 0/1 5m12s 5m12s location-sync-27845830 0/1 2m45s 2m45s
    Example 4: JobPost Archive Failed Status

    This example demonstrates monitoring a JobPost archival CronJob that experienced failures, showing troubleshooting information.

    status:
  active: []
  lastScheduleTime: "2024-01-01T02:00:00Z"
  lastSuccessfulTime: "2023-12-01T02:05:30Z"
  conditions:
  - type: Failed
    status: "True"
    lastTransitionTime: "2024-01-01T02:15:00Z"
    reason: DeadlineExceeded
    message: "Job was active longer than specified deadline"
    # Investigate JobPost archive failure status
kubectl get cronjob jobpost-archive -o yaml | grep -A 20 "status:"
    status: active: [] conditions: - lastTransitionTime: "2024-01-01T02:15:00Z" message: Job was active longer than specified deadline reason: DeadlineExceeded status: "True" type: Failed lastScheduleTime: "2024-01-01T02:00:00Z" lastSuccessfulTime: "2023-12-01T02:05:30Z"
    Example 5: User Notifications Real-time Status

    This example shows comprehensive status monitoring for User model notification processing, including multiple active jobs during peak processing.

    status:
  active:
  - apiVersion: batch/v1
    kind: Job
    name: user-notifications-27845900
    namespace: anihpj-system
    resourceVersion: "76543"
    uid: user-901b2345-c67d-89e0-f123-456789012345
  lastScheduleTime: "2024-01-15T18:00:00Z"
  lastSuccessfulTime: "2024-01-15T18:03:45Z"
    # Get comprehensive User notifications status with events
kubectl describe cronjob user-notifications | tail -20
    Status: Active: 1 (user-notifications-27845900) Last Schedule Time: Mon, 15 Jan 2024 18:00:00 +0000 Last Successful Time: Mon, 15 Jan 2024 18:03:45 +0000 Events: Type Reason Age From Message ---- ------ ---- ---- ------- Normal SuccessfulCreate 3m cronjob-controller Created job user-notifications-27845900 Normal SuccessfulCreate 3h cronjob-controller Created job user-notifications-27845750 Normal SawCompletedJob 3h3m cronjob-controller Saw completed job: user-notifications-27845750 Normal SuccessfulCreate 6h cronjob-controller Created job user-notifications-27845600 Normal SawCompletedJob 6h4m cronjob-controller Saw completed job: user-notifications-27845600 Normal SuccessfulCreate 9h cronjob-controller Created job user-notifications-27845450 Normal SawCompletedJob 9h2m cronjob-controller Saw completed job: user-notifications-27845450

    CronJobList

    CronJobList is a collection of cron jobs, useful for managing multiple scheduled tasks across your Django application infrastructure. This list provides comprehensive oversight of all recurring maintenance, reporting, and data processing jobs.

    Syntax
    apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJobList
metadata:
  resourceVersion: "12345"
  selfLink: /apis/batch/v1/cronjobs
items:
- apiVersion: batch/v1
  kind: CronJob
  metadata:
    name: skills-maintenance
    namespace: anihpj-system
    labels:
      app: anihpj
      model: skills
  spec:
    schedule: "0 3 * * *"
- apiVersion: batch/v1
  kind: CronJob
  metadata:
    name: user-notifications
    namespace: anihpj-system
    labels:
      app: anihpj
      model: user
  spec:
    schedule: "0 8,12,18 * * *"
    Parameters Description
    • apiVersion (string): Always "batch/v1" for CronJobList. Indicates the API version for batch operations in Kubernetes.
    • kind (string): Always "CronJobList". Specifies this is a collection of CronJob resources.
    • metadata (ListMeta): Standard list metadata including resourceVersion for tracking changes across the Django application's scheduled tasks.
    • items ([]CronJob): Array of CronJob objects. Each item represents a scheduled task in your Django application infrastructure.
    Return Value

    Returns the CronJobList object containing all CronJob resources in the specified namespace or across namespaces for the Django application.

    • 200 OK: CronJobList successfully retrieved
    • 401 Unauthorized: Authentication required
    • 403 Forbidden: Insufficient permissions to list CronJobs
    • 404 Not Found: Namespace not found
    5 Practical Django Examples
    Example 1: All Skills-Related CronJobs

    This example shows listing all CronJobs related to Skills model management, including cleanup, validation, and synchronization tasks.

    apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJobList
metadata:
  resourceVersion: "45678"
items:
- apiVersion: batch/v1
  kind: CronJob
  metadata:
    name: skills-cleanup
    namespace: anihpj-system
    labels:
      app: anihpj
      model: skills
      component: maintenance
  spec:
    schedule: "0 3 * * *"
- apiVersion: batch/v1
  kind: CronJob
  metadata:
    name: skills-validation
    namespace: anihpj-system
    labels:
      app: anihpj
      model: skills
      component: validation
  spec:
    schedule: "0 */6 * * *"
    # List all Skills-related CronJobs
kubectl get cronjobs -l model=skills -o name
    cronjob.batch/skills-cleanup cronjob.batch/skills-validation cronjob.batch/skills-sync
    Example 2: Author and User Model CronJobs

    This example demonstrates listing CronJobs for both Author and User models, showing comprehensive user management and reporting tasks.

    apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJobList
metadata:
  resourceVersion: "56789"
items:
- apiVersion: batch/v1
  kind: CronJob
  metadata:
    name: author-reports
    namespace: anihpj-system
    labels:
      app: anihpj
      model: author
  spec:
    schedule: "0 9 * * 1"
- apiVersion: batch/v1
  kind: CronJob
  metadata:
    name: user-notifications
    namespace: anihpj-system
    labels:
      app: anihpj
      model: user
  spec:
    schedule: "0 8,12,18 * * *"
- apiVersion: batch/v1
  kind: CronJob
  metadata:
    name: user-cleanup
    namespace: anihpj-system
    labels:
      app: anihpj
      model: user
  spec:
    schedule: "0 2 * * 0"
    # List CronJobs for Author and User models
kubectl get cronjobs -l 'model in (author,user)' --sort-by=.metadata.name
    NAME SCHEDULE SUSPEND ACTIVE LAST SCHEDULE AGE author-reports 0 9 * * 1 False 0 2d 5d user-cleanup 0 2 * * 0 False 0 1d 3d user-notifications 0 8,12,18 * * * False 1 4h 7d
    Example 3: Location and JobPost Processing CronJobs

    This example shows listing CronJobs responsible for Location and JobPost model processing, including geo-sync and archival operations.

    apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJobList
metadata:
  resourceVersion: "67890"
items:
- apiVersion: batch/v1
  kind: CronJob
  metadata:
    name: location-sync
    namespace: anihpj-system
    labels:
      app: anihpj
      model: location
      component: sync
  spec:
    schedule: "0 * * * *"
- apiVersion: batch/v1
  kind: CronJob
  metadata:
    name: jobpost-archive
    namespace: anihpj-system
    labels:
      app: anihpj
      model: jobpost
      component: archive
  spec:
    schedule: "0 2 1 * *"
- apiVersion: batch/v1
  kind: CronJob
  metadata:
    name: jobpost-indexing
    namespace: anihpj-system
    labels:
      app: anihpj
      model: jobpost
      component: search
  spec:
    schedule: "*/30 * * * *"
    # Get detailed CronJobList for Location and JobPost models
kubectl get cronjobs -l 'model in (location,jobpost)' -o wide
    NAME SCHEDULE SUSPEND ACTIVE LAST SCHEDULE AGE CONTAINERS IMAGES SELECTOR jobpost-archive 0 2 1 * * False 0 15d 1M jobpost-archiver anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0 jobpost-indexing */30 * * * * False 0 25m 2d search-indexer anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0 location-sync 0 * * * * False 1 5m 10d location-sync anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0
    Example 4: Complete anihpj Application CronJobs

    This example demonstrates retrieving all CronJobs across the entire anihpj Django application, providing full infrastructure oversight.

    apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJobList
metadata:
  resourceVersion: "78901"
items:
- apiVersion: batch/v1
  kind: CronJob
  metadata:
    name: skills-maintenance
    labels:
      app: anihpj
      model: skills
- apiVersion: batch/v1
  kind: CronJob
  metadata:
    name: author-analytics
    labels:
      app: anihpj
      model: author
- apiVersion: batch/v1
  kind: CronJob
  metadata:
    name: user-notifications
    labels:
      app: anihpj
      model: user
- apiVersion: batch/v1
  kind: CronJob
  metadata:
    name: location-geocoding
    labels:
      app: anihpj
      model: location
- apiVersion: batch/v1
  kind: CronJob
  metadata:
    name: jobpost-processing
    labels:
      app: anihpj
      model: jobpost
    # List all anihpj application CronJobs with status
kubectl get cronjobs -l app=anihpj -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,SCHEDULE:.spec.schedule,ACTIVE:.status.active,LAST:.status.lastScheduleTime
    NAME SCHEDULE ACTIVE LAST author-analytics 0 9 * * 1 2024-01-15T09:00:00Z jobpost-processing 0 1 * * * 2024-01-16T01:00:00Z location-geocoding 0 */4 * * * 2024-01-16T04:00:00Z skills-maintenance 0 3 * * * 2024-01-16T03:00:00Z user-notifications 0 8,12,18 * * * 1 2024-01-16T08:00:00Z
    Example 5: Cross-Namespace CronJob Discovery

    This example shows advanced CronJobList usage for discovering scheduled tasks across multiple namespaces in a multi-environment Django setup.

    apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJobList
metadata:
  resourceVersion: "89012"
items:
- apiVersion: batch/v1
  kind: CronJob
  metadata:
    name: data-backup
    namespace: anihpj-production
    labels:
      app: anihpj
      environment: production
      component: backup
  spec:
    schedule: "0 1 * * *"
- apiVersion: batch/v1
  kind: CronJob
  metadata:
    name: test-data-reset
    namespace: anihpj-staging
    labels:
      app: anihpj
      environment: staging
      component: testing
  spec:
    schedule: "0 6 * * *"
- apiVersion: batch/v1
  kind: CronJob
  metadata:
    name: dev-cache-clear
    namespace: anihpj-development
    labels:
      app: anihpj
      environment: development
      component: maintenance
  spec:
    schedule: "*/15 * * * *"
    # List CronJobs across all anihpj namespaces
kubectl get cronjobs --all-namespaces -l app=anihpj -o json | jq '.items | length'
    12

    🔧 CronJob Operations Overview

    Read - Get CronJob details Read Status - Get CronJob status List - List CronJobs in namespace List All - List across namespaces Create - Create new CronJob Update - Replace CronJob Update Status - Update CronJob status Patch - Partially update CronJob Patch Status - Update status fields Delete - Remove CronJob Delete Collection - Remove multiple CronJobs
    📖 Read CronJob

    HTTP Request: GET /apis/batch/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/cronjobs/{name}

    Retrieve detailed information about a specific CronJob, including its schedule, job template, and current status.

    # Read specific CronJob
kubectl get cronjob jobpost-cleanup -n anihpj-system -o yaml
    apiVersion: batch/v1 kind: CronJob metadata: name: jobpost-cleanup namespace: anihpj-system resourceVersion: "12345" spec: concurrencyPolicy: Allow failedJobsHistoryLimit: 1 jobTemplate: spec: template: spec: containers: - command: ["python", "manage.py", "cleanup_expired_jobs"] image: anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0 name: cleanup schedule: "0 3 * * *" successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 3 suspend: false status: lastScheduleTime: "2024-01-15T03:00:00Z"
    📊 Read CronJob Status

    HTTP Request: GET /apis/batch/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/cronjobs/{name}/status

    Get the current status information of a CronJob, including active jobs and last execution times.

    # Get CronJob status
kubectl get cronjob weekly-reports -n anihpj-system -o jsonpath='{.status}'
    { "active": [ { "apiVersion": "batch/v1", "kind": "Job", "name": "weekly-reports-27845740", "namespace": "anihpj-system" } ], "lastScheduleTime": "2024-01-15T09:00:00Z", "lastSuccessfulTime": "2024-01-15T09:05:30Z" }
    📋 List CronJobs

    HTTP Request: GET /apis/batch/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/cronjobs

    List all CronJobs in a specific namespace with their schedules and status information.

    # List CronJobs in namespace
kubectl get cronjobs -n anihpj-system
    NAME SCHEDULE SUSPEND ACTIVE LAST SCHEDULE AGE cache-refresh 0 * * * * False 0 52m 2d daily-notifications 0 8 * * * False 0 14h 5d jobpost-cleanup 0 3 * * * False 1 3h 7d
    🌐 List All Namespaces CronJobs

    HTTP Request: GET /apis/batch/v1/cronjobs

    List all CronJobs across all namespaces in the cluster.

    # List all CronJobs across namespaces
kubectl get cronjobs --all-namespaces
    NAMESPACE NAME SCHEDULE SUSPEND ACTIVE LAST SCHEDULE AGE anihpj-system cache-refresh 0 * * * * False 0 52m 2d anihpj-system daily-notifications 0 8 * * * False 0 14h 5d anihpj-system jobpost-cleanup 0 3 * * * False 1 3h 7d kube-system system-maintenance 0 4 * * 0 False 0 4d 30d
    ➕ Create CronJob

    HTTP Request: POST /apis/batch/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/cronjobs

    Create a new CronJob to run scheduled tasks for your Django application.

    # Create CronJob from YAML file
kubectl apply -f location-sync-cronjob.yaml
    cronjob.batch/location-sync created
    # Create CronJob using kubectl command
kubectl create cronjob author-stats --image=anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0 --schedule="0 12 * * 0" --restart=OnFailure -- python manage.py generate_author_stats
    cronjob.batch/author-stats created
    🔄 Update CronJob

    HTTP Request: PUT /apis/batch/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/cronjobs/{name}

    Replace the entire CronJob configuration with updated specifications.

    # Update CronJob from file
kubectl apply -f updated-cronjob.yaml
    cronjob.batch/jobpost-cleanup configured
    # Update CronJob schedule
kubectl patch cronjob jobpost-cleanup -n anihpj-system -p '{"spec":{"schedule":"0 4 * * *"}}'
    cronjob.batch/jobpost-cleanup patched
    📊 Update CronJob Status

    HTTP Request: PUT /apis/batch/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/cronjobs/{name}/status

    Update the status subresource of a CronJob (typically done by the controller).

    # Check current status
kubectl get cronjob daily-notifications -n anihpj-system -o jsonpath='{.status}' | jq
    { "lastScheduleTime": "2024-01-15T08:00:00Z", "lastSuccessfulTime": "2024-01-15T08:02:15Z" }
    🔧 Patch CronJob

    HTTP Request: PATCH /apis/batch/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/cronjobs/{name}

    Partially update specific fields of a CronJob without replacing the entire resource.

    # Suspend CronJob
kubectl patch cronjob cache-refresh -n anihpj-system -p '{"spec":{"suspend":true}}'
    cronjob.batch/cache-refresh patched
    # Update job history limits
kubectl patch cronjob weekly-reports -n anihpj-system -p '{"spec":{"successfulJobsHistoryLimit":5,"failedJobsHistoryLimit":3}}'
    cronjob.batch/weekly-reports patched
    📊 Patch CronJob Status

    HTTP Request: PATCH /apis/batch/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/cronjobs/{name}/status

    Partially update the status fields of a CronJob.

    # View current status before patch
kubectl get cronjob monthly-archive -n anihpj-system -o jsonpath='{.status}'
    { "lastScheduleTime": "2024-01-01T02:00:00Z", "lastSuccessfulTime": "2024-01-01T02:45:30Z" }
    🗑️ Delete CronJob

    HTTP Request: DELETE /apis/batch/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/cronjobs/{name}

    Remove a CronJob and stop future scheduled executions. Active jobs will continue running.

    # Delete specific CronJob
kubectl delete cronjob author-stats -n anihpj-system
    cronjob.batch "author-stats" deleted
    # Delete with grace period
kubectl delete cronjob location-sync -n anihpj-system --grace-period=300
    cronjob.batch "location-sync" deleted
    🗑️ Delete CronJob Collection

    HTTP Request: DELETE /apis/batch/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/cronjobs

    Delete multiple CronJobs at once using label selectors or other criteria.

    # Delete CronJobs by label
kubectl delete cronjobs -n anihpj-system -l app=maintenance
    cronjob.batch "cache-refresh" deleted cronjob.batch "jobpost-cleanup" deleted
    # Delete all CronJobs in namespace (be careful!)
kubectl delete cronjobs -n anihpj-system --all
    cronjob.batch "daily-notifications" deleted cronjob.batch "monthly-archive" deleted cronjob.batch "weekly-reports" deleted

    📈 HorizontalPodAutoscaler v1

    HorizontalPodAutoscaler automatically scales the number of pods in a deployment, replica set, stateful set, or replication controller based on observed CPU utilization. For Django applications, this ensures your anihpj application can handle varying loads by automatically adding or removing pods.

    📋 HorizontalPodAutoscaler v1 Commands Overview

    HorizontalPodAutoscaler

    Main resource configuration

    HorizontalPodAutoscalerSpec

    Autoscaling specification

    HorizontalPodAutoscalerStatus

    Current autoscaling status

    HorizontalPodAutoscalerList

    Collection of autoscalers

    Operations

    CRUD operations for HPA

    ✨ Enhanced Documentation: This guide features professional syntax highlighting powered by Prism.js, matching the quality of industry-standard documentation. All examples use your exact Django models with realistic outputs.

    HorizontalPodAutoscaler

    HorizontalPodAutoscaler is a configuration for horizontal pod autoscaling in Kubernetes. It automatically adjusts the number of pod replicas in a deployment, replica set, or replication controller based on observed CPU utilization or custom metrics. For Django applications like anihpj, this ensures optimal resource usage and availability during traffic fluctuations.

    Syntax
    apiVersion: autoscaling/v1
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: anihpj-hpa
  namespace: anihpj-system
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: autoscaler
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: anihpj-jobpost-app
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 10
  targetCPUUtilizationPercentage: 70
    Parameters Description
    • apiVersion (string): Always "autoscaling/v1" for HorizontalPodAutoscaler v1. Indicates the API version being used.
    • kind (string): Always "HorizontalPodAutoscaler". Specifies the resource type being created.
    • metadata (ObjectMeta): Standard object metadata including name, namespace, labels, and annotations for organizing autoscalers.
    • spec (HorizontalPodAutoscalerSpec): Required. Defines the autoscaling behavior, target reference, and scaling limits.
    • status (HorizontalPodAutoscalerStatus): Current status information about the autoscaler including current replicas and last scale time.
    Return Value

    Returns the HorizontalPodAutoscaler object containing the complete autoscaling configuration for the Django application.

    • 200 OK: HorizontalPodAutoscaler successfully retrieved
    • 401 Unauthorized: Authentication required
    • 403 Forbidden: Insufficient permissions to access HorizontalPodAutoscaler
    • 404 Not Found: HorizontalPodAutoscaler or namespace not found
    5 Practical Django Examples
    Example 1: Skills Service Autoscaling

    This example shows a HorizontalPodAutoscaler for the Skills model service, automatically scaling based on CPU usage for handling skill searches and updates.

    apiVersion: autoscaling/v1
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: skills-service-hpa
  namespace: anihpj-system
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: skills
    model: skills
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: skills-service
  minReplicas: 1
  maxReplicas: 5
  targetCPUUtilizationPercentage: 60
    # Create Skills service HPA
kubectl apply -f skills-hpa.yaml
    horizontalpodautoscaler.autoscaling/skills-service-hpa created
    Example 2: User Authentication Autoscaling

    This example demonstrates autoscaling for User model authentication service, handling login spikes and user registration traffic.

    apiVersion: autoscaling/v1
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: user-auth-hpa
  namespace: anihpj-system
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: authentication
    model: user
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: user-auth-service
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 8
  targetCPUUtilizationPercentage: 75
    # Get User authentication HPA status
kubectl get hpa user-auth-hpa -n anihpj-system
    NAME REFERENCE TARGETS MINPODS MAXPODS REPLICAS AGE user-auth-hpa Deployment/user-auth-service 45%/75% 2 8 3 5m
    Example 3: JobPost Search Autoscaling

    This example shows autoscaling for JobPost model search service, automatically adjusting to handle search query volume and job posting traffic.

    apiVersion: autoscaling/v1
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: jobpost-search-hpa
  namespace: anihpj-system
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: search
    model: jobpost
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: jobpost-search
  minReplicas: 3
  maxReplicas: 15
  targetCPUUtilizationPercentage: 65
    # Describe JobPost search HPA for detailed information
kubectl describe hpa jobpost-search-hpa -n anihpj-system
    Name: jobpost-search-hpa Namespace: anihpj-system Labels: app=anihpj component=search model=jobpost Annotations: CreationTimestamp: Mon, 15 Jan 2024 10:00:00 +0000 Reference: Deployment/jobpost-search Metrics: ( current / target ) resource cpu on pods (as a percentage of request): 42% (42m) / 65% Min replicas: 3 Max replicas: 15 Deployment pods: 3 current / 3 desired Conditions: Type Status Reason Message ---- ------ ------ ------- AbleToScale True ReadyForNewScale recommended size matches current size ScalingActive True ValidMetricFound the HPA was able to successfully calculate a replica count from cpu resource utilization (percentage of request) ScalingLimited False DesiredWithinRange the desired count is within the acceptable range Events: Type Reason Age From Message ---- ------ ---- ---- ------- Normal SuccessfulRescale 2m horizontal-pod-autoscaler New size: 3; reason: All metrics below target
    Example 4: Author Profile Service Autoscaling

    This example demonstrates HPA for Author model profile service, scaling to handle author profile views and updates efficiently.

    apiVersion: autoscaling/v1
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: author-profile-hpa
  namespace: anihpj-system
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: profiles
    model: author
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: author-profile-service
  minReplicas: 1
  maxReplicas: 6
  targetCPUUtilizationPercentage: 80
    # Monitor Author profile HPA scaling events
kubectl get events --field-selector involvedObject.name=author-profile-hpa -n anihpj-system
    LAST SEEN TYPE REASON OBJECT MESSAGE 5m Normal SuccessfulRescale horizontalpodautoscaler/author-profile-hpa New size: 2; reason: cpu resource utilization (percentage of request) above target 2m Normal SuccessfulRescale horizontalpodautoscaler/author-profile-hpa New size: 1; reason: All metrics below target
    Example 5: Location Service Autoscaling

    This example shows comprehensive HPA configuration for Location model service, handling geographic queries and location-based searches.

    apiVersion: autoscaling/v1
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: location-service-hpa
  namespace: anihpj-system
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: geolocation
    model: location
  annotations:
    description: "Auto-scales location service based on CPU utilization"
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: location-service
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 12
  targetCPUUtilizationPercentage: 70
    # Get comprehensive HPA information
kubectl get hpa location-service-hpa -n anihpj-system -o yaml
    apiVersion: autoscaling/v1 kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler metadata: annotations: description: Auto-scales location service based on CPU utilization creationTimestamp: "2024-01-15T10:00:00Z" labels: app: anihpj component: geolocation model: location name: location-service-hpa namespace: anihpj-system resourceVersion: "12345" uid: loc-789a0123-b45c-67d8-e901-234567890123 spec: maxReplicas: 12 minReplicas: 2 scaleTargetRef: apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment name: location-service targetCPUUtilizationPercentage: 70 status: currentCPUUtilizationPercentage: 45 currentReplicas: 2 desiredReplicas: 2 lastScaleTime: "2024-01-15T09:30:00Z" observedGeneration: 1

    HorizontalPodAutoscalerSpec

    HorizontalPodAutoscalerSpec is the specification of a horizontal pod autoscaler, defining the scaling behavior, target reference, and limits. For Django applications, this specification controls how the anihpj application scales in response to load changes.

    Syntax
    spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: anihpj-app
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 10
  targetCPUUtilizationPercentage: 70
    Parameters Description
    • scaleTargetRef (CrossVersionObjectReference): Required. Reference to the scaled resource. The HPA will learn current resource consumption and set desired pods using the Scale subresource.
    • scaleTargetRef.apiVersion (string): API version of the target resource. Typically "apps/v1" for Deployments.
    • scaleTargetRef.kind (string): Required. Kind of the target resource (Deployment, ReplicaSet, ReplicationController, StatefulSet).
    • scaleTargetRef.name (string): Required. Name of the target resource to scale.
    • maxReplicas (int32): Required. Upper limit for the number of pods. Cannot be smaller than minReplicas.
    • minReplicas (int32): Lower limit for number of replicas. Defaults to 1. Can be 0 if HPAScaleToZero feature gate is enabled.
    • targetCPUUtilizationPercentage (int32): Target average CPU utilization as percentage of requested CPU over all pods. If not specified, default autoscaling policy is used.
    Return Value

    Returns the HorizontalPodAutoscalerSpec object containing the autoscaling configuration parameters.

    • 200 OK: HorizontalPodAutoscalerSpec successfully retrieved
    • 400 Bad Request: Invalid specification parameters
    • 401 Unauthorized: Authentication required
    • 403 Forbidden: Insufficient permissions to access HorizontalPodAutoscalerSpec
    • 404 Not Found: HorizontalPodAutoscaler or target resource not found
    5 Practical Django Examples
    Example 1: Skills API Scaling Specification

    This example shows HPA specification for Skills model API, configured for moderate scaling based on API request patterns.

    spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: skills-api
  minReplicas: 1
  maxReplicas: 5
  targetCPUUtilizationPercentage: 60
    # Get HPA spec for Skills API
kubectl get hpa skills-api-hpa -n anihpj-system -o jsonpath='{.spec}' | jq
    { "maxReplicas": 5, "minReplicas": 1, "scaleTargetRef": { "apiVersion": "apps/v1", "kind": "Deployment", "name": "skills-api" }, "targetCPUUtilizationPercentage": 60 }
    Example 2: User Service High-Availability Spec

    This example demonstrates HPA specification for User model service requiring high availability with aggressive scaling.

    spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: user-service
  minReplicas: 3
  maxReplicas: 15
  targetCPUUtilizationPercentage: 50
    # Create User service HPA with specific spec
kubectl apply -f - << EOF
apiVersion: autoscaling/v1
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: user-service-hpa
  namespace: anihpj-system
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: user-service
  minReplicas: 3
  maxReplicas: 15
  targetCPUUtilizationPercentage: 50
EOF
    horizontalpodautoscaler.autoscaling/user-service-hpa created
    Example 3: JobPost Processing Conservative Scaling

    This example shows conservative HPA specification for JobPost model processing service with higher CPU threshold.

    spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: jobpost-processor
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 8
  targetCPUUtilizationPercentage: 85
    # Update existing HPA spec
kubectl patch hpa jobpost-processor-hpa -n anihpj-system -p '{"spec":{"targetCPUUtilizationPercentage":85}}'
    horizontalpodautoscaler.autoscaling/jobpost-processor-hpa patched
    Example 4: Author Analytics ReplicaSet Scaling

    This example demonstrates HPA specification targeting a ReplicaSet for Author model analytics service.

    spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: ReplicaSet
    name: author-analytics-rs
  minReplicas: 1
  maxReplicas: 6
  targetCPUUtilizationPercentage: 75
    # Validate HPA spec targeting ReplicaSet
kubectl describe hpa author-analytics-hpa -n anihpj-system | grep -A 5 "Reference:"
    Reference: ReplicaSet/author-analytics-rs Metrics: ( current / target ) resource cpu on pods (as a percentage of request): 35% (35m) / 75% Min replicas: 1 Max replicas: 6 ReplicaSet pods: 1 current / 1 desired
    Example 5: Location Microservice StatefulSet Scaling

    This example shows HPA specification for Location model microservice using StatefulSet with specific scaling parameters.

    spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: StatefulSet
    name: location-microservice
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 10
  targetCPUUtilizationPercentage: 65
    # Get all HPA specs in namespace
kubectl get hpa -n anihpj-system -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,TARGET:.spec.scaleTargetRef.name,MIN:.spec.minReplicas,MAX:.spec.maxReplicas,CPU:.spec.targetCPUUtilizationPercentage
    NAME TARGET MIN MAX CPU location-microservice-hpa location-microservice 2 10 65 skills-api-hpa skills-api 1 5 60 user-service-hpa user-service 3 15 50 author-analytics-hpa author-analytics-rs 1 6 75 jobpost-processor-hpa jobpost-processor 2 8 85

    HorizontalPodAutoscalerStatus

    HorizontalPodAutoscalerStatus represents the current status of a horizontal pod autoscaler, providing real-time information about scaling decisions and current state. For Django applications, this status helps monitor how the anihpj application is responding to load changes.

    Syntax
    status:
  currentReplicas: 3
  desiredReplicas: 3
  currentCPUUtilizationPercentage: 45
  lastScaleTime: "2024-01-15T10:30:00Z"
  observedGeneration: 1
    Parameters Description
    • currentReplicas (int32): Required. Current number of replicas of pods managed by this autoscaler.
    • desiredReplicas (int32): Required. Desired number of replicas of pods managed by this autoscaler.
    • currentCPUUtilizationPercentage (int32): Current average CPU utilization over all pods, represented as percentage of requested CPU. E.g., 70 means average pod uses 70% of requested CPU.
    • lastScaleTime (Time): Last time the HorizontalPodAutoscaler scaled the number of pods. Used by autoscaler to control scaling frequency.
    • observedGeneration (int64): Most recent generation observed by this autoscaler. Indicates if the autoscaler has processed the latest spec changes.
    Return Value

    Returns the HorizontalPodAutoscalerStatus object containing the current autoscaling state and metrics.

    • 200 OK: HorizontalPodAutoscalerStatus successfully retrieved
    • 401 Unauthorized: Authentication required
    • 403 Forbidden: Insufficient permissions to access HorizontalPodAutoscalerStatus
    • 404 Not Found: HorizontalPodAutoscaler not found
    5 Practical Django Examples
    Example 1: Skills Service Status During Low Load

    This example shows HPA status for Skills model service during low CPU utilization, maintaining minimum replicas.

    status:
  currentReplicas: 1
  desiredReplicas: 1
  currentCPUUtilizationPercentage: 25
  lastScaleTime: "2024-01-15T08:45:00Z"
  observedGeneration: 1
    # Check Skills service HPA status
kubectl get hpa skills-api-hpa -n anihpj-system -o jsonpath='{.status}' | jq
    { "currentCPUUtilizationPercentage": 25, "currentReplicas": 1, "desiredReplicas": 1, "lastScaleTime": "2024-01-15T08:45:00Z", "observedGeneration": 1 }
    Example 2: User Service Status During Scale-Up

    This example demonstrates HPA status for User model service during active scaling due to increased load.

    status:
  currentReplicas: 5
  desiredReplicas: 7
  currentCPUUtilizationPercentage: 85
  lastScaleTime: "2024-01-15T12:15:00Z"
  observedGeneration: 2
    # Monitor User service scaling status
kubectl get hpa user-service-hpa -n anihpj-system --watch
    NAME REFERENCE TARGETS MINPODS MAXPODS REPLICAS AGE user-service-hpa Deployment/user-service 85%/50% 3 15 5 10m user-service-hpa Deployment/user-service 82%/50% 3 15 6 10m user-service-hpa Deployment/user-service 78%/50% 3 15 7 11m user-service-hpa Deployment/user-service 52%/50% 3 15 7 12m
    Example 3: JobPost Service Status at Maximum Scale

    This example shows HPA status for JobPost model service that has reached maximum replicas due to high demand.

    status:
  currentReplicas: 8
  desiredReplicas: 8
  currentCPUUtilizationPercentage: 90
  lastScaleTime: "2024-01-15T14:30:00Z"
  observedGeneration: 1
    # Check if JobPost service hit scaling limits
kubectl describe hpa jobpost-processor-hpa -n anihpj-system | grep -A 3 "Conditions:"
    Conditions: Type Status Reason Message ---- ------ ------ ------- AbleToScale True ReadyForNewScale recommended size matches current size ScalingActive True ValidMetricFound the HPA was able to successfully calculate a replica count from cpu resource utilization (percentage of request) ScalingLimited True TooManyReplicas the desired replica count is more than the maximum replica count
    Example 4: Author Service Status During Scale-Down

    This example demonstrates HPA status for Author model service during scale-down operation as load decreases.

    status:
  currentReplicas: 4
  desiredReplicas: 2
  currentCPUUtilizationPercentage: 35
  lastScaleTime: "2024-01-15T16:20:00Z"
  observedGeneration: 1
    # Get Author service HPA events to see scale-down history
kubectl get events --field-selector involvedObject.name=author-analytics-hpa -n anihpj-system --sort-by='.lastTimestamp'
    LAST SEEN TYPE REASON OBJECT MESSAGE 10m Normal SuccessfulRescale horizontalpodautoscaler/author-analytics-hpa New size: 6; reason: cpu resource utilization (percentage of request) above target 5m Normal SuccessfulRescale horizontalpodautoscaler/author-analytics-hpa New size: 4; reason: All metrics below target 2m Normal SuccessfulRescale horizontalpodautoscaler/author-analytics-hpa New size: 2; reason: All metrics below target
    Example 5: Location Service Complete Status Overview

    This example shows comprehensive HPA status for Location model service including all status fields and recent scaling activity.

    status:
  currentReplicas: 6
  desiredReplicas: 6
  currentCPUUtilizationPercentage: 68
  lastScaleTime: "2024-01-15T18:10:00Z"
  observedGeneration: 3
    # Get comprehensive status of Location service HPA
kubectl get hpa location-microservice-hpa -n anihpj-system -o yaml | yq '.status'
    currentCPUUtilizationPercentage: 68 currentReplicas: 6 desiredReplicas: 6 lastScaleTime: "2024-01-15T18:10:00Z" observedGeneration: 3

    HorizontalPodAutoscalerList

    HorizontalPodAutoscalerList is a collection of horizontal pod autoscaler objects, useful for managing multiple autoscalers across your Django application infrastructure. This provides comprehensive oversight of all scaling policies for the anihpj application.

    Syntax
    apiVersion: autoscaling/v1
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscalerList
metadata:
  resourceVersion: "12345"
  selfLink: /apis/autoscaling/v1/horizontalpodautoscalers
items:
- apiVersion: autoscaling/v1
  kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
  metadata:
    name: skills-hpa
    namespace: anihpj-system
  spec:
    maxReplicas: 5
    minReplicas: 1
    targetCPUUtilizationPercentage: 60
- apiVersion: autoscaling/v1
  kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
  metadata:
    name: user-hpa
    namespace: anihpj-system
  spec:
    maxReplicas: 10
    minReplicas: 2
    targetCPUUtilizationPercentage: 70
    Parameters Description
    • apiVersion (string): Always "autoscaling/v1" for HorizontalPodAutoscalerList. Indicates the API version for the list resource.
    • kind (string): Always "HorizontalPodAutoscalerList". Specifies this is a collection of HorizontalPodAutoscaler resources.
    • metadata (ListMeta): Standard list metadata including resourceVersion for tracking changes across all autoscalers.
    • items ([]HorizontalPodAutoscaler): Required. Array of HorizontalPodAutoscaler objects in the list. Each represents a scaling policy for Django application components.
    Return Value

    Returns the HorizontalPodAutoscalerList object containing all HorizontalPodAutoscaler resources in the specified namespace or across namespaces.

    • 200 OK: HorizontalPodAutoscalerList successfully retrieved
    • 401 Unauthorized: Authentication required
    • 403 Forbidden: Insufficient permissions to list HorizontalPodAutoscalers
    • 404 Not Found: Namespace not found
    5 Practical Django Examples
    Example 1: All Model-Specific HPAs

    This example shows listing all HPAs for specific Django models, providing overview of autoscaling policies for Skills, User, and JobPost services.

    apiVersion: autoscaling/v1
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscalerList
metadata:
  resourceVersion: "23456"
items:
- metadata:
    name: skills-api-hpa
    labels:
      model: skills
  spec:
    maxReplicas: 5
    minReplicas: 1
    targetCPUUtilizationPercentage: 60
- metadata:
    name: user-auth-hpa
    labels:
      model: user
  spec:
    maxReplicas: 8
    minReplicas: 2
    targetCPUUtilizationPercentage: 75
- metadata:
    name: jobpost-search-hpa
    labels:
      model: jobpost
  spec:
    maxReplicas: 12
    minReplicas: 3
    targetCPUUtilizationPercentage: 65
    # List all HPAs with model labels
kubectl get hpa -n anihpj-system -l 'model in (skills,user,jobpost)' -o name
    horizontalpodautoscaler.autoscaling/skills-api-hpa horizontalpodautoscaler.autoscaling/user-auth-hpa horizontalpodautoscaler.autoscaling/jobpost-search-hpa
    Example 2: Namespace-Wide HPA Overview

    This example demonstrates listing all HPAs in the anihpj-system namespace, showing complete autoscaling infrastructure.

    apiVersion: autoscaling/v1
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscalerList
metadata:
  resourceVersion: "34567"
items:
- metadata:
    name: author-profile-hpa
    namespace: anihpj-system
- metadata:
    name: location-service-hpa
    namespace: anihpj-system
- metadata:
    name: skills-processor-hpa
    namespace: anihpj-system
- metadata:
    name: user-notifications-hpa
    namespace: anihpj-system
- metadata:
    name: jobpost-analytics-hpa
    namespace: anihpj-system
    # Get comprehensive HPA list with current status
kubectl get hpa -n anihpj-system -o wide
    NAME REFERENCE TARGETS MINPODS MAXPODS REPLICAS AGE author-profile-hpa Deployment/author-profile 45%/80% 1 6 2 5d location-service-hpa Deployment/location-service 52%/70% 2 12 3 3d skills-processor-hpa Deployment/skills-processor 38%/60% 1 5 1 7d user-notifications-hpa Deployment/user-notifications 67%/75% 2 8 3 2d jobpost-analytics-hpa Deployment/jobpost-analytics 71%/65% 3 12 5 1d
    Example 3: High-Availability Services HPA List

    This example shows listing HPAs for high-availability services that require minimum 2+ replicas across different models.

    apiVersion: autoscaling/v1
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscalerList
metadata:
  resourceVersion: "45678"
items:
- metadata:
    name: user-auth-ha-hpa
    labels:
      availability: high
      model: user
  spec:
    maxReplicas: 15
    minReplicas: 3
    targetCPUUtilizationPercentage: 50
- metadata:
    name: jobpost-search-ha-hpa
    labels:
      availability: high
      model: jobpost
  spec:
    maxReplicas: 20
    minReplicas: 5
    targetCPUUtilizationPercentage: 55
- metadata:
    name: location-geo-ha-hpa
    labels:
      availability: high
      model: location
  spec:
    maxReplicas: 10
    minReplicas: 4
    targetCPUUtilizationPercentage: 60
    # List high-availability HPAs with minimum replica counts
kubectl get hpa -n anihpj-system -l availability=high -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,MIN:.spec.minReplicas,MAX:.spec.maxReplicas,TARGET:.spec.targetCPUUtilizationPercentage
    NAME MIN MAX TARGET user-auth-ha-hpa 3 15 50 jobpost-search-ha-hpa 5 20 55 location-geo-ha-hpa 4 10 60
    Example 4: Component-Based HPA Grouping

    This example demonstrates listing HPAs grouped by application components (API, processing, analytics) for the anihpj Django application.

    apiVersion: autoscaling/v1
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscalerList
metadata:
  resourceVersion: "56789"
items:
- metadata:
    name: skills-api-hpa
    labels:
      component: api
      model: skills
- metadata:
    name: user-api-hpa
    labels:
      component: api
      model: user
- metadata:
    name: jobpost-processor-hpa
    labels:
      component: processing
      model: jobpost
- metadata:
    name: author-analytics-hpa
    labels:
      component: analytics
      model: author
- metadata:
    name: location-geocoder-hpa
    labels:
      component: processing
      model: location
    # List HPAs by component type
kubectl get hpa -n anihpj-system -l component=api --no-headers | wc -l && echo "API HPAs found"
    2 API HPAs found
    Example 5: Cross-Namespace HPA Discovery

    This example shows comprehensive HPA discovery across multiple namespaces for a complete Django application deployment.

    apiVersion: autoscaling/v1
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscalerList
metadata:
  resourceVersion: "67890"
items:
- metadata:
    name: production-user-hpa
    namespace: anihpj-production
    labels:
      environment: production
      model: user
- metadata:
    name: staging-user-hpa
    namespace: anihpj-staging
    labels:
      environment: staging
      model: user
- metadata:
    name: development-user-hpa
    namespace: anihpj-development
    labels:
      environment: development
      model: user
    # List all anihpj HPAs across environments
kubectl get hpa --all-namespaces -l app=anihpj -o json | jq '.items | length'
    15

    🔧 HorizontalPodAutoscaler v1 Operations Overview

    Read - Get HPA details Read Status - Get HPA status List - List HPAs in namespace List All - List across namespaces Create - Create new HPA Update - Replace HPA Update Status - Replace HPA status Patch - Partially update HPA Patch Status - Partially update status Delete - Remove HPA Delete Collection - Remove multiple HPAs
    📖 Get HorizontalPodAutoscaler

    HTTP Request: GET /apis/autoscaling/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/horizontalpodautoscalers/{name}

    Retrieve details of a specific HorizontalPodAutoscaler for monitoring autoscaling configuration.

    # Get Skills service HPA details
kubectl get hpa skills-api-hpa -n anihpj-system -o yaml
    apiVersion: autoscaling/v1 kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler metadata: name: skills-api-hpa namespace: anihpj-system labels: app: anihpj model: skills spec: maxReplicas: 5 minReplicas: 1 scaleTargetRef: apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment name: skills-api targetCPUUtilizationPercentage: 60 status: currentCPUUtilizationPercentage: 42 currentReplicas: 2 desiredReplicas: 2
    📊 Get HorizontalPodAutoscaler Status

    HTTP Request: GET /apis/autoscaling/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/horizontalpodautoscalers/{name}/status

    Get the current status of a HorizontalPodAutoscaler including current metrics and scaling state.

    # Get User service HPA status
kubectl get hpa user-auth-hpa -n anihpj-system -o jsonpath='{.status}' | jq
    { "currentCPUUtilizationPercentage": 67, "currentReplicas": 4, "desiredReplicas": 5, "lastScaleTime": "2024-01-15T14:30:00Z", "observedGeneration": 2 }
    📋 List HorizontalPodAutoscalers

    HTTP Request: GET /apis/autoscaling/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/horizontalpodautoscalers

    List all HorizontalPodAutoscalers in a specific namespace.

    # List all HPAs in anihpj-system namespace
kubectl get hpa -n anihpj-system
    NAME REFERENCE TARGETS MINPODS MAXPODS REPLICAS AGE skills-api-hpa Deployment/skills-api 42%/60% 1 5 2 3d user-auth-hpa Deployment/user-auth 67%/75% 2 8 4 5d jobpost-search-hpa Deployment/jobpost-search 51%/65% 3 12 5 2d author-profile-hpa Deployment/author-profile 38%/80% 1 6 1 7d location-service-hpa Deployment/location-service 45%/70% 2 10 3 1d
    🌐 List HorizontalPodAutoscalers (All Namespaces)

    HTTP Request: GET /apis/autoscaling/v1/horizontalpodautoscalers

    List HorizontalPodAutoscalers across all accessible namespaces.

    # List HPAs across all namespaces
kubectl get hpa --all-namespaces -l app=anihpj
    NAMESPACE NAME REFERENCE TARGETS MINPODS MAXPODS REPLICAS AGE anihpj-system skills-api-hpa Deployment/skills-api 42%/60% 1 5 2 3d anihpj-system user-auth-hpa Deployment/user-auth 67%/75% 2 8 4 5d anihpj-production prod-jobpost-hpa Deployment/jobpost-api 55%/70% 5 20 8 10d anihpj-staging stage-user-hpa Deployment/user-service 35%/60% 1 5 1 2d
    ➕ Create HorizontalPodAutoscaler

    HTTP Request: POST /apis/autoscaling/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/horizontalpodautoscalers

    Create a new HorizontalPodAutoscaler to enable automatic scaling for a deployment or other scalable resource.

    # Create HPA for Author analytics service
kubectl apply -f - << EOF
apiVersion: autoscaling/v1
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: author-analytics-hpa
  namespace: anihpj-system
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: author
    component: analytics
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: author-analytics
  minReplicas: 1
  maxReplicas: 6
  targetCPUUtilizationPercentage: 75
EOF
    horizontalpodautoscaler.autoscaling/author-analytics-hpa created
    🔄 Update HorizontalPodAutoscaler

    HTTP Request: PUT /apis/autoscaling/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/horizontalpodautoscalers/{name}

    Replace the entire HorizontalPodAutoscaler specification with new configuration.

    # Update Location service HPA with new scaling parameters
kubectl apply -f - << EOF
apiVersion: autoscaling/v1
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: location-service-hpa
  namespace: anihpj-system
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: location-service
  minReplicas: 3
  maxReplicas: 15
  targetCPUUtilizationPercentage: 65
EOF
    horizontalpodautoscaler.autoscaling/location-service-hpa configured
    📊 Update HorizontalPodAutoscaler Status

    HTTP Request: PUT /apis/autoscaling/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/horizontalpodautoscalers/{name}/status

    Update the status subresource of a HorizontalPodAutoscaler (typically done by the controller).

    # Check current status before update
kubectl get hpa skills-api-hpa -n anihpj-system -o jsonpath='{.status}' | jq
    { "currentCPUUtilizationPercentage": 58, "currentReplicas": 3, "desiredReplicas": 3, "lastScaleTime": "2024-01-15T16:45:00Z", "observedGeneration": 1 }
    🔧 Patch HorizontalPodAutoscaler

    HTTP Request: PATCH /apis/autoscaling/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/horizontalpodautoscalers/{name}

    Partially update specific fields of a HorizontalPodAutoscaler without replacing the entire resource.

    # Update JobPost HPA target CPU percentage
kubectl patch hpa jobpost-search-hpa -n anihpj-system -p '{"spec":{"targetCPUUtilizationPercentage":70}}'
    horizontalpodautoscaler.autoscaling/jobpost-search-hpa patched
    # Update max replicas for User HPA
kubectl patch hpa user-auth-hpa -n anihpj-system -p '{"spec":{"maxReplicas":12}}'
    horizontalpodautoscaler.autoscaling/user-auth-hpa patched
    📊 Patch HorizontalPodAutoscaler Status

    HTTP Request: PATCH /apis/autoscaling/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/horizontalpodautoscalers/{name}/status

    Partially update the status fields of a HorizontalPodAutoscaler.

    # View current status before patch
kubectl get hpa author-analytics-hpa -n anihpj-system -o jsonpath='{.status}'
    { "currentCPUUtilizationPercentage": 72, "currentReplicas": 4, "desiredReplicas": 4, "lastScaleTime": "2024-01-15T18:20:00Z" }
    🗑️ Delete HorizontalPodAutoscaler

    HTTP Request: DELETE /apis/autoscaling/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/horizontalpodautoscalers/{name}

    Remove a HorizontalPodAutoscaler and disable automatic scaling for the target resource.

    # Delete specific HPA
kubectl delete hpa author-analytics-hpa -n anihpj-system
    horizontalpodautoscaler.autoscaling "author-analytics-hpa" deleted
    # Delete with confirmation
kubectl delete hpa location-service-hpa -n anihpj-system --wait=true
    horizontalpodautoscaler.autoscaling "location-service-hpa" deleted
    🗑️ Delete HorizontalPodAutoscaler Collection

    HTTP Request: DELETE /apis/autoscaling/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/horizontalpodautoscalers

    Delete multiple HorizontalPodAutoscalers at once using label selectors or other criteria.

    # Delete HPAs by label
kubectl delete hpa -n anihpj-system -l component=analytics
    horizontalpodautoscaler.autoscaling "author-analytics-hpa" deleted horizontalpodautoscaler.autoscaling "jobpost-analytics-hpa" deleted
    # Delete all HPAs in namespace (be careful!)
kubectl delete hpa -n anihpj-system --all
    horizontalpodautoscaler.autoscaling "skills-api-hpa" deleted horizontalpodautoscaler.autoscaling "user-auth-hpa" deleted horizontalpodautoscaler.autoscaling "location-service-hpa" deleted

    HorizontalPodAutoscaler v2

    autoscaling/v2 anihpj Django Project

    HorizontalPodAutoscaler v2

    HorizontalPodAutoscaler v2 Overview

    HorizontalPodAutoscalerSpec - Enhanced autoscaler specification
    HorizontalPodAutoscalerStatus - Current autoscaler state
    HorizontalPodAutoscalerList - Collection operations
    Operations - CRUD and management
    Official Docs: HorizontalPodAutoscaler v2 API | API Version: autoscaling/v2

    HorizontalPodAutoscaler is the configuration for a horizontal pod autoscaler (v2), which automatically manages the replica count of any resource implementing the scale subresource based on advanced metrics including CPU, memory, and custom metrics.

    Full Description

    HorizontalPodAutoscaler v2 is an advanced autoscaling resource in Kubernetes that automatically adjusts the number of pod replicas based on observed CPU utilization, memory usage, or custom metrics. When deploying your anihpj Django application, HPA v2 monitors your Django pods and automatically scales them up or down based on demand, ensuring optimal resource utilization and performance for your jobpost application.

    HPA v2 provides significant improvements over v1, including support for multiple metrics (CPU, memory, custom metrics), advanced scaling behaviors, and better control over scaling policies. In production Django deployments, HPA v2 is essential for handling varying loads on your application APIs, from Skills management to JobPost processing.

    Syntax

    # Create a HorizontalPodAutoscaler from YAML file
kubectl create -f horizontalpodautoscaler.yaml
# List all HorizontalPodAutoscalers (long form)
kubectl get horizontalpodautoscalers
# List all HorizontalPodAutoscalers (short form)
kubectl get hpa
# Get detailed information about a specific HorizontalPodAutoscaler
kubectl describe horizontalpodautoscaler {name}
# Get detailed information about a specific HorizontalPodAutoscaler (short form)
kubectl describe hpa {name}
# Get HPA status with metrics information
kubectl get hpa {name} -o yaml
# Watch HPA scaling in real-time
kubectl get hpa {name} -w
# Delete a specific HorizontalPodAutoscaler
kubectl delete horizontalpodautoscaler {name}
# Delete a specific HorizontalPodAutoscaler (short form)
kubectl delete hpa {name}

    YAML Structure

    apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: anihpj-django-hpa
  namespace: default
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: autoscaling
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: anihpj-django-deployment
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 20
  metrics:
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: cpu
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 70
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: memory
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 80
  behavior:
    scaleUp:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 60
      policies:
      - type: Percent
        value: 100
        periodSeconds: 15
    scaleDown:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 300
      policies:
      - type: Pods
        value: 1
        periodSeconds: 60

    Parameters Description

    • apiVersion: Always "autoscaling/v2" for HorizontalPodAutoscaler v2. This indicates the enhanced API version being used.
    • kind: Must be "HorizontalPodAutoscaler" to specify the resource type.
    • metadata.name: Unique name for the HorizontalPodAutoscaler within the namespace. Use descriptive names like "anihpj-django-hpa".
    • metadata.namespace: Kubernetes namespace where the HorizontalPodAutoscaler will be created. Defaults to "default".
    • metadata.labels: Key-value pairs for organizing and selecting HorizontalPodAutoscalers. Useful for grouping Django app autoscaling components.
    • spec.scaleTargetRef: Reference to the target resource (Deployment, ReplicaSet, etc.) to be scaled.
    • spec.minReplicas: Minimum number of replicas. Can be 0 with HPAScaleToZero feature gate enabled.
    • spec.maxReplicas: Maximum number of replicas for scaling up. For Django apps, consider your resource limits.
    • spec.metrics: Array of metric specifications including Resource, Pods, Object, External, and ContainerResource metrics.
    • spec.behavior: Advanced scaling behavior configuration for both scaleUp and scaleDown operations.
    • status.currentReplicas: Current number of replicas managed by the autoscaler.
    • status.desiredReplicas: Desired number of replicas calculated by the autoscaler based on metrics.

    Return Value

    Returns the HorizontalPodAutoscaler object with its current state, including status information about current and desired replicas, metrics, and conditions indicating the autoscaler's health.

    • 200 OK: HorizontalPodAutoscaler successfully created/updated
    • 201 Created: HorizontalPodAutoscaler successfully created
    • 400 Bad Request: Invalid HorizontalPodAutoscaler specification
    • 401 Unauthorized: Authentication required
    • 403 Forbidden: Insufficient permissions
    • 404 Not Found: Target resource or namespace not found
    • 409 Conflict: HorizontalPodAutoscaler already exists
    • 422 Unprocessable Entity: Invalid field values or metric configuration

    5 Practical Django Examples

    Example 1: Skills Model Service Multi-metric HPA (jobpost app)

    This example demonstrates deploying a HorizontalPodAutoscaler for the Skills API service (/jobpost/skills/), using both CPU and memory metrics to automatically scale based on demand for job skills management in the anihpj Django project.

    apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-skills-hpa
  namespace: default
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: jobpost
    service: skills
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: anihpj-jobpost-skills-deployment
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 15
  metrics:
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: cpu
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 70
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: memory
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 80
    # Apply the Skills model HorizontalPodAutoscaler
kubectl apply -f anihpj-jobpost-skills-hpa.yaml
    horizontalpodautoscaler.autoscaling/anihpj-jobpost-skills-hpa created
    Example 2: Author Model Service with Custom Pod Metrics (jobpost app)

    This example shows how to deploy author management autoscaling using custom pod metrics for the Author model, which connects to Django's built-in User model and scales based on authentication requests per second.

    apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-author-hpa
  namespace: default
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: jobpost
    service: author
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: anihpj-jobpost-author-deployment
  minReplicas: 3
  maxReplicas: 20
  metrics:
  - type: Pods
    pods:
      metric:
        name: author_requests_per_second
      target:
        type: AverageValue
        averageValue: "30"
  behavior:
    scaleUp:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 60
      policies:
      - type: Percent
        value: 100
        periodSeconds: 15
    # Apply the Author model HorizontalPodAutoscaler with custom metrics
kubectl apply -f anihpj-jobpost-author-hpa.yaml
    horizontalpodautoscaler.autoscaling/anihpj-jobpost-author-hpa created
    Example 3: User Model Service with External Queue Metrics (jobpost app)

    This example demonstrates autoscaling for User authentication services using external metrics from a message queue, scaling based on user registration and login processing queue length in the anihpj Django system.

    apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-user-hpa
  namespace: default
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: jobpost
    service: user
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: anihpj-jobpost-user-deployment
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 12
  metrics:
  - type: External
    external:
      metric:
        name: user_processing_queue_length
        selector:
          matchLabels:
            queue_name: user-auth-queue
      target:
        type: Value
        value: "50"
    # Apply the User model HorizontalPodAutoscaler with external metrics
kubectl apply -f anihpj-jobpost-user-hpa.yaml
    horizontalpodautoscaler.autoscaling/anihpj-jobpost-user-hpa created
    Example 4: Location Model Service with Object Metrics (jobpost app)

    This example shows autoscaling for Location management services using object metrics, scaling based on requests per second to the location service endpoint in the anihpj Django application.

    apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-location-hpa
  namespace: default
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: jobpost
    service: location
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: anihpj-jobpost-location-deployment
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 10
  metrics:
  - type: Object
    object:
      metric:
        name: requests-per-second
      describedObject:
        apiVersion: v1
        kind: Service
        name: anihpj-jobpost-location-service
      target:
        type: Value
        value: "1k"
    # Apply the Location model HorizontalPodAutoscaler with object metrics
kubectl apply -f anihpj-jobpost-location-hpa.yaml
    horizontalpodautoscaler.autoscaling/anihpj-jobpost-location-hpa created
    Example 5: JobPost Model Service with Container Resource Metrics (jobpost app)

    This example demonstrates deploying autoscaling for the main JobPost management service using container-specific resource metrics, providing fine-grained control over scaling for the core jobpost functionality in the anihpj Django project.

    apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-main-hpa
  namespace: default
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: jobpost
    service: main
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: anihpj-jobpost-main-deployment
  minReplicas: 3
  maxReplicas: 25
  metrics:
  - type: ContainerResource
    containerResource:
      name: cpu
      container: jobpost-container
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 65
  behavior:
    scaleDown:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 300
      policies:
      - type: Pods
        value: 1
        periodSeconds: 60
    # Apply the JobPost model HorizontalPodAutoscaler with container metrics
kubectl apply -f anihpj-jobpost-main-hpa.yaml
    horizontalpodautoscaler.autoscaling/anihpj-jobpost-main-hpa created

    HorizontalPodAutoscalerSpec

    HorizontalPodAutoscalerSpec describes the desired functionality of the HorizontalPodAutoscaler with enhanced v2 features including multiple metrics, advanced scaling behavior, and custom metrics support for Django applications.

    Syntax
    spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: anihpj-django-deployment
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 20
  metrics:
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: cpu
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 70
  behavior:
    scaleUp:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 60
      policies:
      - type: Percent
        value: 100
        periodSeconds: 15
    scaleDown:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 300
      policies:
      - type: Pods
        value: 1
        periodSeconds: 60
    Parameters Description
    • scaleTargetRef (CrossVersionObjectReference): Reference to the target resource (Deployment, ReplicaSet, etc.) to be scaled. Must specify apiVersion, kind, and name.
    • minReplicas (int32): Minimum number of replicas. Can be 0 with HPAScaleToZero feature gate enabled. For Django apps, typically 1-3.
    • maxReplicas (int32): Maximum number of replicas for scaling up. For Django apps, consider your resource limits and expected traffic.
    • metrics ([]MetricSpec): Array of metric specifications for scaling decisions including Resource, Pods, Object, External, and ContainerResource metrics.
    • behavior (HorizontalPodAutoscalerBehavior): Advanced scaling behavior configuration for both scaleUp and scaleDown operations to prevent flapping.
    Return Value

    Returns the HorizontalPodAutoscalerSpec object defining the desired autoscaling behavior, including target reference, replica limits, metrics configuration, and scaling policies for the Django application deployment.

    Example 1: JobPost Processing with Memory Metrics

    This example shows a HorizontalPodAutoscaler spec for the JobPost processing service using memory-based autoscaling to handle varying workloads efficiently.

    spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: jobpost-processor
  minReplicas: 3
  maxReplicas: 25
  metrics:
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: memory
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 75
    # Apply the JobPost processor HPA spec
kubectl apply -f jobpost-processor-hpa.yaml
# Check HPA status
kubectl get hpa jobpost-processor-hpa
    horizontalpodautoscaler.autoscaling/jobpost-processor-hpa created NAME REFERENCE TARGETS MINPODS MAXPODS REPLICAS AGE jobpost-processor-hpa Deployment/jobpost-processor 45%/75% 3 25 3 30s
    Example 2: Skills API with Custom Pod Metrics

    This example demonstrates HPA spec using custom pod metrics for the Skills API service to scale based on skill matching operations per second.

    spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: skills-api
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 18
  metrics:
  - type: Pods
    pods:
      metric:
        name: skill_matches_per_second
      target:
        type: AverageValue
        averageValue: "20"
    # Create the Skills API HPA with custom metrics
kubectl apply -f skills-api-hpa.yaml
# Monitor the custom metrics scaling
kubectl describe hpa skills-api-hpa
    horizontalpodautoscaler.autoscaling/skills-api-hpa created Name: skills-api-hpa Namespace: default Reference: Deployment/skills-api Metrics: ( current / target ) "skill_matches_per_second" on pods: 15 / 20 Min replicas: 2 Max replicas: 18 Deployment pods: 2 current / 2 desired
    Example 3: User Service with Advanced Scaling Behavior

    This example shows HPA spec with sophisticated scaling behavior configuration for the User service to prevent rapid scaling oscillations.

    spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: user-service
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 16
  behavior:
    scaleUp:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 60
      policies:
      - type: Percent
        value: 50
        periodSeconds: 30
    scaleDown:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 300
      policies:
      - type: Pods
        value: 2
        periodSeconds: 60
    # Apply the User service HPA with scaling behavior
kubectl apply -f user-service-hpa.yaml
# Watch scaling events over time
kubectl get events --field-selector involvedObject.name=user-service-hpa
    horizontalpodautoscaler.autoscaling/user-service-hpa created LAST SEEN TYPE REASON OBJECT MESSAGE 2m15s Normal SuccessfulRescale HorizontalPodAutoscaler/user-service-hpa New size: 4; reason: cpu resource utilization above target
    Example 4: Location Service with External Queue Metrics

    This example demonstrates HPA spec using external metrics from a message queue to scale the Location geocoding service based on pending work.

    spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: location-geocoder
  minReplicas: 1
  maxReplicas: 12
  metrics:
  - type: External
    external:
      metric:
        name: location_geocoding_queue_length
      target:
        type: Value
        value: "50"
    # Apply the Location geocoder HPA with external metrics
kubectl apply -f location-geocoder-hpa.yaml
# Check external metrics configuration
kubectl get hpa location-geocoder-hpa -o yaml | grep -A 10 external
    horizontalpodautoscaler.autoscaling/location-geocoder-hpa created external: metric: name: location_geocoding_queue_length target: type: Value value: "50"
    Example 5: Author Analytics with Multiple Metrics

    This example shows an advanced HPA spec with multiple metrics including CPU, container-specific memory, and composite scaling decisions for Author analytics service.

    spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: author-analytics
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 20
  metrics:
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: cpu
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 65
  - type: ContainerResource
    containerResource:
      name: memory
      container: analytics-worker
      target:
        type: AverageValue
        averageValue: "1Gi"
    # Apply the Author analytics HPA with multiple metrics
kubectl apply -f author-analytics-hpa.yaml
# Monitor multiple metrics scaling decisions
kubectl describe hpa author-analytics-hpa | grep -A 15 "Metrics:"
    horizontalpodautoscaler.autoscaling/author-analytics-hpa created Metrics: ( current / target ) resource cpu on pods (as a percentage of request): 45% (450m) / 65% "memory" on container "analytics-worker": 800Mi / 1Gi Min replicas: 2 Max replicas: 20 Deployment pods: 3 current / 3 desired

    HorizontalPodAutoscalerStatus

    HorizontalPodAutoscalerStatus describes the current status of a horizontal pod autoscaler with enhanced v2 status information including current metrics, conditions, and scaling history for Django applications.

    Syntax
    status:
  currentReplicas: 8
  desiredReplicas: 8
  currentMetrics:
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: cpu
      current:
        averageUtilization: 72
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: memory
      current:
        averageUtilization: 68
  conditions:
  - type: AbleToScale
    status: "True"
    lastTransitionTime: "2024-01-15T10:30:00Z"
    reason: ReadyForNewScale
    message: "recommended size matches current size"
  - type: ScalingActive
    status: "True"
    lastTransitionTime: "2024-01-15T10:30:00Z"
    reason: ValidMetricFound
  lastScaleTime: "2024-01-15T10:25:00Z"
  observedGeneration: 3
    Parameters Description
    • currentReplicas (int32): Current number of replicas managed by the autoscaler as last seen by the autoscaler.
    • desiredReplicas (int32): Desired number of replicas calculated by the autoscaler based on current metrics and scaling policies.
    • currentMetrics ([]MetricStatus): Last read state of metrics used for scaling decisions including Resource, Pods, Object, External metrics.
    • conditions ([]HorizontalPodAutoscalerCondition): Set of conditions required for autoscaler to function properly (AbleToScale, ScalingActive, ScalingLimited).
    • lastScaleTime (Time): Last time the HorizontalPodAutoscaler scaled the number of pods, used to control scaling frequency.
    • observedGeneration (int64): Most recent generation observed by this autoscaler for tracking spec changes.
    Return Value

    Returns the HorizontalPodAutoscalerStatus object with current state information, including actual replica counts, current metrics values, scaling conditions, and last scale operation timestamp for monitoring Django application autoscaling health.

    Example 1: JobPost API Scaling Status

    This example shows how to check the detailed status of a JobPost API HPA including current metrics, replica counts, and scaling conditions.

    # Get complete JobPost API HPA status
kubectl get hpa jobpost-api-hpa -n anihpj-system -o yaml | grep -A 20 "status:"
    status: currentReplicas: 8 desiredReplicas: 8 currentMetrics: - type: Resource resource: name: cpu current: averageUtilization: 72 - type: Resource resource: name: memory current: averageUtilization: 68 conditions: - type: AbleToScale status: "True" lastTransitionTime: "2024-01-15T10:30:00Z" - type: ScalingActive status: "True" lastTransitionTime: "2024-01-15T10:30:00Z" lastScaleTime: "2024-01-15T10:25:00Z"
    Example 2: User Auth Service Status Check

    This example demonstrates how to examine the User authentication service HPA status with custom pod metrics and scaling behavior.

    # Describe User auth HPA for detailed status information
kubectl describe hpa user-auth-hpa -n anihpj-system
    Name: user-auth-hpa Namespace: anihpj-system Reference: Deployment/user-auth-service Metrics: ( current / target ) "auth_requests_per_second" on pods: 25 / 30 Min replicas: 3 Max replicas: 15 Deployment pods: 3 current / 3 desired Conditions: Type Status Reason Message AbleToScale True ReadyForNewScale recommended size matches current size ScalingActive True ValidMetricFound the HPA was able to successfully calculate a replica count from pods metric auth_requests_per_second Events:
    Example 3: Skills Service External Metrics Status

    This example shows how to monitor Skills service HPA status using external metrics with custom output formatting for specific data points.

    # Get Skills HPA status with custom columns showing external metrics
kubectl get hpa skills-external-hpa -n anihpj-system --output=custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,REFERENCE:.spec.scaleTargetRef.name,TARGETS:.status.currentMetrics[*].external.current.value,MINPODS:.spec.minReplicas,MAXPODS:.spec.maxReplicas,REPLICAS:.status.currentReplicas
    NAME REFERENCE TARGETS MINPODS MAXPODS REPLICAS skills-external-hpa skills-service 45 1 10 6
    Example 4: Location Service Scaling Conditions

    This example demonstrates how to extract specific scaling condition information from the Location service HPA status for monitoring and alerting.

    # Extract scaling conditions status from Location HPA
kubectl get hpa location-object-hpa -n anihpj-system -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[*].type}: {.status.conditions[*].status}'
    AbleToScale ScalingActive ScalingLimited: True True False
    Example 5: Author Analytics Container Metrics Status

    This example shows how to monitor Author analytics service HPA status with container-specific resource metrics and current scaling state.

    # Get container-specific metrics from Author analytics HPA status
kubectl get hpa author-container-hpa -n anihpj-system -o yaml | grep -A 10 currentMetrics
    currentMetrics: - containerResource: container: author-container current: averageUtilization: 58 name: cpu type: ContainerResource currentReplicas: 4 desiredReplicas: 4 lastScaleTime: "2024-01-15T09:45:00Z"

    HorizontalPodAutoscalerList

    HorizontalPodAutoscalerList is a collection of horizontal pod autoscaler objects providing bulk operations and listing capabilities for managing multiple autoscalers in anihpj Django environments.

    Syntax
    apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscalerList
metadata:
  resourceVersion: "12345"
  continue: ""
items:
- apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
  kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
  metadata:
    name: anihpj-jobpost-skills-hpa
    namespace: default
  spec:
    scaleTargetRef:
      apiVersion: apps/v1
      kind: Deployment
      name: anihpj-jobpost-skills-deployment
    minReplicas: 2
    maxReplicas: 15
  status:
    currentReplicas: 4
    desiredReplicas: 4
    Parameters Description
    • apiVersion (string): API version "autoscaling/v2" for enhanced autoscaling features and metrics support.
    • kind (string): Resource type "HorizontalPodAutoscalerList" to specify the collection type.
    • metadata (ListMeta): List metadata including pagination information like resourceVersion and continue tokens.
    • items ([]HorizontalPodAutoscaler): Array of HorizontalPodAutoscaler objects containing the actual autoscaler configurations and status.
    Return Value

    Returns the HorizontalPodAutoscalerList object containing a collection of HorizontalPodAutoscaler resources with pagination metadata, enabling bulk operations and comprehensive autoscaler management across Django application deployments.

    Example 1: List All HPAs in Anihpj Namespace

    This example shows how to list all HorizontalPodAutoscaler resources in the anihpj namespace with current status and target metrics information.

    # List all HPAs in the anihpj namespace
kubectl get hpa -n anihpj-system
    NAME REFERENCE TARGETS MINPODS MAXPODS REPLICAS AGE jobpost-api-hpa Deployment/jobpost-api 72%/70%, 68%/80% 2 20 8 5d user-auth-hpa Deployment/user-auth 25/30 3 15 3 3d skills-external-hpa Deployment/skills-service 45/30 1 10 6 2d location-object-hpa Deployment/location 800m/1k 2 12 4 1d author-container-hpa Deployment/author-service 58%/60% 1 8 4 12h
    Example 2: List HPAs with Detailed Output

    This example demonstrates how to get detailed information about HPAs including metric types and specific target values for comprehensive monitoring.

    # Get detailed HPA information with wide output format
